title: The Hybrid's Secret
content:
The gates of Silver Fang Academy rose against the morning sky like ribs of some ancient beast, wrought iron twisted into the shape of howling wolves. Maya stood at the center of the orientation courtyard, sweat already beading at her temples despite the October chill.

She counted the other new students. Eighty-seven. All of them purebloods. All of them staring.

"Keep your head down," her mother had said that morning, hands shaking as she adjusted Maya's collar. "Don't let them smell your fear. Don't let them smell anything else."

Easy for a human to say. Humans didn't have to worry about their scent betraying every drop of mixed blood in their veins.

A tall girl with copper hair bumped Maya's shoulder as she passed. Hard enough to mean it. "Watch yourself, mutt."

Maya said nothing. She'd learned silence in eighteen years of hiding. Silence when the neighbor's dogs growled at her through the fence. Silence when her wolf clawed at her insides during full moons, begging to run. Silence when her mother cried at the kitchen table, wondering if she'd done the right thing, keeping a half-breed child instead of turning her over to the packs.

Professor Thornwood climbed the stone steps at the courtyard's edge. Old. Silver-eyed. The kind of wolf who'd lived long enough to forget what mercy felt like. His voice carried without effort, a blade wrapped in velvet.

"Welcome to Silver Fang Academy. You are here because your bloodlines earned you a place. Because your families have served the Great Pack for generations." His gaze swept the crowd. Stopped on Maya. "Most of you."

A ripple of laughter. Maya's nails bit into her palms.

"Orientation will test your control. Your instincts. Your worth." Thornwood's lips curved, but his eyes stayed cold. "By sunset, we will know who belongs here. And who does not."

The first test came before noon.

They filed into the shifting chamber, a circular room lined with mirrors and silver-etched wards. The air smelled of pine and ozone, heavy with magic that made Maya's skin prickle. Tension hummed through the students. Purebloods born, raised to shift before they could walk. But this was different. This was performance. Judgment.

"First shift," Thornwood announced. "Show me your wolves."

The girl next to Maya stripped off her uniform jacket with practiced ease. Her name was Celeste—Maya had heard it whispered in the dorms that morning, accompanied by words like *alpha heir* and *don't cross her*. Her copper hair fell across bare shoulders as she shed her clothes without shame, without fear.

Her bones cracked. Her spine arched. Fur erupted across her skin, russet and thick.

The wolf that stood in her place was beautiful. Powerful. Golden eyes fixed on Maya with something that looked like a challenge.

More students shifted. Wolves of black and brown and gray filled the chamber, panting, growling, testing their new forms. The room vibrated with wild energy.

Maya's turn.

She stepped forward, heart hammering against her ribs. Her fingers found the buttons of her blouse. Undid them one by one. The air felt cold on her skin, but not as cold as the stares.

*Don't let them see you hesitate.*

She'd shifted before. In secret. In the woods behind her mother's house, where no one could witness the wrongness of her change. A half-breed's shift was supposed to be painful. Unnatural. Her mother had warned her about the old stories—wolves who tried to force mixed blood into pure forms, who tore themselves apart from the inside.

Maya closed her eyes.

Let the wolf rise.

It came like a flood, like drowning, like finally breathing after years underwater. Her bones rearranged. Her skin rippled. The change hurt, but it was a good hurt—the ache of muscles remembering what they were meant to be.

She opened her eyes.

The chamber had gone silent.

Maya looked down at her paws. Silver. Not the dull gray of a common wolf, but silver like moonlight on still water, like the edge of a blade before it strikes. Her fur shimmered with it, catching the ward-light and scattering it across the mirrors.

Someone gasped.

Thornwood's expression didn't change, but his silver eyes sharpened. "Step forward."

Maya's legs moved before her mind caught up. She walked on four paws, feeling the weight of every stare, every whisper she couldn't quite hear.

"Silver," a boy muttered. "I thought silvers were extinct."

"They are," another hissed back. "She must have—"

"Quiet." Thornwood circled Maya, slow and deliberate. His hand reached out, fingers brushing her silver flank. She forced herself not to flinch. "Remarkable."

The word hung in the air like a threat.

"Your lineage?" Thornwood asked.

Maya couldn't speak in this form. Couldn't explain that her mother was human, her father a rogue whose face she'd never seen, whose name her mother refused to speak. Couldn't tell them that the silver in her blood was a mystery, a curse, a gift she didn't know how to use.

She shifted back. The change was smoother now, faster. She stood naked and shivering in the center of the chamber, refusing to cross her arms over her chest.

"I don't know my father's line," she said.

Lies wouldn't work. Not here. The wolves would smell them.

Thornwood studied her for a long moment. "No. I suppose you wouldn't." He turned to the class. "Continue. Maya, you will remain."

The other students shifted back. Some dressed with pointed slowness, watching her. Celeste's golden eyes held something new—not contempt, but calculation.

When the last student filed out, Thornwood closed the chamber doors.

"The last silver wolf I saw was killed forty years ago," he said. "He was a pureblood. Powerful. And he went mad."

Maya's throat tightened. "I'm not mad."

"No. You're something else." Thornwood walked to the mirrors, touched his reflection. "Silver wolves were hunted because they could do what others couldn't. They could walk between worlds—the mortal and the spirit, the human and the beast. The Council feared that power."

"I don't have power. I'm half-human."

"Exactly." Thornwood turned back to her. "You are a living contradiction. A hybrid with a wolf that should not exist in your blood. The Council will want to study you. The packs will want to destroy you. And the students—" He smiled, cold and sharp. "The students will decide if you're worth their respect."

He left her alone in the chamber.

Maya dressed slowly, her fingers numb. The silver wolf stirred beneath her skin, restless and hungry. It had tasted freedom for the first time in front of witnesses. It wanted more.

She walked out into the afternoon light, and the whispers followed her like shadows.

By evening, everyone knew.

The dining hall buzzed with it. Maya sat alone at the end of a long table, her tray untouched. Meat and potatoes and gravy that smelled like home but tasted like ash.

"She's a silver."

"I heard she killed her father."

"That's impossible, hybrids can't shift."

"I saw it. Silver. Like the old stories."

Maya kept her eyes on her plate. Breathe. Eat. Survive.

A shadow fell across the table.

Celeste slid into the seat across from her. Her copper hair was braided now, her uniform immaculate. Up close, Maya could see the fine bones of her face, the sharp intelligence in her eyes.

"Your shift was impressive," Celeste said. "For a mutt."

Maya's fork paused halfway to her mouth. "Thanks."

"I didn't say it was a compliment." Celeste leaned forward. "My family has ruled the Northern Pack for seven generations. We don't welcome competition. We crush it."

"I'm not competing with anyone."

"You exist. That's competition enough." Celeste's smile didn't reach her eyes. "I'm going to watch you fall, half-breed. And when you do, I'll be the one holding the knife."

She stood. Walked away. The whispers followed her like a wake.

Maya set down her fork. Her appetite was gone.

That night, she lay in her narrow dorm bed, staring at the ceiling. The room smelled like lavender and wolf musk, a combination she'd never get used to. Her roommate, a quiet girl named Elise, had already fallen asleep, her breathing soft and even.

Maya's wolf stirred.

*They fear us.*

She pressed her palms against her eyes. *Stop.*

*We are stronger. We are faster. We are—*

*Stop.*

The wolf retreated, but reluctantly. Maya felt it pacing at the edges of her consciousness, waiting.

She thought about her mother. About the way her hands shook. About the folder hidden in her closet at home, marked with a single word in her mother's handwriting: *FATHER.*

Maya had never opened it.

Tomorrow, there would be combat trials.

Tomorrow, Celeste would try to break her.

Tomorrow, everyone would watch to see if the silver wolf was a miracle or a monster.

Maya closed her eyes.

The wolf howled in her blood.

And somewhere in the darkness, something howled back.

title: Blood in the Moonlight
content:
The dormitory hall stretched before Maya like a throat waiting to swallow her whole.

Stone walls lined with iron sconces. Flickering blue flames that gave off no heat. The kind of magic she'd only read about in the old texts her mother kept hidden beneath the floorboards of their cottage.

Room 217. End of the hall. Last door on the left.

She'd counted every step from the entrance. Eighty-three. Eighty-four. The number meant nothing. It was just something to focus on besides the way her wolf pressed against her ribs, wanting out, wanting to run, wanting to do anything but walk into a room full of strangers who already hated her.

The door wasn't locked.

Maya pushed it open and stopped breathing.

Three girls looked up from their beds. Two of them sat cross-legged on matching silk comforters, identical in the way wolves from the same bloodline often were. Same sharp cheekbones. Same pale gold eyes that tracked her like prey.

The third girl lay sprawled across the far bed, one arm draped over her face, a phone balanced on her stomach. She didn't move when Maya entered.

"You're late," said the twin on the left.

"Curfew was ten minutes ago," said the right.

Maya set her bag down. "I was with Headmaster Thorne."

The twins exchanged a look. Something passed between them—words without sound, the way born wolves communicated when they didn't want humans to hear.

Except Maya wasn't human.

"My name's Maya." She kept her voice steady. "You can call me Maya or you can call me nothing. I don't care which."

The twin on the right snorted. "We know who you are, half-breed. Everyone knows."

"Then we're done with introductions."

Maya turned to the empty bed. The frame was iron, the mattress thin, the sheets standard issue. Nothing like the silk-covered nests the twins had made for themselves. She didn't care about that either. She'd slept on worse.

She was reaching for the pillow when the air shifted.

The third girl moved faster than Maya's eyes could track. One moment she was lying still. The next she stood beside Maya's bed, close enough that Maya caught the scent of jasmine and iron.

"You're in my spot."

Maya looked at the girl. Dark hair cropped short. Scars that ran from her jaw to her collarbone, pale lines against brown skin. Her eyes were the color of rust—not gold, not silver. Something that had once been bright and had dulled with time.

"There are three empty beds," Maya said.

"Not for you."

"Then where should I sleep?"

The girl smiled. It wasn't friendly. "The kennels are comfortable this time of year. Heated floors. Plenty of space to run."

The twins laughed. The sound was sharp, cutting.

Maya's wolf stirred. Fur rising. Teeth lengthening. The primal part of her that had survived eighteen years of hiding wanted to answer this challenge the way wolves did—with blood and submission.

She held it back.

"Maybe tomorrow," Maya said. "Tonight I'm tired."

She pulled back the sheets and sat on the bed.

The scarred girl watched her for a long moment. Then she shrugged and returned to her own mattress, dropping onto it with the boneless grace of a predator who knew she could strike anytime she wanted.

"Name's Rosa," she said to the ceiling. "Remember it. You'll be saying it a lot when you beg me to go easy on you."

"I don't beg."

"Everyone begs. Eventually."

The twins whispered to each other. The blue flames in the sconces flickered. Maya lay down and stared at the ceiling, counting the cracks in the stone until her eyes burned.

---

Morning came with bells.

Loud. Clanging. The kind of sound that drilled into your skull and stayed there.

Maya was dressed before the second ring. Her uniform hung on the closet door—gray slacks, white blouse, navy blazer with the Silver Fang crest stitched over the heart. The fabric smelled like lavender and something chemical. Preservatives. Like they'd been waiting for a student who might never come.

She pulled them on anyway.

The bathroom was shared. Six stalls. Six sinks. Mirrors that showed your reflection from every angle, making it impossible to hide.

Maya avoided her own eyes.

Rosa stood at the sink, washing her face with water so cold it left frost on her fingers. The twins had already left. Their beds were made with military precision, sheets tight enough to bounce a coin off.

"First day jitters?" Rosa didn't look up. "Don't bother denying it. I can smell your pulse."

Maya turned on the tap. "I'm fine."

"You're terrified. But you hide it better than most humans." Rosa dried her face on a towel that had seen better centuries. "That's why they let you in, you know. Not because you earned it. Because you're interesting."

"Thanks for the vote of confidence."

"I'm not giving you confidence. I'm telling you the truth." Rosa dropped the towel. "The purebloods have a betting pool on how long you last. End of first week is the favorite. Some of the smarter ones have you making it to midterms."

"What do you have?"

Rosa's rust-colored eyes met hers in the mirror. "I don't bet on dead girls."

She walked out.

Maya stood alone in the bathroom, listening to the water drip from the tap. The mirror showed her everything—the slight tremor in her hands, the way her jaw clenched, the dark circles under eyes that looked too human to belong in this place.

Get it together.

She splashed cold water on her face. Three times. Four. Until her skin went numb and her thoughts settled.

Then she went to class.

---

The combat training hall took up the entire east wing.

One hundred feet of open floor. Wooden walls scarred with claw marks. A ceiling high enough to let wolves shift fully without touching the beams.

Maya stood at the edge of the mat with forty other first-years. All of them pureblood. All of them watching her with the same expression—curiosity mixed with contempt.

The instructor was a man named Kael. Mid-thirties. Built like someone who had broken bones for a living and enjoyed it. His left eye was white, the pupil clouded, and the scar that ran through it suggested a claw had done the damage.

"Welcome to Combat Fundamentals," he said. His voice carried without effort. "I'm going to teach you how to hurt people. Some of you will be good at it. Most of you will be average. A few of you will die."

He smiled. It wasn't a joke.

"Today we're going to test baseline aggression. You'll pair up. You'll fight. No weapons. No shifting. Just hands and teeth."

The students shifted. Some cracked their necks. Others rolled their shoulders. The air filled with the smell of adrenaline and wolf musk.

"Sir." A voice from the back. Deep. Confident. "Who do we pair with?"

Maya turned.

The boy who spoke stood a head taller than most. Broad shoulders. Hair the color of ash. Eyes like molten gold that caught the light and held it.

She recognized him. Everyone recognized him.

Lucian Blackwood. Alpha heir of the Blackwood pack. First in every class since he'd entered Silver Fang three years ago. The kind of wolf who didn't need to prove anything because everyone already knew.

Kael followed his gaze. The instructor's scarred face split into something that might have been a grin.

"Good question, Blackwood. You'll partner with our newest student."

The hall went silent.

Maya felt the weight of forty pairs of eyes. Forty wolves waiting to see her fall.

Lucian stepped onto the mat. His movements were fluid, economical. The kind of grace that came from years of training and a body that had never known weakness.

"Maya." He said her name like he was tasting it. "I've heard a lot about you."

"None of it good, I'm sure."

"Actually, I heard you broke Chen's wrist in the entrance exam."

She blinked. "That's not public knowledge."

"It is now." He circled her, slow. "He's been telling everyone it was a lucky shot. But I saw the way he held himself at dinner. He's scared of you."

"Are you?"

Lucian stopped. His golden eyes met hers—and for a moment, something flickered behind them. Not contempt. Not curiosity. Something else. Something she couldn't name.

"I'm terrified," he said.

Then he moved.

Maya barely got her hands up in time. His first strike came for her face, fast enough to blur. She blocked it, felt the impact shiver up her arms. He followed with a kick to her ribs that she deflected at the last second, the heel of his boot scraping her uniform.

He was faster than her. Stronger. More experienced.

But she'd been fighting her whole life.

She dropped low. Swept her leg at his ankles. He jumped over it, landed with perfect balance, but it bought her a second to reset.

The class watched in silence.

Lucian came at her again. A flurry of strikes—fists, elbows, knees. She absorbed some, dodged others. Every hit she took sent pain through her body, but she didn't stop moving.

He was testing her. She realized it after the tenth exchange. He wasn't trying to end the fight. He was probing, searching, measuring her limits.

She didn't have limits. Not anymore.

Maya stopped retreating.

She stepped into his next strike, took the hit to her shoulder, and drove her forehead into his nose.

Bone cracked. Blood sprayed.

Lucian staggered back. His hand came up to his face, came away red. For a long moment, he just stared at her.

Then he laughed.

"Fuck," he said. "You actually hit me."

"You were wide open."

"I was." He wiped the blood on his sleeve. "Most people are too scared to try."

"I'm not most people."

"No." His eyes were different now. Warmer. Hungrier. "You're not."

Kael stepped between them. The instructor grabbed Lucian's chin, tilted his face to examine the damage. "You'll live. Get cleaned up." Then he turned to Maya.

For a long moment, Kael just looked at her.

"Not bad, half-breed." He said it like it cost him something. "You've got instincts. But you telegraph your left hook. Fix that by Friday, or I'll break it."

"Yes, sir."

The class dispersed. Students whispered. Some glared. Others refused to meet her eyes.

Maya walked to the edge of the mat and sat down. Her hands were shaking. Her ribs ached. But she was still standing, and that was more than the betting pool expected.

Rosa appeared beside her. The scarred girl held out a bottle of water.

"That was stupid," she said.

"I know."

"You headbutted the alpha heir in front of everyone."

"I know that too."

Rosa sat down next to her. For a long time, neither of them spoke.

Then Rosa said, quietly: "He's going to come for you tonight."

Maya's blood went cold. "What?"

"The purebloods have a tradition. First full moon of the semester. They take new students out to the forest, show them what it means to run with a real pack." Rosa's voice was flat. "But they don't bring you back the same way. They break you. Make you understand your place."

"Who's 'they'?"

"Everyone." Rosa met her eyes. "I was a half-breed too, once

title: The Moon's Cruelest Joke
content:
The scream died in Maya's throat.

The classroom tilted. Desks warped. Faces blurred into smears of pale flesh and dark uniforms. Her knees buckled. She caught herself on the edge of a desk, knuckles white against the wood grain.

*Something was wrong.*

Her wolf thrashed inside her chest. Not with fear. With *recognition.*

The silver chain around Professor Ashwood's neck glowed. Not warm and soft like candlelight. Cold. Clinical. Like a scalpel under operating room lights.

"You feel it, don't you?" His voice dropped to a whisper that somehow filled every corner of the room. "The tether. The pull."

Maya's lungs burned. She couldn't breathe. Couldn't think. Couldn't do anything but stare at that chain as it pulsed with sickly light.

"That's impossible," she gasped. "Only—only the Moon Goddess—"

"Binds mates together. Yes." Ashwood stepped closer. The chain swung. Maya's chest heaved. "Tell me, Miss Chen. What do you think happens when a half-breed's inner wolf recognizes its fated mate?"

The room erupted.

Gasps. Whispers. Someone laughed—a sharp, brittle sound that cut through the chaos.

"Fated mate?" Lila's voice rang out from the front row. "Professor, that chain only reacts to *true mates.* Purebloods. Not—not *mutts.*"

"And yet." Ashwood stopped three feet from Maya. Close enough that she could see the gray threading through his dark hair. The faint scar cutting through his left eyebrow. The cold satisfaction in his eyes. "The silver burns for her."

Maya's vision swam.

This was wrong. This was *all* wrong. Fated mates were a myth. A bedtime story. Old wolves told tales of the Moon Goddess choosing perfect matches, of chains that glowed when soulmates stood in the same room. But nobody actually believed—

"I don't have a mate," she forced out. "I'm half-human. The bond doesn't—"

"The bond doesn't care about your bloodline." Ashwood's smile didn't reach his eyes. "The Moon Goddess chooses whom she chooses. And apparently, she's chosen... *you.*"

The whispers grew louder.

"It's a trick."

"Professor must have enchanted the chain."

"Can you imagine? A half-breed with a fated mate?"

"They said her mother was Luna's bloodline. What if—"

"Don't be stupid. That's impossible."

Maya's hands shook. She pressed them flat against her thighs, willing them still. Willing her wolf to calm. Willing her heart to stop trying to claw its way out of her ribcage.

"Who is it?" she asked. Her voice cracked. She didn't care. "Who's my mate?"

Ashwood tilted his head. "That's not how the chain works. It only indicates *presence.* Not identity." He paused. "But rest assured, whoever he is, he's in this room."

The world narrowed.

Maya's gaze swept across the classroom. Thirty faces stared back. Some curious. Some disgusted. A few—a very few—looked almost sympathetic.

Alex Blackwood sat in the back corner, jaw tight, hands gripping his desk like he wanted to break it. His eyes met hers for a fraction of a second. Something flickered there. Something she couldn't read.

Then he looked away.

*No. Not Alex. Anyone but Alex.*

"That's enough for today." Ashwood's voice cut through her spiraling thoughts. "Miss Chen, I expect you in my office after sixth period. We have much to discuss."

He turned and walked back to his desk, chain swinging against his chest.

Maya didn't move.

She couldn't.

The bell rang. Students exploded from their seats, conversations already buzzing with gossip and speculation and cruelty. Nobody looked at her. Nobody wanted to be caught staring at the freak who supposedly had a fated mate.

Lila pushed past her shoulder, hard enough to send her stumbling. "Congratulations," she hissed. "You just became the most interesting thing at this school. Try not to disappoint."

Maya didn't respond.

She couldn't.

Her wolf was still thrashing. Still *screaming.* And for the first time in eighteen years, Maya didn't know if she was the one in control.

---

The hallway stretched endless.

Maya walked. One foot in front of the other. Shove through crowds. Ignore the whispers. Pretend she couldn't feel every pair of eyes burning into her back.

She made it to her second class—Defensive Magic, Professor Rourke—and sat in the back. Stared at the board. Didn't see a single word.

*Fated mate.*

The phrase echoed in her skull.

She'd grown up hearing stories. Her mother used to tell them, her eyes soft and faraway, voice dropping to a whisper like she was sharing sacred knowledge. *The Moon Goddess chooses your other half, Maya. The one who completes you. The one you'd die for.*

Her mother had believed.

Maya never had.

Because if fated mates were real, why had her mother ended up with a human? Why had the Moon Goddess abandoned her to a life of hiding, of running, of pretending she didn't have claws and fangs and a wolf desperate to be free?

*The bond doesn't care about your bloodline.*

But it should. It *should.*

She was half-human. Impure. Broken. The Moon Goddess had no business binding her to a pureblood—because who else could it be? The academy was filled with alphas and heirs and ancient bloodlines. No way her mate was another half-breed.

No way.

"Miss Chen."

Maya jerked. Professor Rourke stood over her desk, face unreadable. The rest of the class had already packed up. When had the bell rung?

"You're dismissed," Rourke said. "But I'd suggest you get some rest. You look... pale."

"I'm fine."

"Of course." Rourke's eyes narrowed. "And Miss Chen? If you need to talk to someone about this morning, the school counselor—"

"I said I'm fine."

Maya grabbed her bag and fled.

---

Lunch was a nightmare.

She sat alone at the far end of a table, tray untouched, while students whispered and pointed and laughed. Someone threw a crumpled napkin at her head. She didn't react.

*Who is it?*

The question gnawed at her.

She studied every face she passed. Every boy in the hallway. Every male teacher. Every older student. Did any of them feel it too? That strange, impossible pull?

No. None of them looked at her twice.

Except Alex.

He was standing at the center of the courtyard, surrounded by his usual crowd. Lila clung to his arm. Another girl laughed at something he said. He looked perfect. Untouchable. Like a statue carved from moonlight and arrogance.

His eyes found hers.

For one heartbeat. Two.

Then he turned away.

Maya's chest ached. Her wolf whimpered. And she knew—she *knew*—that whatever the Moon Goddess had planned, it wasn't going to be kind.

---

Sixth period ended.

Maya dragged herself to Professor Ashwood's office. The door was open. He sat behind a mahogany desk, hands folded, chain resting on the blotter like an accusation.

"Sit."

She sat.

"You're scared." His voice was softer now. Almost gentle. "That's normal. Most wolves are terrified when they first feel the bond."

"I'm not a wolf."

"You're both." He leaned forward. "The Moon Goddess doesn't make mistakes, Miss Chen. She chose your mate for a reason. Whether you accept it or not is up to you."

Maya's throat tightened. "Who is it?"

"I don't know."

"Bullshit."

Ashwood's eyebrow rose. "Excuse me?"

"You know something." She gripped the arms of her chair. "You brought that chain today on purpose. You wanted to humiliate me. You wanted everyone to see—"

"I wanted everyone to *know.*" His voice sharpened. "Half-breeds don't belong at Silver Fang. The Council made that clear when they admitted you. But if you have a fated mate—a *pureblood* fated mate—that changes things."

Maya's blood ran cold.

"You're using me."

"I'm helping you." Ashwood smiled. "Think about it. If your mate is powerful enough, connected enough, he could protect you. Give you status. Make you untouchable."

"And if he doesn't want me?"

"Then you're still the half-breed who claims to have a fated bond." Ashwood shrugged. "Either way, you're more valuable than you were yesterday."

Maya stood. Her legs shook. Her hands shook. Her whole body trembled with rage and fear and something else she refused to name.

"I want to go back to my dorm."

"Of course." Ashwood waved a hand. "But consider my offer, Miss Chen. I can help you find your mate. All I ask is a little... cooperation."

She didn't answer.

She walked out.

The door slammed behind her.

---

The dormitory was quiet.

Maya collapsed on her bed, staring at the ceiling. Her wolf had stopped thrashing. Now it just lay there, curled up, waiting.

*For what?*

She didn't know.

She didn't know anything.

Her phone buzzed. A text from an unknown number.

*We need to talk. Meet me behind the gym. Midnight.*

Maya stared at the screen.

No name. No explanation.

But her wolf stirred. Recognized. *Knew.*

Her fingers trembled as she typed: *Who is this?*

Three dots appeared. Disappeared. Appeared again.

Then the reply came.

*Someone who knows what you are.*

*And what you're about to become.*

title: The Moon's Secret Library
content:
The basement corridor smelled like wet stone and old blood.

Maya pressed her back against the cold wall, counting her breaths. Three floors above, the dining hall would be filling with students for the morning meal. They'd notice her empty seat. Some would smirk. Others wouldn't care.

But Maya couldn't eat. Not after what she'd found in the abandoned records room last night.

A silver locket. Tarnished and warped, hidden behind a loose stone in the foundation wall. Inside, a photograph of a woman with Maya's eyes — the same flecks of amber in the irises, the same stubborn set to her jaw.

Her mother.

The woman she'd been told died in childbirth. The woman who supposedly had no wolf, no connection to the clans.

The locket told a different story.

Maya pulled it from her pocket now, running her thumb over the engraved initials on the back. E.V.

Elena Vance.

A name that appeared in none of the academy's records. A name the librarian had claimed didn't exist when Maya had asked yesterday afternoon.

"You should be studying pack hierarchy, not genealogy," the librarian had snapped, her eyes flashing yellow. "Some histories are buried for good reason."

The way she said it — the tremor beneath the words — told Maya everything. Elena Vance was a secret. And secrets at Silver Fang Academy had teeth.

"Maya."

The voice came from the shadows to her left. Low. Male. Familiar.

Damien Blackwood stepped into the dim light, his silver eyes catching the basement's single flickering bulb. The Alpha heir. The boy who'd watched her stumble through her first transformation without offering help. The boy who'd said nothing when the purebloods mocked her wolf form.

"What do you want?" Maya's voice came out harder than she intended.

He held up his hands. "Peace."

"Peace doesn't exist at this school."

"Maybe not. But information does." He took a step closer, close enough that she caught his scent — pine and rain and something wild underneath. "I know what you found."

Maya's blood went cold. "The locket."

"The librarian reported you. Said you were asking questions about an unregistered she-wolf." Damien's jaw tightened. "The Headmaster knows. He's calling a council meeting tonight."

"About me?"

"About your mother."

The words hit like a physical blow. Maya gripped the locket so hard the edges bit into her palm.

"Why would they care about a dead woman?"

Damien looked away. For a moment, he seemed almost human — unsure, uncomfortable, fighting some internal war. When he spoke again, his voice dropped to barely a whisper.

"Because Elena Vance didn't die in childbirth. She was executed."

The corridor tilted. Maya grabbed the wall to steady herself.

"Executed for what?"

"Treason." Damien met her eyes. "She was the first hybrid — half-wolf, half-human. The council sentenced her to death when she refused to give up her human husband. Your father."

"My father died before I was born."

"That's what they told you." Damien's expression softened — almost pitying. "He's alive, Maya. And he's been looking for you for eighteen years."

The words didn't make sense. Couldn't make sense. Maya had grown up in foster homes, shuffled between families who couldn't handle a child with night terrors and too-sharp teeth. She'd learned survival young — how to hide the wolf, how to fake weakness, how to disappear.

And all that time, her father had been searching?

"How do you know this?"

"Because my mother was on the council that condemned Elena." Damien's voice cracked. "Because I read the classified records. Because I've been trying to find you since I was twelve years old."

Maya's wolf surged beneath her skin, claws threatening to break through. "You knew about me? Before I came here?"

"I knew a hybrid existed. I didn't know where until the admissions office flagged your bloodwork." He stepped closer, close enough that she could see the guilt written in every line of his face. "I'm the reason you were admitted. I pulled strings, called in favors, threatened my father's political allies. I needed you here."

"Needed me here for what?"

"To finish what your mother started."

The locket burned against Maya's palm. She wanted to run. Wanted to shift and tear through the basement walls and never stop running.

But the wolf inside her — the half that had always felt incomplete, always hungering for something she couldn't name — that part of her wanted answers.

"What did my mother start?"

Damien glanced down the corridor, checking for listeners. When he turned back, his eyes held something new. Fear.

"She was building an army. A hybrid army. Wolves who wouldn't be bound by pack law, who could move between worlds, who could challenge the council's authority." He swallowed hard. "She was three months from succeeding when they caught her."

Three months. Maya did the math in her head, the dates lining up like puzzle pieces. Three months before her birth. Three months before the execution.

"Then what?"

"Then the council destroyed everything she'd built. Killed her followers, burned her research, erased her name from every record." Damien's voice dropped to barely audible. "Except one. A hidden library, buried beneath the academy's original foundation. Your mother's library. It's been sealed for eighteen years."

Maya's heart hammered. "Where?"

"The Headmaster's office. Behind the portrait of his grandfather. There's a passage that leads down."

"And you expect me to just walk in there?"

"I expect you to be smart about it." Damien pulled a folded paper from his jacket. A map, hand-drawn, the ink fresh. "There's a council meeting tonight. The Headmaster will be occupied for at least two hours. That's your window."

Maya took the map, her fingers brushing his. A spark of heat traveled up her arm — the wolf recognizing something in him, some kinship she didn't understand.

"Why are you helping me?"

Damien's smile held no warmth. "Because my mother sat on that council. Because I've spent my whole life knowing my family helped murder an innocent woman." He stepped back, the shadows reclaiming him. "And because the moon doesn't forget debts, Maya. Neither do I."

He disappeared into the darkness before she could respond.

Maya stood alone in the basement corridor, clutching the locket in one hand and the map in the other. Above her, the academy hummed with morning activity — students laughing, doors slamming, the distant clatter of plates.

They had no idea what lurked beneath their feet.

She unfolded the map, tracing the route with her finger. Through the kitchens, past the boiler room, up a service staircase that had been bricked over and reopened. A path designed for ghosts.

For the daughter of a traitor.

The dining hall bells rang, signaling the end of breakfast. Maya folded the map and tucked it into her pocket next to the locket.

She had two hours. Two hours to find her mother's secrets, to unearth the truth that had been buried for eighteen years.

Two hours to decide if she was ready to become the enemy the council had always feared.

As she climbed the stairs toward the main floor, Maya felt something shift inside her. The wolf that had always felt like a curse, a burden, a thing to hide — it stretched and yawned and looked at the world through new eyes.

Not a curse.

A weapon.

And tonight, she would learn how to wield it.

The portrait of Headmaster Aldric's grandfather stared down at her with painted eyes that seemed to follow her every move. Maya checked her watch. 7:47 PM. The council meeting had started thirteen minutes ago.

She pressed her ear to the oak paneling beside the portrait. Silence.

The map said the trigger was hidden in the painting's frame — a small indent where the gilded wood had been carved with a crescent moon. Maya found it easily, her fingers tracing the symbol.

She pressed.

Nothing happened.

She pressed harder, pushing at different angles, desperation clawing up her throat. The portrait remained still, the wall solid.

"Think," she whispered to herself. "Think like Elena."

Her mother had been a hybrid. A creature of two worlds, bound by neither. The lock — if it could be called a lock — would require something similarly split.

Maya pulled out the locket, holding it up to the painted moon on the frame.

The silver began to glow.

A soft clicking sound came from behind the portrait, followed by the groan of ancient mechanisms waking from a long sleep. The painting swung inward, revealing a narrow staircase descending into absolute darkness.

Maya's wolf stirred, sensing something ancient below. Something that smelled like moonlight and blood and home.

She took the first step down.

The darkness swallowed her whole.

And somewhere in the depths of the academy, a door that hadn't opened in eighteen years creaked wide, inviting the daughter of Elena Vance to finally claim her inheritance.

title: The Wolf She Hid
content:
The library smelled like old paper and secrets.

Maya pressed her palm flat against the spine of *Bloodline Hierarchies of the Northern Packs* and pulled. The book slid out with a soft whisper, leather binding cracked and warm from generations of hands. She'd spent the last hour hunting for anything—anything at all—about hybrids.

Nothing.

Not a single mention in any of the twelve books she'd already checked. Like people like her didn't exist.

The library's main hall stretched three stories high, oak paneled and lit by crystal chandeliers that flickered with enchanted flame. Students hunched over tables in small clusters, their whispers carrying like wind through leaves. Purebloods. Every single one of them. Maya caught the way they glanced at her, quick and dismissive, before returning to their books.

She didn't care.

Her fingers traced the table of contents in the book she held. Chapter Seven: *The Lunar Bloodline*. Chapter Twelve: *Alpha Succession and the Firstborn Mandate*. Chapter Eighteen: *Anomalies and Aberrations*.

Page 347.

Her heart kicked.

She flipped through the pages, past diagrams of family trees and faded photographs of wolves with eyes like molten gold. Page 332. 339. 345.

347.

The heading read: *On the Matter of Mixed Bloodlines*.

Maya pulled out the chair and sat, the wood scraping against the floor. She read the first paragraph once, then again, her jaw tightening with each word.

*The mingling of wolf and human blood produces offspring of diminished capacity. Such creatures lack the full connection to the moon, the strength of the pack bond, and the primal instincts required for survival in our world. History records no instance of a mixed-blood achieving the rank of Beta or higher. They are, by nature, broken.*

Broken.

She shut the book. The sound echoed through the quiet hall. A few heads turned. She stared back until they looked away.

The book was wrong. It had to be. She'd survived eighteen years, hidden her wolf from hunters who would have killed her for what she was, made it through the first week of this school without shifting in public and exposing herself. That wasn't broken.

That was steel.

She pushed the book across the table and stood. The air in the library felt too thin, too old, too full of centuries of pureblood certainty that people like her didn't belong. She needed air. She needed space.

She needed to run.

The hallway outside stretched long and empty, the last of the sunset bleeding through tall windows in shades of orange and red. Maya walked fast, her boots clicking against the marble, her mind still turning over the words she'd read.

*Diminished capacity.*

*Broken.*

A door opened ahead of her. Kael stepped out, his dark hair slightly damp like he'd just showered, his grey eyes finding her immediately. He wore a simple black t-shirt and jeans, no uniform, no pretension. Just him.

He looked at her face and stopped walking.

"You found something."

It wasn't a question.

"Your library has a really charming section on hybrids," Maya said, her voice flat. "Very educational. Apparently I'm broken."

Something flickered in his eyes. Anger, maybe. Or recognition. "You shouldn't have read that."

"Why? Because it's lies?"

"Because it's politics." He stepped closer, his voice dropping. "That book was written by the Crimson Fang Alpha Council. They've spent three generations trying to keep the bloodlines pure. Of course they're going to write garbage about anyone who isn't a full wolf."

"Then where's the truth?" Maya heard the edge in her own voice, the sharpness she couldn't smooth away. "Where's the book that says hybrids aren't broken?"

Kael's jaw worked. He looked away, down the empty hallway, then back at her. "There isn't one."

The words hit like a punch to the chest.

"Because no one's written it," he continued. "Because no mixed-blood has ever made it through an academy like this. Because the Council makes sure any record of success gets buried."

Maya stared at him. "You knew."

"I knew the books were slanted. I didn't know exactly what they said." He ran a hand through his hair. "But I knew the school wouldn't make it easy for you."

"Easy." She laughed, but there was no humor in it. "Easy would have been staying human. Pretending I didn't have a wolf at all. I came here because I wanted to learn what I am. And instead I get a library full of propaganda telling me I'm a mistake."

"You're not a mistake."

The words came out rough, intense. Kael's eyes held hers, and for a moment the hallway felt like it had shrunk to just the two of them. Maya's breath caught.

"Then what am I?" she asked.

He opened his mouth. Closed it. Shook his head.

"I don't know. But I want to find out."

Something passed between them then, something Maya didn't have words for. It wasn't just attraction—though that was there too, a low hum she couldn't ignore. It was recognition. Like he saw something in her that she hadn't seen in herself.

The moment broke when footsteps echoed from the far end of the hall.

Kael stepped back, his expression smoothing into neutrality. "Come on. I'll show you the restricted section."

"You have a restricted section?"

"Every school with secrets does."

He led her through a door hidden behind a tapestry, down a spiral staircase that smelled of dust and old magic. The air grew cooler with each step. At the bottom, a single iron door stood barred with three locks, each one glowing faintly with engraved symbols.

Kael pressed his palm to the center of the door. The locks clicked open one by one.

"Only full wolves can open it," he said. "The magic reads your bloodline."

Maya looked at the open door. At him. "You brought a hybrid to your secret bloodlocked library."

"I told you." He pushed the door open. "I want to find out what you are."

The room beyond was small, circular, lined with books that seemed to breathe. Maya felt them as she stepped inside—old magic, old knowledge, old secrets pressing against her skin. She ran her fingers along the spines, reading titles in languages she didn't recognize.

"These are the real histories," Kael said. "Before the Council started rewriting them. There's got to be something in here about—"

He stopped.

Maya stopped too.

Because on the central table, open to a page marked with a black ribbon, sat a book she hadn't touched. A book that had been waiting for her.

She walked toward it slowly, her heart hammering. The page showed a drawing of a wolf unlike any she'd seen—half in shadow, half in light, its eyes burning silver rather than gold.

The text beneath read:

*The Shadow Wolf. Neither pureblood nor human. Born from the union of two worlds, meant to bridge them. Feared by those who cling to the old ways. The first of her kind in three centuries.*

*The last of her kind, if the Council has its way.*

Maya's hands trembled as she touched the page.

"Kael." Her voice came out barely a whisper. "This book knew I was coming."

He was beside her now, reading over her shoulder. She felt the warmth of him, the solid presence. "That's not possible."

"Then explain it."

Neither of them could.

The page beneath her fingers seemed to pulse, the ink shifting like it was alive. The silver-eyed wolf in the drawing turned its head.

Maya jerked her hand back.

The image stilled.

"What the hell," she breathed.

Kael grabbed her wrist, his grip firm. "We need to get out. Now."

"Why?"

"Because that book is spelled to respond to a specific bloodline. And if it's responding to yours, then the Council already knows you're here."

"The Council? The same Council that wants hybrids—"

A sound cut her off. Heavy footsteps from above. Voices.

The library door was still open.

Kael cursed under his breath. He grabbed the book from the table, shoved it into Maya's hands, and pulled her toward a narrow passage she hadn't noticed behind a shelf.

"Go. Don't stop until you reach the end."

"What about you?"

"I'll hold them off. They can't know you were here."

"Kael—"

"Go!" He pushed her into the passage, his eyes fierce. "Trust me."

She wanted to argue. Every instinct screamed at her to stay, to fight, to demand answers. But the footsteps were getting closer, and the book in her hands felt warm, urgent, alive.

She ran.

The passage twisted and turned, dark and narrow, her shoulders brushing stone walls. She held the book close, her breath ragged, her mind a storm of questions.

The Shadow Wolf.

Her kind.

The passage ended at a metal grate. She pushed it open and tumbled out into the night, into the cold air and the silver moonlight. She was behind the dorms, hidden by trees, the library a dark silhouette against the stars.

She looked down at the book.

The page had changed.

Now it showed her.

Maya, drawn in ink that seemed to glow, her eyes open and silver, her wolf form half-visible behind her like a shadow cast in moonlight.

And beneath it, new words:

*She comes. The Council sees. The hunt begins.*

Maya's blood turned to ice.

She heard footsteps behind her. Not from the library.

From the trees.

She turned, slow, the book clutched to her chest.

Three wolves stepped out of the shadows. Not students. Older. Bigger. Their eyes glowed amber in the dark.

One of them spoke, and his voice was gravel and menace.

"Maya Chen. You're coming with us."

title: The Hunt Begins
content:
Maya's muscles locked. The book pressed against her ribs like a second heartbeat, warm and pulsing. Three wolves. Not students. Not professors. These were something else entirely—their fur darker, their eyes ancient, their presence pressing down on her like physical weight.

"You have something that doesn't belong to you." The speaker was the largest, a brute with a scar cutting through his left eye. His voice scraped against her ears, rough and commanding.

Maya's mind raced. Kael was still in the library. The dorms were fifty yards behind her. The forest stretched in every other direction, dark and unknown.

"I don't know what you're talking about." She forced her voice steady. "I was just taking a walk."

The wolf on the left let out something between a growl and a laugh. "The book, girl. Hand it over, and maybe we'll let you keep your limbs attached."

So they knew. Of course they knew. The book had said as much—*the Council sees, the hunt begins.* These weren't random enforcers. They were sent specifically for her.

Maya's fingers tightened on the leather cover. Something shifted inside the book, a vibration that traveled up her arms and settled in her chest. Her wolf stirred, restless, pushing against the edges of her control.

"I don't think so." The words came out stronger than she felt.

The scarred wolf stepped forward, his massive paws silent on the grass. "You think you have a choice? You're a half-breed playing at being a student. You don't belong here. You never did. The only reason you're still breathing is because the Council wanted to see if you'd self-destruct first."

Maya's blood heated. Every insecurity, every whispered insult, every moment of doubt—he'd just thrown them in her face like weapons.

"Is that what happened to the others?" She didn't know where the question came from. Instinct, maybe. Or the book, feeding her words. "The hybrids before me?"

The scarred wolf's eyes flickered. A reaction. A crack in his confidence.

"There were no others."

"Liar." The word came out cold. "The book says I'm the first in three centuries. That means there were others before that. What happened to them?"

The three wolves exchanged glances. Something passed between them—communication, silent and swift.

The scarred wolf's growl deepened. "Last warning, girl. The book. Now."

Maya made a decision. Not with her head. With something deeper. Something that had been sleeping inside her for eighteen years, waiting for this exact moment.

"No."

She turned and ran.

The forest swallowed her whole. Branches whipped at her face, roots tried to trip her, but she moved with a speed she'd never known she possessed. Her lungs burned. Her legs pumped. The book stayed clutched against her chest, its warmth guiding her like a compass pointing north.

Behind her, the wolves gave chase. She heard their paws pounding the earth, their breath hot and hungry. They were faster than her. Stronger. Older.

But they didn't have the book.

Maya veered left, ducking under a fallen log, then right, splashing through a shallow stream. The moonlight filtered through the canopy in silver threads, barely enough to see by. She relied on instinct, on the wolf inside her that was clawing to get out.

*Let me out,* it urged. *I can run faster. Fight harder. I can protect us.*

*I don't know how,* Maya screamed back. *I've never shifted before.*

*Then learn. Now.*

A shape crashed through the trees to her left. The scarred wolf, outpacing his companions, his amber eyes locked on her. He was close. Too close. She could smell him—earth and blood and old magic.

Maya pushed harder, but her legs were giving out. She wasn't built for this. She'd spent eighteen years hiding, pretending, surviving by staying small and invisible. Now she was running for her life, and her body was betraying her.

The book pulsed. Heat seared through her palms.

And suddenly, she *knew.*

Not in words. In bones. In blood. The knowledge poured into her like water into dry earth—how to shift, when to shift, what it meant to become something more than human. The book wasn't just teaching her. It was *unlocking* her.

Maya skidded to a stop.

The scarred wolf lunged.

Time fractured.

Maya felt her skin rip, her bones realign, her entire being fold in on itself and expand at the same moment. Pain. Ecstasy. Terror. Freedom. All of it crashed together in a single, impossible second.

And then she was on four legs.

Her wolf form was smaller than his. Leaner. Her fur was dark, almost black, with streaks of silver that caught the moonlight like liquid metal. Her eyes—she could feel them burning, could see the world in colors she'd never known existed.

The scarred wolf skidded to a halt, his eyes widening. "Impossible. You're not supposed to be able to shift until the Blood Moon ceremony."

Maya couldn't speak, but she didn't need to. Her body answered for her. She lunged.

They collided in a tangle of fur and fangs. He was bigger, but she was faster. He had centuries of experience; she had raw, untamed instinct and the book's magic still singing through her veins. She dodged his first bite, sank her teeth into his foreleg, and tasted blood.

He howled and threw her off. She hit a tree trunk hard, pain exploding through her ribs, but she scrambled up before he could press his advantage.

The other two wolves burst through the trees. Three against one.

Maya's wolf didn't hesitate. It crouched, ready to fight to the death.

*No.* Her human mind fought for control. *This is suicide.*

*This is survival,* her wolf answered.

"Enough."

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. It silenced the forest. Even the wind stopped blowing.

A fourth wolf emerged from the shadows. This one was different. Smaller. Older. Her fur was pure white, her eyes a pale blue that seemed to see through flesh and bone into the soul beneath.

The three enforcers immediately bowed their heads. "Elder Sable."

The white wolf ignored them. She walked past them, straight to Maya, and stopped inches away. Maya's wolf held its ground, trembling but unwilling to submit.

"You shifted early." Elder Sable's voice was soft, almost kind. "The book chose you. Do you know what that means?"

Maya couldn't answer. She was fighting to hold her wolf form, to keep from collapsing, to understand what was happening.

"It means you're the one we've been waiting for." Elder Sable's eyes glowed. "And the one they've been hunting."

She turned to the three enforcers. "Leave. Tell the Council that the girl stays. If they want her, they'll have to go through me."

"Elder Sable, with respect—" the scarred wolf started.

"I wasn't asking." Her voice hardened into something ancient and terrible. "Go. Now."

They went. They didn't run, but they moved with the speed of creatures who knew better than to argue.

The moment they were gone, Elder Sable turned back to Maya. "You have questions. I have answers. But first, you need to shift back."

Maya tried. She focused, pushed, willed her bones to reform, her skin to reknit. Nothing happened. Her wolf form felt permanent, locked in place by adrenaline and fear and the overwhelming newness of being *this.*

"I can't," she tried to say. It came out as a whine.

Elder Sable's expression softened. "First shift is always disorienting. You're not stuck. You're just not ready to let go yet. Breathe. Trust yourself."

Maya breathed. She thought of Kael, still in the library, probably fighting his own battle. She thought of the book, still clutched in her paws, its magic warm and patient. She thought of her mother, who had died protecting a secret Maya was only beginning to understand.

She let go.

The shift back was gentler than the shift into wolf. Like water smoothing over rocks. She collapsed onto the forest floor, naked, exhausted, the book somehow still in her hands.

Elder Sable shifted too, becoming an old woman with silver hair and kind eyes. She wore a simple white dress, unbothered by the cold. She draped a cloak over Maya's shoulders.

"You have her eyes," Elder Sable said softly. "Your mother's eyes."

Maya's breath caught. "You knew my mother?"

"I did. I was there when she died." The old woman's voice cracked. "I was there when she made me promise to find you, when the time was right."

"The time for what?"

Elder Sable knelt beside her, her ancient face full of sorrow and hope. "For the war that's coming. The war between those who want to keep the old ways, and those who believe hybrids have a right to exist. Your mother died to protect that war from starting too soon. But it's starting now, Maya. And you're the only one who can end it."

Maya stared at her, the book warm against her chest, her body still trembling from her first shift.

"I'm just a half-breed who can't even control her own wolf."

Elder Sable smiled, sad and knowing. "No, child. You're the Shadow Wolf. The bridge between worlds. And the Council just found out you exist."

She helped Maya to her feet.

"Now, let's get you back before that boy tears the library apart looking for you."

Maya looked up toward the academy. A howl split the night—Kael's howl, desperate and furious.

He'd found the library empty. He'd found her gone.

And soon, everyone would know she was something more than a failed experiment.

The hunt had only just begun.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Price of Answers

The three wolves circled, their movements synchronized, practiced. Hunters. I'd seen that gait before, in the forests back home, from predators who knew their prey had nowhere left to run.

"Don't make this harder than it needs to be," the one who'd spoken said. He was the largest, his fur a deep charcoal, scars crossing his muzzle like old battle wounds. "The Council just wants to talk."

"Funny," I said, my voice steadier than I felt. "That's what people always say before they kill you."

The wolf on the left laughed, a low, ugly sound. "She's got spirit. Pity."

I tightened my grip on the book. It was warm against my chest, almost hot now, like it was feeding off my fear. Or my anger. I couldn't tell which.

The charcoal wolf took a step forward. "The book. Hand it over, and we'll be gentle."

"You mean the book that tells me I'm the first hybrid in three centuries? The one that says your Council wants me dead?" I let the words hang in the air. "Yeah. Not happening."

His eyes narrowed. "Then we do this the hard way."

He lunged.

I dove left, rolling through the dirt, the book still pressed against me. I felt his claws rake the air where I'd been standing, felt the wind of his passage. I scrambled up, my heart hammering, my wolf surging beneath my skin, demanding release.

But I couldn't shift. Not yet. Not until I understood what I was, what I could become. Shifting now meant losing control, and losing control meant death.

The other two wolves flanked me, cutting off my escape routes. The trees were too dense to run through, the dorms too far. I was trapped.

"You can't win, girl," the charcoal wolf said, turning to face me again. "You're half-human. Weak. Broken. The Council has ended stronger wolves than you."

"Then why are you still talking?"

He snarled and charged.

This time, I didn't dodge.

I dropped to my knees, let him sail over me, and slammed the book into his back as he passed. He yelped, stumbled, crashed into a tree. The impact shook leaves loose, sent them spiraling down like dark snow.

The other two hesitated. They hadn't expected me to fight back.

Neither had I.

The book pulsed in my hands, and I felt something shift inside me. Not my wolf. Something older. Something that had been sleeping, waiting.

I looked down at the open page. The ink was moving again, forming new words: *The wolf within is not your only weapon. You carry her fire. Use it.*

Her fire. My mother's fire.

My human mother, who had died before I could remember her face. Who had been told she was worthless by the same Council that now hunted me. Who had loved a wolf and paid for it with her life.

The anger I'd been holding back broke free.

I didn't think. I didn't plan. I just raised my hand and *pushed*.

A wave of silver light exploded from my palm, catching the two wolves mid-lunge. They flew backward, crashing into each other, rolling across the clearing. The charcoal wolf got to his feet, his eyes wide, his bravado gone.

"What the hell are you?"

I looked at my hand. Silver light still flickered around my fingers, faint but real. I had no idea what I'd done, how I'd done it, or if I could do it again.

But he didn't know that.

"I'm the girl who's going to burn your Council to the ground," I said. "Now get out of my sight before I decide to start with you."

He held my gaze for a long moment. Then he growled, turned, and disappeared into the trees. The other two followed, limping, whimpering.

I stood there, shaking, the cool night air biting at my skin. The silver light faded. The book went cold in my hands.

I'd just done something impossible. Something that wasn't supposed to exist.

And I had no idea what it meant.

---

I didn't go back to my dorm.

I couldn't. My roommate, a pureblood named Serena who looked at me like I was something she'd scraped off her shoe, would have a thousand questions. And I didn't have a thousand answers.

Instead, I found a hidden alcove behind the gymnasium, a forgotten corner where the maintenance crew stored old equipment. I sat on a cracked wrestling mat, the book open on my lap, and tried to make sense of what I'd seen.

The page had changed again.

The drawing of me was gone. In its place was a map—a detailed, intricate map of Silver Fang Academy, but different. There were tunnels I'd never seen, rooms that didn't exist in the official blueprints, and one location marked with a glowing silver dot.

The library's restricted section.

I traced the dot with my finger. The ink rippled, and words appeared beneath it: *The Shadow Wolf's legacy awaits. But first, you must survive the night.*

"Great," I muttered. "Thanks for the vote of confidence."

A twig snapped behind me.

I spun, the book clutched to my chest, silver light flickering at my fingertips again. But it wasn't the Council's wolves.

It was Kael.

He looked worse than when I'd left him. His shirt was torn, a cut bleeding above his eyebrow, his knuckles raw and bruised. But he was standing, and he was alive, and for a second, I felt something that wasn't fear or anger.

Relief.

"You're okay," I said. It came out softer than I intended.

"Define okay." He winced, touching his ribs. "Three Council enforcers. I held them off long enough for you to get out, but they got some hits in."

"Enforcers? Those were Council?"

"Elite guard. They don't send them for students." He limped closer, his eyes landing on the book. "You found something."

"I found a lot of things." I held up the book. "This thing is alive, Kael. It knows me. It showed me—" I stopped, the words catching in my throat. "It showed me what I am."

He was quiet for a moment. Then he sat down across from me, wincing as he bent his knees.

"A Shadow Wolf."

I stared at him. "You knew?"

"I suspected. When I saw the book respond to you, when I saw the way the Council reacted to your name." He met my eyes. "Shadow Wolves are legends, Maya. Stories the elders tell to scare pups. Wolves born of two worlds, with powers that don't follow the rules."

"Powers like what? Shooting silver light out of my hands?"

His eyes widened. "You did that?"

"I don't know how. I don't know why. I just—" I looked at my hands, still trembling. "I was scared. And angry. And then it just happened."

Kael leaned forward, his voice urgent. "Listen to me. What you did tonight, what you found—the Council will do anything to destroy it. And you. They've spent centuries keeping the truth about hybrids buried. If the other packs found out that Shadow Wolves exist, that they can wield power like that—"

"Then the Council loses control."

"Yes."

I looked down at the book. The map was still glowing, the silver dot pulsing like a heartbeat.

"There's something in the restricted section," I said. "Something the book wants me to find. It says it's my legacy."

Kael's face went pale. "Maya, no. The restricted section is sealed by Council magic. If you go in there, they'll know. They'll kill you."

"They're going to kill me anyway." I closed the book, stood up. "But at least this way, I'll know why."

He stood too, his hand catching my wrist. "Then I'm coming with you."

"Kael, you're injured. You can barely stand."

"I can stand well enough to fight." His grip tightened. "You don't have to do this alone."

I looked at him, at the determination in his eyes, the blood on his face. I'd known him for less than a week. He had no reason to risk his life for me.

But he was here anyway.

"Why?" I asked. "Why help me?"

He held my gaze. "Because my father was on the Council. And I know what they did to people like you. I've been looking for the truth my whole life." He nodded at the book. "Looks like you found it first. I'm not letting you face it alone."

I wanted to argue. Every instinct told me to push him away, to protect him from whatever was coming.

But I was tired of being alone.

"Fine," I said. "But if we die, I'm haunting you."

He almost smiled. "Deal."

---

The restricted section was hidden behind a wall in the library's deepest corner, accessible only through a door that didn't exist unless you knew what to look for.

The book showed me how.

I pressed my palm against the stone, and the wall rippled, a door appearing where there had been none. The handle was cold iron, carved with symbols I didn't recognize.

"This is it," I whispered.

Kael stood behind me, his breathing steady despite his injuries. "Once we go in, there's no turning back."

"I know."

I turned the handle.

The door swung open, revealing a spiral staircase descending into darkness. The air that rose from below was cold, ancient, heavy with secrets.

I took the first step.

And then I heard it.

A howl. Not from outside. From below.

From the heart of the restricted section.

The book in my hands burned hot, and the silver dot on the map began to move.

Not toward the restricted section.

Away from it.

Straight toward me.

The howl came again, closer this time, and I realized with dawning horror that the map wasn't showing me where to go.

It was showing me what was coming.

The silver dot reached my location. The map went dark.

And from the depths of the staircase, a voice spoke. Not a wolf's voice. Something older. Something that had been waiting.

"Hello, little Shadow. I've been expecting you."

Kael grabbed my arm, pulling me back. "Maya. We need to run."

But I couldn't move. I couldn't breathe. Because the voice wasn't coming from the darkness.

It was coming from inside my head.

And it knew my name.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Hunt Begins

Maya's wolf surged beneath her skin, demanding release. Three elders. Full-grown. Battle-hardened. She'd seen their kind before—enforcers who answered only to the Council. They didn't negotiate. They didn't reason. They took.

"The book," the lead wolf growled. He was massive, his fur the color of wet stone, his eyes ancient and cold. "Hand it over. Come quietly. No one gets hurt."

Maya's grip tightened on the leather-bound text. It pulsed against her palms, warm as a heartbeat. "Who sent you?"

"You know who."

"Then tell them I'm not interested in their invitation."

The wolf's lip curled. "That wasn't a request."

He moved. Fast. Faster than she expected. But Maya had spent eighteen years running from things bigger than her. She ducked, rolled, came up with the book pressed to her chest and her claws extended.

She hadn't meant to shift. It just happened.

Half-form. Her fingers elongated, nails blackening into points. Her canines dropped. Her vision sharpened until she could count the individual hairs on the wolf's muzzle. The power thrummed through her, wild and unfamiliar, like wearing a coat that didn't quite fit.

But it fit better than it had yesterday.

The elder paused. His eyes narrowed. "So it's true. The hybrid can shift."

"Surprise," Maya said, her voice rougher, edged with something feral. "I'm full of them."

The other two wolves circled, flanking her. She was fast, but they were three. Experienced. They wouldn't make the mistakes the students made.

"Last chance," the lead wolf said. "The book. Your compliance. Or we take both by force."

Maya thought about running. The trees were close. She could shift fully, disappear into the dark, find Kael, figure out what the hell was happening. But the book felt different now—heavier, more urgent. Like it was trying to tell her something.

She looked down.

The ink had changed again. New words, forming as she watched:

*They fear what you'll become. Show them.*

Maya's breath caught. The book was *talking* to her. Not in a voice, but in instinct, in the way her wolf understood things her human mind couldn't quite grasp. The words weren't instructions. They were permission.

She looked up at the three wolves. At their amber eyes and bared teeth. At the way they stood like they'd already won.

"I'm not going anywhere," she said.

The lead wolf lunged.

Maya met him head-on.

She didn't think. Didn't plan. Her body moved on its own, the half-form responding faster than her conscious mind could follow. She sidestepped his charge, hooked her claws into his flank, and used his momentum to spin him into a tree. He hit with a crack of wood and a grunt of surprise.

The other two came at her together.

Maya dropped low. Swept one's legs. Elbowed the other in the throat before he could recover. They were bigger, stronger, but they fought like enforcers—by the book, by the rules. Maya fought like someone who'd never had rules. Like someone who'd learned to survive in a world that wanted her dead.

She was on the third wolf before he could stand, her knee on his chest, her claws at his throat. "Tell the Council," she said, her voice low and dangerous, "that I'm not their property. Not their experiment. Not their weapon."

The wolf beneath her bared his teeth but didn't move.

"I'm not afraid of them," Maya continued. "And if they want this book, they can come get it themselves."

She released him. Backed away. Kept the book pressed to her chest as she disappeared into the trees.

She ran until her lungs burned, until the dorms were a distant glow through the branches, until she was sure no one followed. Then she collapsed against a birch tree, her half-form receding, her hands shaking as she looked down at the book.

The page was blank now. Just old paper, faintly warm, smelling of dust and something metallic.

But when she touched it, she felt it again. That pulse. That awareness.

The book knew her.

And somewhere in the darkness of Silver Fang Academy, she suspected, the book had always been waiting.

---

She found Kael in the greenhouse, just past midnight. He was pacing, his jaw tight, his hands shoved in his pockets. When he saw her, the relief that crossed his face was almost painful.

"You're alive."

"Barely." She held up the book. "Your library is very hospitable."

He took it from her, turning it over in his hands. "This is older than anything I've ever seen in the restricted section. The binding, the script—this is pre-Council. Pre-Academy. This is from before the hybrids were—" He stopped.

"Before they were what?"

Kael's jaw worked. "Before they were eliminated."

The word hit her like a punch. Eliminated. Not killed. Not murdered. Eliminated. Like a problem. Like a mistake.

"And now I have it," Maya said. "And the Council knows I have it. And they sent enforcers to take it back."

Kael's head snapped up. "Enforcers? Here?"

"Three of them. Big. Ugly. Very insistent."

He cursed, running a hand through his hair. "That's not supposed to happen. The Academy is neutral ground. The Council isn't supposed to interfere with students."

"Well, someone forgot to send them the memo."

Kael started pacing again, faster now. "If they sent enforcers, it means they're desperate. It means this book is more important than I thought. It means—" He stopped. Turned to face her. "Maya, what did you see in the pages?"

She thought about the silver-eyed wolf. The shifting ink. The drawing of herself. "It showed me. My wolf. And words. It said the hunt begins."

Kael's face went pale. "The hunt. That's not a metaphor. The Council has a protocol for threats to the pureblood order. They call it the Hunt. It means they've authorized capture by any means necessary."

"Capture? Or kill?"

He didn't answer. He didn't have to.

Maya felt the cold settle into her bones. She'd known, on some level, that being a hybrid made her a target. But this was different. This was organized. This was centuries of prejudice and fear and power, all focused on her.

"So what do I do?" she asked. "Run? Hide? Give them the book?"

"You do none of those things." Kael's voice was hard now, determined. "You learn what's in that book. You learn what you are. And then you decide what you want to become."

"That's a nice speech. It doesn't answer my question."

"It's the only answer I have." He handed the book back to her. "I can't protect you from the Council. No one can. But I can help you understand what you're dealing with."

Maya looked down at the book. It felt heavier now. More real. "Why are you helping me? You don't know me. I could be dangerous. I could be exactly what the Council says I am."

Kael was quiet for a moment. Then he said, "Because I've seen what happens to people who don't fight. Who give up. Who let the Council decide their worth." His voice dropped. "I won't watch it happen to you."

She wanted to ask what he meant. Wanted to push, to understand the shadows that passed behind his eyes when he talked about the Council. But before she could, the greenhouse door slammed open.

Professor Thorne stood in the doorway, her face carved from stone, her eyes fixed on Maya.

"Maya Chen. The Headmaster wants to see you. Immediately."

Maya's blood went cold. She looked at Kael. His expression was unreadable.

"The book," Thorne said. "Bring it."

It wasn't a request.

Maya followed her across the campus, the book tucked under her arm, her heart hammering so loud she was sure everyone could hear it. The halls were empty, the students confined to their dorms, the silence oppressive.

The Headmaster's office was in the oldest part of the Academy, a tower that predated the rest of the buildings by centuries. The stairs spiraled upward, worn smooth by generations of feet. At the top, a heavy oak door stood open.

Inside, the Headmaster sat behind his desk, his hands folded, his face unreadable. He was old. Ancient, even for a werewolf. His eyes were the color of ash, and they held a weight that made Maya want to shrink.

"Close the door," he said.

Thorne did. The click of the lock echoed in the small room.

"Sit."

Maya sat. She placed the book on her lap, her hands resting on its cover.

The Headmaster studied her for a long moment. Then he said, "You've had an eventful first week."

"You could say that."

"You've made enemies. You've made allies. You've broken into the restricted section, fought the Academy's enforcers, and uncovered a book that hasn't seen the light of day in three hundred years." He leaned back. "You've done more in seven days than most students do in seven years."

"I'm an overachiever."

A ghost of a smile crossed his face. "So it seems."

He reached into his desk and pulled out a file. Thin. Official-looking. He slid it across the desk toward her.

Maya opened it. Inside was a single photograph. A woman, young, her face familiar in ways that made Maya's chest ache. She had Maya's eyes. Maya's jaw. Maya's stubborn chin.

"Your mother," the Headmaster said. "Elena Chen. Human. She died when you were three."

Maya's hands trembled. She'd never seen a picture of her mother. Her father had burned them all after the funeral. "How do you know about her?"

"Because your mother came to this Academy, Maya. Twenty years ago. She came looking for answers about the father of her child." His voice softened. "She came looking for you."

Maya's world tilted. "She knew I was a hybrid? Before I was born?"

"She knew. And she knew what it would mean. She came here to beg the Council to let you live." The Headmaster's eyes were ancient, tired, full of things he'd seen and never spoken of. "They refused. They told her that any hybrid born would be eliminated at birth. They told her to end the pregnancy."

Maya's vision blurred. "She didn't."

"No. She ran. Hid. Kept you safe for three years until she couldn't anymore." He paused. "She died protecting you, Maya. Against the Council's hunters."

The room was spinning. The book in her lap was the only thing anchoring her. "You're telling me the Council has been hunting me since before I was born?"

"I'm telling you that the Council is afraid of you. Not because of what you are, but because of what you represent." He leaned forward. "You are proof that their system is flawed. That purity is a lie. That the future doesn't belong to them."

Maya's hands curled into fists. "Why are you telling me this?"

"Because the Hunt has begun. And because I made a promise to your mother, nineteen years ago." His voice cracked, just slightly. "I promised her that if you ever came here, I would protect you. That I would give you the truth

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Price of Power

The three wolves didn't move. They didn't need to. Their presence alone was a cage, their amber eyes pinning Maya in place like prey.

She tightened her grip on the book. The pages hummed against her fingers, warm and alive. "Who sent you?"

The lead wolf—gray-furred, scarred across one eye—stepped closer. His shift was seamless, bone and muscle rearranging in a ripple of shadow until a man stood before her. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Wearing a Council uniform of black and silver.

"The better question," he said, "is who sent you?"

"I asked first."

A flicker of something crossed his face. Surprise? Respect? Hard to tell with someone who'd just been a wolf. "Alpha Marcus sent us. The Council has concerns about your enrollment."

"Concerns. That's a polite word for it."

"We can do this the easy way or the hard way, hybrid."

Maya's wolf surged beneath her skin, claws threatening to break through. Three against one. Bad odds. But she'd faced worse. She'd spent eighteen years hiding what she was. She wasn't about to let them take her without a fight.

The book pulsed. Once. Twice. A third time, harder, like a heartbeat.

The lead wolf's eyes flicked to it. His expression shifted. "What is that?"

"None of your business."

"Give it to me. Now."

"No."

He moved fast. Faster than she expected. His hand shot out, grabbing her wrist, twisting. Pain flared up her arm. The book slipped—

And something *exploded* out of it.

Not fire. Not light. A wave of force that sent the Council wolves flying backward, slamming into trees, the ground, each other. Maya stumbled, the book still in her hands, its pages glowing silver so bright she had to look away.

The lead wolf picked himself up, blood dripping from his nose. His eyes were wide. Afraid.

"That's forbidden text," he hissed. "Where did you get that?"

"Library."

"The library doesn't have books like that. Not anymore. The Council burned them all."

"Guess they missed one."

He took a step back. "You don't know what you've found, hybrid. That book—"

"Is mine now."

A new voice. Deep. Commanding.

Maya turned.

Kael stood at the edge of the trees, his shirt torn, a cut bleeding along his jaw. But his eyes—his eyes were wolf-gold, burning with something she'd never seen in him before. Fury. But controlled. Focused.

He walked past her like she wasn't there. Stopped in front of the lead Council wolf. Didn't speak. Just stared.

The lead wolf's bravado cracked. "Kael. You don't want to do this."

"I don't want to do anything," Kael said, his voice low. "But I will."

"She's in possession of forbidden material. The Council has jurisdiction—"

"The Council has jurisdiction over nothing on this campus. Not anymore. Not since they tried to burn it down last year."

The lead wolf's jaw tightened. "That was an accident."

"Was it?"

Silence stretched. The other two Council wolves had gotten to their feet, but they didn't advance. They were waiting. Watching. Measuring.

Maya could feel the book humming in her hands, like it was feeding on the tension, growing stronger with every heartbeat.

The lead wolf finally stepped back. "This isn't over, Kael. You can't protect her forever."

"I don't intend to protect her. I intend to teach her."

A strange smile crossed the lead wolf's face. "Teach her what? To be a weapon? To be a threat? You know what happens to threats in our world."

"I know what happens to cowards who hide behind Council badges."
