"What?" Maya's voice came out wrong. Too deep. Too layered. Like two voices speaking at once. "What am I not?"

He stared at her. At her silver eyes. At her half-shifted form. At the way the moonlight seemed to bend around her, like she was standing in a different world, a different story.

"The Shadow Wolf," he whispered. "It's true. The prophecy is—"

"What prophecy?"

But he wouldn't answer. His eyes rolled back, and he went limp.

Maya stood over him, breathing hard, her body humming with power she didn't understand. The female wolf was still unconscious. The third was long gone.

She should have felt victorious.

Instead, she felt watched.

She turned.

The book lay open on the ground, its pages glowing faintly. But that wasn't what made her blood freeze.

Behind the book, standing at the edge of the treeline, was a figure she recognized.

Professor Aldric. Her combat instructor. The one who'd trained her, pushed her, believed in her when no one else did.

He was holding a silver blade. And his eyes were fixed on her with an expression she'd never seen on his face before.

Pity.

"Maya," he said quietly. "You need to come with me."

"Professor—"

"The Council felt your shift. They're on their way. If they find you here, like this, with them—" He gestured at the two wolves. "—there's nothing I can do to save you."

"Save me?" Maya's voice cracked. "I was defending myself. They attacked me. They said they were going to kill me."

"I know."

"Then why—"

"Because the Council doesn't care who started the fight." His jaw tightened. "They only care that a hybrid just defeated two elder purebloods in under thirty seconds. You're not a student to them anymore. You're a weapon. And weapons are either controlled or destroyed."

Maya felt the power drain from her, leaving her human again, cold and shaking in the moonlight.

"What do I do?"

Aldric's hand trembled on the blade. For a moment, just a moment, she saw the man beneath the professor. The wolf who'd lost someone, once. Who understood.

"Run," he said. "And don't stop until you find the ones who wrote that book."

He pointed at the tome on the ground.

"They're the only ones who can keep you alive now."

Maya picked up the book. It was warm again, pulsing like a heartbeat. She looked at Aldric, at the unconscious wolves, at the dark trees that stretched toward a sky full of stars that suddenly felt very far away.

"Where do I find them?"

"The book will show you." He stepped back, into the shadows. "I'll hold them off as long as I can. But Maya?"

"Yes?"

"Trust no one. Not even the ones who seem to help you. The Council has eyes everywhere. And they've been waiting for you to reveal yourself for a long, long time."

He disappeared into the dark.

Maya stood alone, the book in her hands, the distant howls of approaching wolves growing louder.

She opened the book.

The page showed her a map. Not of the academy. Not of the territory.

Of somewhere else. Somewhere marked with a symbol she'd never seen before. A wolf's head, split in two. One half silver. One half shadow.

And beneath it, three words:

*Come find us.*

Maya ran.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Hunters' Game

The three wolves circled, their movements synchronized, practiced. Hunters. Not students playing at dominance—real predators who had done this before.

Maya's wolf surged beneath her skin, demanding release. But she held it back. Barely.

"You're making a mistake," she said, her voice steadier than she felt. "I'm a student at this academy. You have no authority—"

"We have all the authority we need." The lead wolf stepped closer. He was massive, gray-furred, one eye clouded white with an old scar. "The Council doesn't need permission to collect strays."

*Collect.* The word hit her like a physical blow. Not *arrest.* Not *detain.* Collect. Like she was an animal to be caged.

"The Council has no jurisdiction over students during the academic year." A new voice cut through the night, sharp and cold. Headmaster Thornwood emerged from the shadows of the east dormitory, his cane tapping against the frozen ground. He was still in his formal robes, as if he'd been expecting company. "Or has that changed without my notification, Marcus?"

The lead wolf—Marcus—snarled, but his ears flattened slightly. "This doesn't concern you, Thornwood. The girl is half-breed. The Council's decree stands."

"The Council's decree," Thornwood repeated, stopping between Maya and the wolves, "was that she be *observed.* Not collected. Not hunted. Observed. There's a difference, unless your reading comprehension has deteriorated along with your coat."

One of the younger wolves growled, stepping forward. Marcus stopped him with a raised paw.

"Clever words, Headmaster. But the Council will hear of this interference."

"I'm counting on it." Thornwood didn't move. "Now get off my grounds before I decide to test which of us still remembers how to fight."

The wolves held their ground for a long, tense moment. Then Marcus's eyes slid to Maya, and he smiled—a predator's smile, full of promise.

"This isn't over, half-breed. The hunt has only just begun."

They melted back into the trees, silent as smoke. Maya didn't relax until the last trace of their presence faded from her senses.

Thornwood turned to her, his expression unreadable. "You have the book."

It wasn't a question.

Maya clutched it tighter. "I found it. In the restricted section. Kael helped me—"

"Kael should have known better." But there was no heat in his voice. He held out his hand. "Give it to me."

"No."

The word hung in the air between them. Thornwood's eyebrows rose.

"No?"

"It showed me things. About what I am. About the Shadow Wolf." Maya's grip on the book tightened. "You knew. You've always known what I am, and you let me walk into this school blind."

"I let you walk into this school alive." Thornwood's voice was quiet, but it carried weight. "Do you know how many people wanted you dead before you arrived? How many still do? The book in your hands is a target on your back. A literal one—it's enchanted. Every time it's opened, it sends a pulse that can be tracked by certain members of the Council."

Maya's blood went cold. "Then why was it in the library?"

"Because I put it there." Thornwood's eyes met hers, ancient and tired. "For the one who would know to look for it."

"Me."

"You."

A beat of silence. The wind picked up, carrying the scent of pine and snow.

"Why?" Maya asked. "Why help me? You're Headmaster. You answer to the Council. If they find out—"

"They'll do what they've always done." Thornwood's hand fell to his side. "But I've been answering to the Council for three hundred years, Maya. I've watched them purge everything they didn't understand. I've watched them destroy promising young wolves because they didn't fit the mold. And I've watched them try to erase the existence of the Shadow Wolves entirely."

"Shadow Wolves. Plural." Maya's heart hammered. "There were others."

"Three before you. All hybrids. All killed before their eighteenth birthday." Thornwood's voice was distant now, haunted. "The Council doesn't want hybrids to exist, Maya. They want the legend to die. But I've been searching for you since the day you were born. And I will not let them take you."

"Why?" she asked again. "What do you care?"

Thornwood was silent for a long moment. When he spoke, his voice was barely a whisper.

"Because the last Shadow Wolf was my daughter."

The words hit Maya like a physical force. She staggered back a step, the book nearly slipping from her fingers.

"Your—"

"Her name was Elara. She was born three months before the Council's purge. I hid her. I thought I'd hidden her well enough." His jaw tightened. "I was wrong."

Maya couldn't speak. The Headmaster—the cold, calculating Headmaster—had lost a daughter to the same war she was now caught in.

"That's why you admitted me," she said, the pieces clicking into place. "That's why you broke the rules. You think I'm some kind of... second chance."

"I think," Thornwood said slowly, "that you are exactly what the prophecy foretold. And I will not lose another."

The book pulsed in Maya's hands. She looked down. The ink had shifted again, forming new words:

*The Headmaster's grief is a chain. Break it, or be bound by it.*

"What the hell does that mean?" She showed it to Thornwood.

He read it, and his face went pale. "The book is showing you warnings. That means it's bonded to you. It responds to your bloodline, your choices, your future." He met her eyes. "But it also means the Council can see what it shows you. Every time you look, they see a reflection."

"Then why did you give it to me?"

"Because you need to know the truth before they find you. And they will find you, Maya. The hunters tonight were just the first. They'll keep coming. The question is whether you'll be ready."

Maya stared at the words on the page. *Break it, or be bound by it.*

She thought about Kael, risking himself to help her. She thought about the three Shadow Wolves who died before their eighteenth birthdays. She thought about Elara, Thornwood's daughter, who never got to grow up.

She thought about herself.

"I want to learn," she said. "I want to know what I am. I want to fight."

Thornwood studied her for a long moment. Then, slowly, he nodded.

"Then meet me in the old training grounds at midnight. Every night. Don't tell anyone. Not even Kael."

"Kael saved my life tonight. He deserves—"

"Kael is a pureblood from a powerful family. If the Council suspects he's helping you, they'll use him against you. They'll use everyone you care about." Thornwood's voice was hard. "You want to protect your friends? Keep them in the dark."

Maya wanted to argue. But she knew he was right.

"Fine. Midnight. Training grounds."

Thornwood turned to leave, then paused. "One more thing. The hunters tonight—they weren't just testing you. They were testing me. They wanted to see how far I'd go to protect you."

"And?"

"And now they know." His eyes were shadowed. "Which means they'll change their strategy. They won't come for you directly anymore. They'll come for someone you love."

He disappeared into the darkness, leaving Maya alone with the book and the weight of his words.

She looked down at the page again. The words had changed once more:

*The first blow has been struck. The second comes for the one who stands beside you.*

*Kael's blood will be the price of your survival.*

Maya's blood turned to ice.

She ran.

The dorms were dark, silent. She didn't bother with the front entrance—she scaled the side of the building, her wolf lending her speed and strength, and dropped onto the third-floor balcony. Kael's room.

The door was unlocked.

She pushed it open.

The room was empty. His bed was made. His books were stacked neatly on the desk. But there was a smell in the air—blood, fresh and metallic.

And on the floor, written in crimson letters:

*You can't save them all, half-breed.*

Maya's hands shook. She grabbed her phone, dialed Kael's number. It went straight to voicemail.

She tried again. Nothing.

A sound behind her. She spun, claws extending, ready to fight.

But it was just a piece of paper, fluttering to the floor from where it had been pinned to the wall.

She picked it up.

The handwriting was elegant, precise, nothing like the crude message on the floor:

*Dear Ms. Chen,*

*Your presence at Silver Fang Academy has been noted with great interest. We have observed your progress, your alliances, your growth. You have exceeded our expectations.*

*However, we must inform you that your continued existence presents an unacceptable variable in the established order. Therefore, we have taken steps to ensure your cooperation.*

*Kael Alistair is currently our guest. He will remain unharmed as long as you comply with our demands. Should you choose to defy us... well. I'm sure you can imagine the consequences.*

*Your first task: Deliver the Shadow Book to the old clock tower at midnight tomorrow. Come alone. Tell no one.*

*Fail, and Kael dies.*

*Succeed, and you may yet survive this.*

*—The Council of Elders*

Maya read the letter three times. Each word felt like a blade.

She thought of Kael's fierce eyes, his steady hand, the way he'd pushed her into that passage without hesitation.

She thought of Thornwood's warning: *They'll come for someone you love.*

She thought of the book's prophecy: *Kael's blood will be the price of your survival.*

Her wolf howled inside her, furious and terrified.

But Maya Chen had survived eighteen years by being smarter than the wolves who hunted her.

She folded the letter, tucked it into her pocket, and looked at the book.

"Alright," she whispered to the empty room. "You want a game? Let's play."

The book pulsed once, warm against her chest.

And somewhere in the darkness, she heard a howl—long, low, and hungry.

title: The Blood That Binds
content:
The wolves didn't move. They didn't need to. Their presence alone was a cage, their amber eyes pinning Maya in place like prey.

Three of them. All pureblood. All older than any student she'd faced. Their fur was dark—one charcoal, one rust-red, one black as oil—and their muscles rippled beneath coats that seemed to absorb the moonlight.

Maya's fingers dug into the book's leather cover. Warmth pulsed from it, a steady heartbeat against her palms.

The charcoal wolf took a step forward. His muzzle curled, revealing teeth that gleamed wet and white. "The book. Hand it over."

Her voice came out steadier than she felt. "I don't know what book you're talking about."

The rust-red wolf laughed, a low, ugly sound. "Clever little half-breed. Thinking she can lie to the Council's hounds."

Council's hounds. Maya's stomach dropped. These weren't random enforcers. These were the Council's personal trackers. The ones sent when someone needed to disappear.

The black wolf hadn't spoken. He stood slightly behind the others, watching her with an intensity that made her skin crawl. His eyes weren't amber like the others. They were pale gold, almost white.

Human eyes in a wolf's face.

Maya shifted her weight, preparing to run. The dorms were fifty meters away. If she could make it to the door, trigger the wards—

"Don't." The black wolf's voice was soft, almost gentle. "You won't make it three steps."

She froze.

He tilted his head, studying her. "You can feel it, can't you? The pull of the book. The way it calls to your blood." He took a step forward, and the other two parted for him. "That book was hidden for a reason. Sealed with blood magic keyed to a specific lineage. Only one person in three centuries could have opened it."

Maya's throat tightened. "I didn't open it. It was already—"

"You touched it." His pale eyes fixed on hers. "That's all it needed."

The book pulsed again, stronger this time. Maya looked down. The ink on the cover was moving, swirling like smoke trapped under glass, forming symbols she didn't recognize but somehow understood.

*Blood calls. Blood answers. Blood binds.*

The black wolf was close now. Close enough that she could smell him—old forests and cold stone and something metallic, like iron left in rain.

"My name is Theron." He said it like she should know it. "And you, Maya Chen, have awakened something that was meant to stay asleep."

"I didn't ask for this." Her voice cracked. She hated it. "I didn't ask for any of this. I just wanted to survive."

"Survival is a luxury hybrids don't get." Theron's voice wasn't cruel. It was worse. It was matter-of-fact. "The Council has been watching you since the moment you set foot on this campus. Your test scores. Your fights. Your alliance with Kael Blackthorn." His eyes narrowed. "Your resistance to the Alpha's command."

Maya's blood went cold. "How do you know about that?"

"We know everything." Theron extended a paw, claws glinting. "The book, Maya. Now. Or I take it by force, and your body pays the price for your stubbornness."

Behind her, she heard the library door slam open. Footsteps. Voices.

Kael. He'd bought her time, but not enough.

The rust-red wolf growled. "Tracker's coming. We need to move."

Theron didn't flinch. "Maya. Last chance."

She looked at the book. At her own face staring back from the ink, silver-eyed and half-shadow. At the words that promised a hunt.

She thought about running. About fighting. About every door that had slammed in her face since she arrived at Silver Fang, every whispered insult, every challenge she'd had to claw her way through.

And she thought about what it meant to be the first of her kind in three centuries. The last, if the Council had its way.

She lifted her chin.

"No."

Theron's eyes flickered. Surprise? Respect? She couldn't tell.

"You don't understand what you're refusing," he said quietly. "The Council doesn't offer second chances."

"Then I won't need one."

The black wolf studied her for a long moment. Then he did something she didn't expect.

He shifted.

It was smooth, seamless—a ripple of fur and bone that resolved into a man in his late twenties, lean and sharp-featured, with pale gold eyes and dark hair streaked with gray at the temples. He wore a simple black shirt and dark pants, no shoes, no weapons.

He looked almost human.

Almost.

"You have her stubbornness," he said. "Your mother, I mean."

Maya's breath caught. "You knew my mother?"

"I knew of her." Theron's voice was carefully neutral. "Enough to know she died protecting something. I never knew what, until now." He glanced at the book in Maya's hands. "You carry her legacy in your blood. And her enemies in your shadow."

The footsteps from the library were getting louder. Voices—Kael's, and someone else's, sharper, angrier.

Theron's jaw tightened. "The Council's hounds aren't the only ones looking for you. There are older things in these woods. Things that serve no master." He met her eyes. "If you want to survive, you'll need more than guts and a haunted book. You'll need allies who don't answer to the Council."

"Like you?"

A ghost of a smile touched his lips. "I answer to no one. But I've been waiting three hundred years for someone to open that book. I'm not about to let the Council snuff out the only chance we have."

"We?" Maya's mind raced. "We who?"

The shout came from the library door. "Maya!"

Kael. He was running toward her, his face a mask of fury and fear. Behind him, the library door hung open, and shadows moved inside.

Theron stepped back, his body already beginning to shift. "Keep the book close. Trust no one. Not even the ones who claim to love you." His eyes flicked to Kael. "Especially not them."

"Wait—"

But he was already gone, a streak of black fur disappearing into the trees. The other two wolves followed, silent as smoke.

Kael reached her, breathless, grabbing her shoulders. "Are you okay? I heard voices—who was here?"

Maya stared at the treeline. The book was warm against her chest, pulsing like a second heartbeat.

"Council trackers," she said. "And someone else. Someone who knew my mother."

Kael's face went pale. "That's not possible. Your mother's records were sealed. Erased."

"Apparently not completely." She looked down at the book. The cover had changed again. Now it showed a tree, ancient and massive, its roots twisting into darkness, its branches reaching toward a sky full of stars. A single word was carved into the trunk:

*ASHWOOD.*

"What is this?" she whispered.

Kael's grip on her shoulders tightened. "Maya. Where did you get that book?"

"You were there. In the library."

"No." His voice was strange, strained. "I mean, where did it come from? Before the library?"

She frowned. "I don't know. It was just... there. On the table."

Kael's face told her everything. That wasn't possible. The library's collection was catalogued, tracked, warded. Books didn't just appear.

But this one had.

And it had been waiting for her.

A howl split the night. Not close, but not far. Answering howls rose from the east, the west, the south.

The hunt was spreading.

Kael grabbed her hand. "We need to move. Now."

"Where? Everywhere we go, they find us."

He hesitated. Then his jaw set. "There's a place. Off-campus. A sanctuary for wolves who don't fit the Council's mold."

"Rogues?"

"Survivors." He met her eyes. "My mother ran there when she was pregnant with me. Before my father dragged her back to Silver Fang."

Maya stared at him. Kael never talked about his family. Never.

"How do I know this isn't a trap?"

"You don't." His voice was raw. "But you don't have time to find a better option."

The howls were getting closer. Closer than they should be.

Maya clutched the book. *Trust no one. Not even the ones who claim to love you.*

But Kael's hand was warm in hers, and his eyes held something she hadn't seen before.

Fear. Not for himself. For her.

"One wrong move," she said, "and I'm gone."

"I know."

"Show me."

They ran.

The trees swallowed them, darkness closing in like a living thing. Maya followed Kael's lead, her hybrid senses straining to track the howls, the footsteps, the beating of her own heart.

The book pulsed against her chest.

*Ashwood.*

*Ashwood.*

*Ashwood.*

She didn't know what it meant. But she knew, with a certainty that burned like silver fire in her blood, that it was the only answer she had left.

They broke through the treeline into a clearing. An old cabin stood in the center, roof half-collapsed, windows dark.

Kael pulled her toward it.

"Wait." Maya stopped. "Something's wrong."

The howls had stopped.

All of them.

The silence was absolute, pressing, wrong.

Kael turned, his eyes scanning the treeline. "What is it?"

"I don't know." Maya's skin prickled. "It's too quiet."

A twig snapped behind them.

They spun.

A figure stood at the edge of the clearing. Tall. Hooded. No face visible, only darkness beneath the cowl.

But Maya felt it. A presence like cold iron, like ancient stone, like something that had been waiting in the dark for a very long time.

The figure raised a hand. Pointed at the book.

A voice came from nowhere and everywhere, old and dry as dust:

"The Blood of Ashwood returns. The Council's leash tightens. But the chains of fate are not yet forged."

Maya's blood ran cold. "Who are you?"

"I am what the Council fears. What the purebloods deny. What you will become, if you survive."

The figure stepped forward. Moonlight caught the edge of a face—pale, scarred, with eyes that glowed silver.

The same silver as Maya's.

"The hunt has begun, daughter of Ashwood. But you are not the prey."

She felt the book pulse, felt something answer inside her chest, felt the silver in her eyes flare to life.

"You are the hunter."

The cabin door creaked open behind her. Warm light spilled out.

Inside, shadows moved.

And Maya had to choose.

Step into the unknown. Or face the wolves closing in from every side.

She looked at Kael.

He looked back, his hand still holding hers.

"Together?" she asked.

"Together."

They ran inside.

The door slammed shut behind them.

And outside, the howling began again.

But this time, it wasn't hunting.

It was screaming.

title: The Council's Game
content:
The wolves circled, their amber eyes never leaving Maya's face. She recognized the leader—Deputy Director Aldric, the man who'd looked at her on her first day like she was a stain on his floor.

"Put the book down," Aldric said. "Slowly."

Maya's fingers tightened on the spine. The leather was warm now, almost hot, pulsing against her palm like a second heartbeat. "You knew about this."

"Everyone knows about the Shadow Wolf prophecy. What we didn't know"—his eyes dropped to the book—"was that you were the one."

"I didn't know either."

"And yet here you are. In a restricted section. With a book that's been locked away for fifty years." He took a step closer. Behind him, the other two wolves shifted, their hackles rising. "That's not coincidence, Maya. That's destiny. And destiny gets people killed."

The words hit harder than any threat. She'd spent her whole life hiding, surviving, proving she belonged. And now this man was telling her that her very existence was a death sentence.

"I'm not going anywhere with you."

Aldric's lips curled. "You don't have a choice."

"You're wrong."

The voice came from behind her. Maya spun, heart slamming against her ribs, and found Kael stepping out of the passage. His shirt was torn. A thin line of blood ran down his cheek. But his eyes were pure gold, his wolf barely contained.

"Kael," Aldric said, the name dripping with contempt. "I should have known you'd be involved."

"He's not involved." Maya stepped between them. "I came alone. He found me."

"Loyal. Stupid, but loyal." Aldric gestured, and the two wolves flanked him. "Step aside, Maya. This doesn't have to be difficult."

The book in her hands blazed. She gasped, nearly dropping it, but the heat didn't burn—it *filled* her, rushing up her arms and into her chest like molten silver. Her vision sharpened. The moonlight became painfully bright. She could smell Aldric's fear beneath his anger, could hear his heartbeat quicken.

"Give me the book," he snarled.

"No."

"You don't understand what you're holding."

"Then explain it."

Aldric's composure cracked. For a split second, she saw something raw in his face—not anger, but terror. "That book is a record. A history of every hybrid who ever lived, every one who tried to bridge the gap between our world and theirs. And every single one of them died before their twentieth birthday. The Council didn't kill them. The prophecy did. It marked them, and the world made sure they never—"

"Enough."

The new voice came from the trees. A woman stepped into the clearing, tall and silver-haired, her wolf form shimmering beneath her skin like water trapped in glass. Maya had never seen her before, but she knew her instantly.

The Alpha of the Silver Fang territory. Director Astrid Voss.

Aldric dropped to one knee. The other wolves followed, their aggression evaporating into submission. Only Maya and Kael remained standing.

"Director," Aldric said, his voice flat. "I was handling—"

"You were about to traumatize a student and compromise a century of protection." Astrid's silver eyes fixed on Maya. "You have questions."

"I have a lot of questions."

"Then walk with me." Astrid turned without waiting, disappearing into the trees.

Maya looked at Kael. He nodded once, the movement tight, controlled. *Trust her.*

She didn't. But she had no other options.

The forest swallowed them. Astrid moved through the darkness like she was part of it, her silver hair catching stray beams of moonlight. Maya followed, the book still burning against her chest, her mind racing through a dozen impossible scenarios.

They reached a clearing. In the center stood a stone table, covered in carvings that matched the symbols in the book. Astrid stopped beside it and turned.

"Show me the page."

Maya opened the book. The ink had shifted again. Now the image showed her and Astrid, standing exactly where they stood now, the stone table between them.

"Every hybrid who carried that book saw their future written in its pages. Most of them saw their death." Astrid's voice was calm, clinical. "Do you see yours?"

Maya looked. The image showed nothing beyond this moment. "No."

"Then you're different." Astrid touched the table, and the carvings began to glow. "The Shadow Wolf prophecy has three parts. The first is the birth—a hybrid strong enough to survive. That's you. The second is the awakening—the moment the hybrid's true power emerges. That's tonight."

"What's the third?"

Astrid's eyes met hers. "The choice. The hybrid must decide whether to unite the packs or destroy them. There is no middle ground. No running. No hiding."

Maya's blood went cold. "Unite the packs? I'm a first-year student. I can't even shift on command without—"

"You can now."

The words hit like a blow. Maya looked at her hands. The silver glow was still there, pulsing beneath her skin. She *felt* different—stronger, sharper, more alive than she'd ever been.

"The book awakened your wolf fully," Astrid continued. "The version of you that existed before tonight is gone. You'll never be that girl again."

"What if I don't want this?"

"You don't have a choice." Astrid's voice softened, just slightly. "None of us do. The prophecy has been waiting for you for three hundred years. The Council has been hunting for you for three hundred years. And now that you're here, the game changes."

"What game?"

Astrid smiled. It was not a warm expression. "The Council thinks they control the packs. They think they decide who lives and who dies, who is pure and who is abomination. But they forgot something."

"What?"

"The prophecy isn't a warning. It's a weapon. And you, Maya Chen, are the hand that wields it."

The ground shook. A deep, resonant rumble that came from beneath the stone table. The carvings flared so bright Maya had to look away. When she looked back, the table had split down the middle, revealing a staircase that spiraled into darkness.

"The third part isn't a choice," Astrid said. "It's a door. And you just opened it."

Maya stared into the darkness. She could feel something down there, ancient and waiting, pulsing in rhythm with the book in her hands.

"What's at the bottom?"

"The truth. About the Council, about the packs, about what you really are." Astrid stepped aside. "But if you go down there, you'll never be able to pretend again. You'll know everything. And you'll have to decide what to do with it."

Kael appeared at Maya's side. His hand found hers, squeezing once, tight. "I'm coming with you."

"You don't have to—"

"I know."

Maya looked at the staircase. At the book. At the silver glow still pulsing beneath her skin. She thought about every moment she'd spent hiding, every insult she'd swallowed, every time she'd made herself smaller to fit into a world that didn't want her.

No more.

She took the first step.

The darkness swallowed her whole. Behind her, she heard Astrid's voice, cold and final.

"The Council will know by sunrise. You have until then to find what you need."

The stone table ground shut above them, sealing them in.

Maya and Kael descended into the dark.

The stairs went deeper than any basement should have gone. The air grew thick, heavy with the scent of old magic and older blood. The walls changed from stone to something else—black, smooth, reflective, like obsidian. Maya's reflection flickered in the surface, but it wasn't her reflection. It showed her wolf form, silver-eyed and massive, standing in a ruin that looked like it had been built for giants.

"What is this place?" Kael's voice echoed.

"A tomb. A temple. A prison." Maya didn't know how she knew. The words came from the book, from the warmth in her chest, from the blood singing in her veins. "This is where they buried the first hybrid."

"The first?"

"Before the Council. Before the packs. Before any of this." She stopped. The stairs opened into a vast chamber, bigger than the library above, bigger than the entire academy. In the center stood a single pillar of light, and inside that light, suspended in midair, was a wolf.

Not alive. A skeleton, ancient and massive, its bones glowing with the same silver fire that burned in Maya's chest.

The book fell open in her hands. The pages turned on their own, faster and faster, until they stopped on a single image.

A wolf, whole and living, standing before a crowd of bowed figures. The words beneath it read: *The First Shadow. She who refused to choose. She who was sealed away until her successor came to set her free.*

Maya's breath caught.

"Set her free?"

The skeleton moved.

Bone ground against bone as the great skull turned, empty eye sockets fixing on Maya with an awareness that should have been impossible. A voice filled the chamber, ancient and vast, echoing from everywhere and nowhere at once.

*"You came."*

Kael grabbed Maya's arm, pulling her back. "We need to leave. Now."

But Maya couldn't move. The silver fire in her chest was screaming, reaching toward the skeleton, toward the voice, toward something that felt like home and death all at once.

*"I have waited so long."* The voice was almost gentle now. *"My daughter. My heir. My weapon."*

"I'm not a weapon."

*"You are. You always were. The prophecy doesn't make you one. It only tells the world what you already are."* The skeleton's jaw moved, the bone scraping in what might have been a smile. *"The question is: will you wield yourself? Or will you let them wield you?"*

Maya looked at Kael. At the book. At the silver fire that was no longer just beneath her skin—it was *her*, every cell, every breath, every thought.

"The third choice," she said slowly. "The one the prophecy doesn't name."

The skeleton's glow flickered.

*"There is no—"*

"There is." Maya stepped forward, the words coming from somewhere deeper than knowledge, somewhere that had always known. "I don't have to unite the packs. I don't have to destroy them. I can remake them."

Silence.

Then the skeleton laughed, a sound like crumbling stone.

*"You really are my daughter."*

The fire in Maya's chest exploded outward. The chamber blazed with silver light, and the last thing she saw before everything went white was Kael's face, his eyes wide with shock and something that looked like hope.

When the light faded, the skeleton was gone.

And Maya stood alone in the center of the chamber, her wolf form fully manifested for the first time in her life, silver eyes burning like twin suns.

Above her, buried deep in the academy's foundation, an alarm began to sound.

The Council had arrived.

title: The Hunt Begins
content:
The book slipped from Maya's fingers, hitting the grass with a soft thud. Three wolves. Not students—these were full-grown, their muscles coiled under dark fur, their jaws lined with teeth that gleamed like daggers in the moonlight.

"You're making a mistake," Maya said, her voice steadier than she felt.

The lead wolf snorted. A sound almost like laughter. "The only mistake was letting you through the gates, hybrid."

He shifted. The transformation was seamless, brutal—bone and muscle rearranging in seconds. Where the wolf had stood, a man now rose. Tall. Scarred. His eyes never left hers.

"I'm Marcus," he said. "Council Enforcer. You're going to come with us quietly, or we're going to carry you. Your choice."

Maya's mind raced. Kael was still in the library. The dorms were a hundred yards away. The book lay at her feet, its pages still glowing faintly.

"No."

Marcus's eyes narrowed. "No?"

"I said no." She bent down, slow and deliberate, and picked up the book. "You want me? You're going to have to explain to the Headmaster why you dragged a student out of her school in the middle of the night."

"The Headmaster answers to the Council."

"Then I guess we're at an impasse."

The other two wolves shifted. A woman with cropped silver hair and a man built like a wall of muscle. They spread out, flanking her.

Maya's wolf stirred. She felt it pacing beneath her skin, restless and hungry. *Not yet,* she told it. *Not until I have no choice.*

"Last chance," Marcus said.

A howl split the night. Not from the wolves in front of her—from the direction of the library. Long and sharp and unmistakably Kael.

Marcus's head snapped toward the sound. For a split second, his attention wavered.

Maya ran.

She didn't think. She just moved, her body remembering years of hiding and fleeing and surviving. She dove into the tree line, branches whipping at her face, the book pressed against her chest.

"After her!"

Footfalls behind her. Heavy. Fast. Wolves running on four legs, crashing through the undergrowth.

The trees blurred past. Maya's lungs burned. She didn't know where she was going—only that she couldn't stop. Couldn't let them catch her.

A root caught her foot. She stumbled, hit the ground hard, the book skidding out of her hands.

She scrambled for it—

A wolf landed on top of her.

The woman. Silver hair. Amber eyes. Her weight pinned Maya to the earth, her jaws inches from Maya's throat.

"Got you."

Maya's wolf erupted.

She didn't shift—not fully. But something surged through her, hot and wild and silver-bright. Her vision sharpened. Her muscles screamed with power she'd never felt before.

She threw the woman off.

The wolf yelped as she crashed into a tree. Maya was on her feet before the woman could recover, the book back in her hands, her heart pounding so hard she thought it might burst.

Marcus emerged from the shadows. His lips curled. "Interesting."

"Stay back."

"Or what? You'll do that again?" He took a step forward. "I've been hunting rogues for twenty years, girl. I've seen tricks. That little burst of power cost you. I can smell it—you're running on empty now."

He was right. Maya felt it draining out of her, the silver light fading. Her legs trembled.

The other wolf—the wall of muscle—circled around behind her.

Trapped.

"Give me the book," Marcus said. "And come quietly. I won't ask again."

Maya's grip tightened on the cover. *The Shadow Wolf. The last of her kind.* The words burned in her memory.

"No."

Marcus sighed. "So be it."

He moved—

And something slammed into him from the side.

Kael.

He hit Marcus like a missile, both of them rolling across the forest floor. Kael's wolf was massive, darker than shadow, his eyes blazing with fury. He snapped at Marcus's throat.

"You little—" Marcus shifted mid-struggle, meeting Kael's attack with his own jaws.

The two wolves tore at each other. Fur and blood flew.

"The passage!" Kael growled between bites. "Go!"

Maya didn't hesitate. She turned and ran, crashing through the trees, the book a weight and a promise in her hands.

Behind her, the sounds of battle grew distant.

She burst out of the tree line and nearly collided with a solid wall of stone. The old chapel. Abandoned since before she'd arrived, its doors boarded up, its windows dark.

She didn't think. She found a loose board, pried it free, and slipped inside.

The air was thick with dust and silence. Moonlight filtered through cracked stained glass, casting fractured colors across the pews.

Maya collapsed against the door, gasping. Her hands shook. Her whole body shook.

She looked down at the book.

The page had changed again.

Gone was the drawing of her, the warning words. Instead, a map. A labyrinth of tunnels beneath the school, marked with symbols she didn't recognize. And at the center, a single word:

*Sanctuary.*

Footsteps outside.

Maya's breath caught. She pressed herself against the wall, listening.

The footsteps stopped at the chapel door. A pause. Then a low voice:

"She's in there."

"Break it down."

Maya looked at the map. There. Behind the altar—a trapdoor she hadn't noticed before.

She moved.

Silent. Fast. She slid behind the altar, found the iron ring, and pulled. The trapdoor opened onto darkness and stone steps.

She descended into the dark, pulling the door closed above her.

The darkness was absolute. The air smelled of earth and old stone. She felt her way down, step by step, her free hand brushing against walls slick with moisture.

The steps ended. A corridor stretched ahead, narrow and low.

Maya walked.

Minutes passed. Or hours. She couldn't tell. The silence was so complete she could hear her own heartbeat.

Then—light.

Faint at first. A glow ahead, around a bend.

She followed it.

The corridor opened into a chamber. Round. Vaulted. Lit by candles that burned with silver flames.

And in the center, on a pedestal of black stone, a second book.

This one was bound in leather so dark it seemed to drink the light. No title. No markings.

Maya approached slowly, her own book still clutched in her hands.

The second book opened on its own.

Pages flipped. Stopped.

Words formed in letters that glowed like embers:

*You found me. I knew you would.*

Maya's blood ran cold. "Who are you?"

The letters shifted, rearranged:

*I am what you will become. I am the shadow between worlds. I am the wolf who was never meant to exist.*

*I am you, Maya. Three years from now.*

*And I am writing this to warn you.*

The candles flickered. The silver flames dimmed.

*The Council will not stop hunting you.*

*Neither will the purebloods.*

*But the real enemy is not who you think.*

*Trust no one.*

*Not even Kael.*

The words faded. New ones appeared:

*He has already made his choice. And it was not you.*

*Turn back now, or learn the truth too late.*

Maya stared at the page, her heart cracking open.

"No," she whispered. "You're lying."

The book didn't answer.

But the candles went out.

And somewhere in the darkness, something breathed.

title: The Blood in the Stone
content:
Maya didn't run.

She'd spent eighteen years running—from bullies, from questions, from the truth of what she was. Every time she'd run, she'd lost something. A piece of herself. A chance to fight back.

Not this time.

She set the book on the ground, slow and deliberate. Her hands came up, palms open. "You're Council," she said. Not a question.

The lead wolf shifted. His body rippled, bones cracking, fur receding, until a man stood in his place. Mid-forties. Gray streaking his temples. Eyes the color of old gold. He wore a dark suit that looked expensive and completely wrong for the woods behind the dorms.

"Clever girl." He didn't smile. "I'm Aldric Vance. You've heard of me?"

She had. Every student at Silver Fang knew the name. Head of the Council's Enforcement Division. The wolf who'd personally put down three rogue packs in the last decade. The one they whispered about in the dorms at night.

"Your reputation precedes you," Maya said, keeping her voice flat. "Should I be flattered or terrified?"

"Both." Aldric took a step forward. The two wolves behind him stayed in form, their growls low and constant. "The book you're holding is older than this academy. Older than the Council itself. It was locked in that library for a reason."

"And yet it called to me."

"Which is exactly the problem." His eyes flicked to the book, then back to her face. "Do you know what happens when an old spell awakens to new blood, Miss Chen?"

She didn't. But she wasn't about to admit that. "Why don't you tell me?"

"Warnings get sent. Ripples spread. Every ancient wolf within five hundred miles felt that pulse. Including the ones we've been hunting for years." He paused, letting the words sink in. "You've put a target on this academy. On every student here. Do you understand that?"

Maya's stomach dropped. But she didn't let it show on her face. "That's a convenient story. Blame the hybrid for your failure to control your own borders."

Aldric's jaw tightened. The wolves behind him snarled.

"You have no idea what you've awakened."

"Then educate me." She stepped forward, not back. "Instead of sending goons to drag me into the woods, try telling me the truth for once. What am I? Why does that book exist? And why does the Council want me dead?"

"Dead?" Aldric's eyebrows rose. "Who said anything about dead?"

"The book. It said the hunt begins."

"The hunt for the truth, Miss Chen. Not for your life." He took another step, closing the distance between them. "If the Council wanted you dead, we wouldn't have sent me. We'd have sent assassins. There's a difference."

"Comforting."

"It's not meant to be." He stopped three feet from her. Close enough that she could smell him—old books, gunpowder, wolf musk. "I'm here to offer you a choice. Come with me willingly. Answer our questions. Prove you're not a threat. Or stay here and wait for the wolves who don't share my restraint to find you first."

Maya studied him. Looking for the lie. Looking for the trap.

She found neither.

"If I come with you, what happens to Kael?"

Aldric's expression flickered—surprise, maybe respect. "The Blackwood boy? Nothing. He was following orders from his Alpha. We have no quarrel with him."

"He protected me."

"Then he's smarter than most purebloods." Aldric almost smiled. "Consider that noted."

Maya made her decision.

"Fine. I'll come with you. But I'm bringing the book."

"That's not—"

"Non-negotiable." She picked it up, holding it against her chest. "If you want me to trust you, start by trusting me."

Aldric stared at her for a long moment. Then he nodded, once.

"Deal."

The two wolves flanked them as they walked. Maya kept her head high, her breathing steady. She didn't know where they were taking her. She didn't know if she'd come back. But for the first time in her life, she was walking toward answers instead of away from them.

That had to count for something.

The Council's safe house was underground.

Maya had expected something grand—marble halls, ancient tapestries, the trappings of old power. Instead, she got concrete walls, fluorescent lights, and the sterile smell of a place that wasn't meant to be lived in.

Aldric led her to a room with a metal table and two chairs. No windows. One door. The wolves stayed outside.

"Sit."

She sat. He sat across from her. The book stayed in her lap, warm against her thighs.

"Ask your questions," she said. "I'll answer. But I want the same from you."

"That's fair." He leaned back, studying her. "Start from the beginning. What do you know about your parents?"

Maya's throat tightened. She'd expected this question. Dreaded it. "My mother was human. My father—" She stopped. "I never met him. She died when I was twelve. She never told me his name."

"And yet you knew you were a wolf."

"Barely. I shifted for the first time when I was eight. She made me promise never to tell anyone. Never to shift again." Maya's hands clenched on the book. "I kept that promise for ten years. Until the academy found me."

"The academy found you because the Council flagged your bloodwork at your mother's death."

Maya went cold. "What?"

"Standard procedure. Every wolf death triggers a bloodline scan. Yours came back anomalous." Aldric's voice softened, just slightly. "We've been watching you since you were twelve years old, Maya. Every school you attended. Every friend you made. Every time you almost shifted."

"You—" Her voice cracked. "You've been stalking me my whole life?"

"Protecting you. From a distance. The Council knew what you were. Or rather, what you might become." He paused. "The Shadow Wolf isn't just a legend. It's a prophecy. Every three hundred years, a wolf is born who can bridge the divide between pureblood and human. The last one started a war that nearly destroyed us all."

"And you think I'm that wolf."

"We think you might be. That book confirmed it."

Maya looked down at the text. The ink had stilled, but the silver-eyed wolf still seemed to watch her. "What happened to the last Shadow Wolf?"

Aldric's face went hard. "The Council killed him. After he killed three hundred of their best enforcers. They didn't want to. They had no choice."

"He was evil?"

"He was alone." Aldric leaned forward, his voice dropping. "The Shadow Wolf isn't born evil, Maya. He's made evil. By fear. By persecution. By a world that sees him as a weapon instead of a person. The Council doesn't want to repeat that mistake."

"Then why—"

"Because not everyone on the Council agrees with me." He held her gaze. "There are factions who believe the only good hybrid is a dead one. They've been waiting for you to show yourself. And now you have."

Maya's blood ran cold. "The book said the hunt begins."

"Yes." Aldric's voice was grim. "But it didn't say which side would be hunting you."

Before she could respond, the door burst open.

A woman stood in the frame. Tall. Silver hair pulled tight. Eyes like frozen mercury. She wore Council robes, dark and severe, and her smile didn't reach her eyes.

"Aldric. You brought her here without consulting me."

"Elena." Aldric's voice was ice. "I didn't realize I needed your permission."

"Every hybrid acquisition requires Council approval. You know the protocol."

"Protocol be damned. She would have been dead by morning if I'd waited."

Elena's gaze slid to Maya. Lingered. "So this is the Shadow Wolf. Smaller than I imagined."

"I make up for it in personality," Maya said.

Elena's smile thinned. "Charming. And reckless. The book recognized her?"

"It did."

"Then the ritual is already in motion. We have three days before the convergence. If she hasn't claimed her power by then, the window closes forever."

Maya looked between them. "What ritual? What convergence?"

Elena ignored her. "Aldric, a word. Outside. Now."

Aldric stood, his jaw tight. "Stay here. Don't touch anything."

"Where would I go?"

He didn't answer. He followed Elena out, and the door clicked shut behind them.

Maya sat alone in the concrete room, the book warm in her hands. She could hear their voices, muffled through the walls. She caught fragments.

"—too dangerous—"

"—only chance—"

"—three days or she's useless—"

She opened the book again. The pages had changed. Now they showed a map—Silver Fang Academy, but different. Marked with symbols she didn't recognize. A path leading from the library to the old bell tower, then down, deep into the earth.

And at the bottom, a single word, written in ink that looked wet:

*Descend.*

Maya's fingers traced the letters. The ink bled, and the word changed.

*Or die.*

She snapped the book shut.

The door opened. Aldric walked in, alone, his face unreadable.

"Change of plans," he said. "You're not staying here."

"What?"

"There's a faction within the Council that wants you eliminated. Elena leads them. She's given me an ultimatum—hand you over for termination, or be labeled a traitor."

"What did you choose?"

Aldric met her eyes. "I chose to get you out. There's a passage beneath this building. It leads to the old tunnels beneath the academy. You follow the map in that book, and you find what's waiting for you."

"And if I don't?"

"Then the convergence happens. The power goes unclaimed. And the Council's extremists will have their excuse to hunt every hybrid on the continent."

Maya stood, the book clutched to her chest. "Why help me?"

"Because I've seen what happens when fear wins." Aldric's voice was quiet. "I was there three hundred years ago, Maya. I was the one who killed the last Shadow Wolf. I've carried that weight long enough."

She stared at him. "You're that old?"

"I'm that old. And I'm that tired." He stepped aside, revealing a narrow door behind him. "Go. Don't stop. Don't trust anyone but yourself."

Maya moved toward the door. Paused. Looked back.

"Will I see you again?"

Aldric's smile was sad. "If you survive the descent, maybe. If not—" He shrugged. "Then we'll meet in whatever comes after."

She wanted to say something. Anything. But the words wouldn't come.

She stepped through the door.

The tunnel was dark, narrow, and smelled of wet stone. She held the book out, and its pages glowed faintly, illuminating the path ahead.

Behind her, she heard voices raised in anger. A door slamming. Footsteps.

Then silence.

She walked.

The tunnel sloped downward, deeper and deeper, until the air grew thick and heavy. The walls changed from concrete to rough stone, then to something else—something that looked almost organic, like the inside of a living creature.

The book's glow intensified

title: The Pact in Blood
content:
Maya didn't run.

She'd spent eighteen years running—from bullies, from questions, from the truth of what she was. The three wolves in front of her were massive, their pelts dark against the moonlit trees, their eyes holding the cold authority of men who had never been told no.

But the book in her hands was warm. Alive. And it had shown her exactly what she was.

"I'm not going anywhere with you," she said.

Her voice didn't shake. She was proud of that.

The lead wolf stepped forward, shifting as he moved. Bones cracked. Fur receded. In three strides, he was a man—tall, broad-shouldered, with silver threading his dark hair and a scar cutting through his left eyebrow. He wore a dark suit that looked expensive and entirely wrong for the woods behind a school.

"Your mother said the same thing once." His voice was gravel and memory. "It didn't end well for her."

Maya's heart stopped.

"Don't talk about my mother."

"I'll do more than talk, girl. I'll tell you the truth she tried to hide." He took another step. "You think you're the first hybrid? You think you're special? There have been others. There have always been others. And every single one of them—"

"Is dead." A new voice. Behind him.

The scarred man froze.

Luna Sterling stepped out of the shadows like she owned them. She wore a black silk robe over her nightclothes, her silver hair loose around her shoulders, and she looked less like a headmistress and more like a goddess of vengeance.

"Headmaster Chen sent you, didn't he, Marcus?" Her voice was ice. "I told him if he sent his dogs to my school, I'd skin them myself."

Marcus bared his teeth. "This doesn't concern you, Luna."

"Everything that happens in my school concerns me." She walked past him like he was furniture, stopping in front of Maya. Her eyes softened—just barely. "Are you hurt?"

Maya shook her head, unable to form words.

"Good." Luna turned back to Marcus. "You have three seconds to leave before I forget that we were once friends."

"We were never friends."

"No. We weren't." She smiled, and it was terrifying. "Three. Two—"

Marcus shifted back to wolf form in a blur of motion. He snarled at Luna, then at Maya, and then he and his pack melted into the trees.

The silence they left behind was heavier than their presence had been.

Maya's legs gave out.

She hit the ground hard, the book still clutched to her chest, her breath coming in ragged gasps. Luna was beside her in an instant, a hand on her shoulder, warm and steady.

"Breathe, child. You're safe."

"They knew my mother." Maya's voice cracked. "He knew my mother."

"Yes."

"You knew."

Luna was quiet for a long moment. Then she sat down on the grass beside Maya, heedless of her expensive robe. "I knew your mother before you were born. I knew her when she was just a girl with too much fire and not enough sense. I knew her when she fell in love with a man she shouldn't have." She paused. "I knew her when she died."

Maya's vision blurred. "How?"

"Not here. Not like this." Luna's hand tightened on her shoulder. "But I will tell you. Everything. I promise you that."

"Why not now?"

"Because the Council isn't the only thing hunting you tonight." Luna looked toward the library. "Kael bought you time, but not much. The book you're holding—it's bonded to you now. That means the Silent Ones felt it activate. And they'll be here within the hour."

"The Silent Ones?"

"Older than the Council. More dangerous. They've been waiting for someone like you for three hundred years." Luna stood, pulling Maya to her feet. "I need you to trust me. Can you do that?"

Maya looked at the book in her hands. The ink had settled, the image of herself faded, replaced by words she hadn't seen before:

*The pact is sealed. The path is chosen. Trust the one who waits in shadows.*

She thought of Kael. Of the way he'd pushed her into that passage without hesitation. Of the way he'd said *trust me* like he meant it with every cell in his body.

"Kael," she said. "Where is he?"

Luna's expression flickered. "He's buying you more time. He knows the cost."

"What cost?"

"Answer my question first. Do you trust me?"

Maya stared at the headmistress—at the woman who had let her into Silver Fang, who had protected her from the Council's first strike, who had known her mother. She thought about how easy it would be to say no, to run, to keep hiding like she'd done her whole life.

But she was done hiding.

"Yes," she said. "I trust you."

Luna nodded once. "Then follow me. And don't let go of that book for any reason."

---

They moved through the shadows of the school like ghosts.

Luna led her past the dorms, past the training grounds, past a locked gate that opened at her touch. They entered a part of the campus Maya had never seen—a graveyard hidden behind a wall of ivy, the headstones worn smooth by centuries of weather.

In the center stood a mausoleum, its doors carved with wolves in various stages of transformation.

"She's buried here," Luna said softly. "Your mother. I brought her body here because I knew the Council would desecrate her grave if I let them take her."

Maya's hand touched the cold stone. "I never got to say goodbye."

"You can say it now."

The doors groaned open, revealing darkness within. Luna produced a small lantern from somewhere and lit it, the flame casting dancing shadows on the walls.

Inside, the mausoleum was larger than it appeared from outside. Shelves lined the walls, each holding an urn or a small box. In the center, on a raised platform, lay a single stone coffin.

Maya approached it like walking through water.

The name carved into the lid read: *LI MEIYING. BELOVED. FORGOTTEN. NEVER FORGIVEN.*

"Open it," Luna said.

Maya's hands shook as she pushed the lid. It moved easier than it should have, sliding aside to reveal not a body, but a compartment. Inside lay a silver dagger, a vial of dark liquid, and a letter sealed with red wax.

Maya picked up the letter. Her mother's handwriting. She'd know it anywhere.

*My dearest Maya,*

*If you're reading this, I'm gone, and you've found your way to Luna. I'm sorry I couldn't be there to see you grow, to tell you the stories myself. But I knew the Council would come for me eventually, and I couldn't let them take you too.*

*You are not a mistake. You are not an abomination. You are the culmination of everything I fought for, everything I loved. Your father was a good man, Maya. A human man. And he loved you before you were even born.*

*The wolf inside you is not a curse. It's a gift. But it's a gift that others will try to take from you.*

*The dagger is for protection. The vial is for transformation—drink it only when there is no other choice. And the book you're holding? It's your inheritance. It will show you the path I couldn't walk.*

*I love you. I've always loved you. And I'll be watching from wherever I am.*

*Be brave, my little wolf. Be fierce. Be everything they fear you could become.*

*—Mom*

Maya's tears fell on the page, smudging the ink. She clutched the letter to her chest, her body shaking with sobs she'd held back for years.

Luna stood in the doorway, her back turned, giving Maya privacy.

"I'm sorry," Maya whispered. "I'm sorry I wasn't there. I'm sorry I couldn't save you."

She didn't know how long she stayed there, crying over her mother's empty coffin. But eventually, the tears stopped. She wiped her face, tucked the letter into her pocket, and picked up the dagger and the vial.

The book pulsed warm against her chest.

When she stepped out of the mausoleum, the moon had shifted, the shadows grown longer. Luna was speaking to someone in the distance—a figure that resolved into Kael as Maya approached.

He was leaning against a tree, his arm wrapped in a bloody bandage, his face pale.

"Kael." Maya ran to him. "What happened?"

"Marcus happened." He tried to smile, but it turned into a wince. "Don't worry. I've had worse."

"From the Council?"

"No." His eyes met hers. "From my own pack."

Before she could ask what that meant, Luna held up a hand. "They're here."

The air changed.

It grew cold, heavy, pressing down on them like a physical weight. The shadows in the graveyard deepened, twisting into shapes that weren't natural. And from between the trees, figures emerged—not wolves, not humans, but something in between.

They moved like smoke given form, their eyes hollow pits of darkness, their mouths lipless slits.

The Silent Ones.

The one in front stopped ten feet away. When it spoke, its voice was the sound of wind through dead leaves.

"The hybrid carries the Blood Book. She must be brought to the elders."

"She's not going anywhere with you," Luna said.

The Silent One tilted its head. "You cannot protect her, Luna Sterling. You could not even protect her mother."

"I can try."

"Then you will die."

The shadows surged forward.

Kael moved first, putting himself between Maya and the advancing darkness. Luna shifted mid-step, her wolf form erupting from her skin in a blur of silver and fury.

But Maya didn't run.

She looked down at the book in her hands. The pages were blank now, save for a single line of text that appeared as she watched:

*The pact is sealed. The blood is spilled. Now comes the choice.*

Maya looked at the silver dagger in her hand. At the vial of dark liquid. At the darkness coming for her.

She made her choice.

She uncorked the vial and drank.

The liquid burned like fire, like ice, like lightning in her veins. Her bones broke and reformed. Her skin ripped and healed. The book in her hands exploded with light, and the last thing Maya saw before the transformation took her was her mother's face, smiling in the darkness.

Then the wolf emerged.

And the Silent Ones learned what it meant to hunt the Shadow Wolf.

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER 9: The Price of Trust

The book burned against Maya's chest.

Not literally—but the warmth had become something else, a pulsing heat that matched her heartbeat, as if the pages had fused to her skin. She stared at the three wolves, their amber eyes unblinking in the moonlight, their bodies half-loomed in the shadows of the trees.

The one who had spoken stepped forward. He was massive, his coat a deep charcoal gray, his shoulders broad even in wolf form. When he shifted, it happened in a single fluid motion—fur to skin, snout to face, paws to hands—and then he stood before her, naked and unashamed, his eyes never leaving hers.

"I said you're coming with us."

Maya's grip tightened on the book. "Who the hell are you?"

"The Council's eyes." His voice was flat, emotionless. "We've been watching you since you stepped onto Academy grounds. Did you think they'd let a hybrid wander unchecked?"

She felt the words like a slap. *Wander unchecked.* Like she was a disease, a threat, a problem to be managed.

"I'm a student," she said, forcing steel into her voice. "I was admitted. Legally."

"Legally." The wolf-man almost smiled. "There's nothing legal about what you are, girl. The Council tolerated your presence because the Headmaster vouched for you. But now..." He nodded at the book. "That doesn't belong to you."

"It was in the library."

"It was sealed. Protected. Meant for eyes that have earned the right to see it." He took another step forward. "You've been touching things you don't understand."

Maya's wolf stirred inside her, restless and angry. *Don't let them take it,* the wolf whispered. *It's ours. It knows us.*

She agreed.

"The book chose me," she said.

The wolf-man's eyes flickered—just a fraction, but she caught it. Doubt. Or maybe surprise.

"The sealed texts don't choose anyone," he said. "They're inert. Dead. They only respond to—"

He stopped. His gaze dropped to the book, then back to Maya's face. Something shifted in his expression. Recognition. Fear.

"Your eyes," he breathed.

Maya didn't know what he meant until she felt it—a burning in her irises, a silver light reflecting off the book's pages. She looked down and saw her own face staring back, eyes glowing pale and bright, the ink shimmering like liquid metal.

*She comes. The Council sees. The hunt begins.*

The wolf-man took a step back.

"Change of plans," he said to the others. "We take her alive. And we take the book."

The other two shifted simultaneously, fur rippling over muscle, bones cracking and reforming. They moved with the fluid grace of predators, spreading out to flank her.

Maya's heart hammered. She was strong—stronger than most wolves her age, she'd proven that. But three Council enforcers? In the dark, alone, with no backup?

She thought of Kael, still in the library, buying her time.

She thought of the Headmaster, who had vouched for her.

She thought of every pureblood who had sneered, every professor who had doubted, every whispered word about the half-breed who didn't belong.

And she made a decision.

She ran.

Not away from the trees—into them. She crashed through the underbrush, the book clutched to her chest, her legs pumping, her breath sharp and desperate. Behind her, she heard the wolves give chase, their paws thudding against the earth, their growls low and hungry.

She didn't know where she was going. The Academy grounds stretched for miles—forests, training fields, the old stone buildings that housed the elite. She weaved between oaks, leaped over fallen logs, her hybrid body pushing past limits that purebloods couldn't match.

But they were fast. Older. Experienced.

A shadow lunged from her left.

Maya twisted, barely avoiding the snap of jaws, and slammed the book into the wolf's snout. It yelped, recoiling, and she kept running.

The trees thinned. Ahead, she saw the old observatory—a crumbling stone tower that had been abandoned for decades. No one went there. No one would think to look.

She sprinted for it, her lungs burning, her legs screaming. The door was rotted, hanging on one hinge. She shouldered through it, stumbled into darkness, and slammed it shut behind her.

The bar was still there—rusted, but functional. She dropped it into place and collapsed against the door, gasping.

Silence.

The wolves had stopped. She could hear them outside, circling, their breath heavy. One of them growled low, and then the flat voice of the leader drifted through the cracked wood.

"You can't stay in there forever, hybrid."

Maya didn't answer. She pressed her back against the door, the book warm against her chest, and waited.

Minutes passed. The wolves didn't leave.

She finally allowed herself to breathe, to look around. The observatory was a wreck—dust and cobwebs, broken glass, the rusted skeleton of an old telescope pointing at a hole in the roof. Moonlight streamed through, silver and cold.

She opened the book.

The pages had stopped shifting. They lay still now, showing her a diagram—a map of the Academy grounds, marked with symbols she didn't recognize. Lines connected buildings, circled certain areas, crossed others out. In the center, where the library stood, was a star.

And beneath the star, written in ink that glowed faintly silver:

*The Shadow Wolf's awakening begins where the old blood sleeps.*

"What does that mean?" she whispered.

The book didn't answer. But the warmth in her chest pulsed, once, like a heartbeat.

She heard a sound.

Not from outside. From inside.

She turned, slow, her muscles tensed. The observatory was empty—she'd checked, she was sure. But now, in the corner where the shadows lay thickest, something moved.

A figure stepped out of the dark.

Not a wolf. A woman. Tall, elegant, with silver hair that fell past her shoulders and eyes the color of mercury. She wore a long coat, black and severe, and she held a staff carved from bone.

Maya's blood turned cold.

"Impressive," the woman said, her voice smooth as silk. "You found the observatory. You found the book. And you're still alive." She smiled, but it didn't reach her eyes. "I underestimated you, Maya Chen."

"Who are you?"

The woman tilted her head. "I'm the reason the Council knows about you. I'm the reason they sent those wolves." She stepped closer, her staff clicking against the stone floor. "And I'm the only one who can help you survive what's coming."

Maya's wolf snarled inside her. *Liar. She's one of them.*

"Why would you help me?"

"Because I'm not with the Council." The woman's smile faded. "I used to be. Before I learned the truth." She stopped a few feet away, her mercury eyes fixed on Maya. "The Council isn't trying to kill hybrids, Maya. They're trying to control them. To use them. And they've been doing it for three centuries."

"Use them for what?"

The woman's gaze dropped to the book. "To open the gate."

Maya felt the words like a physical blow. "What gate?"

"The one between worlds. The one your parents tried to close." The woman's voice softened. "You think you're the first hybrid, Maya? You're not. You're just the first one to survive past infancy."

The book trembled in Maya's hands.

"The Council has been breeding hybrids for centuries," the woman continued. "Experimenting. Trying to create the perfect key. Your parents were part of that experiment. They ran. They hid. And they died to keep you safe."

Maya's vision blurred. Her parents had died in a car accident. That's what she'd been told. That's what she'd believed.

"They didn't die in an accident," the woman said, as if reading her mind. "The Council found them. And when they refused to give you up, the Council killed them."

The air left Maya's lungs.

Outside, the wolves howled.

The woman extended her hand. "Come with me. I can teach you what the book means. I can show you how to use what you are." Her fingers were pale, slender, waiting. "Or you can stay here, and let the Council take you, and become exactly what they've been trying to create for three hundred years."

Maya looked at the book. At the map. At the star above the library.

*The Shadow Wolf's awakening begins where the old blood sleeps.*

She thought of Kael, who had pushed her into the passage. Who had said *trust me.*

She thought of her parents, who had died to keep her hidden.

She looked at the woman's outstretched hand.

And then the observatory door exploded inward.

The three wolves surged through the broken wood, their eyes blazing, their fur bristling with rage. The leader shifted mid-leap, his human form landing in a crouch, a blade already in his hand.

"Step away from the hybrid, Selene."

The woman—Selene—didn't flinch. She turned to face him, her staff glowing with pale light. "You're too late, Marcus. She's already seen the truth."

"The truth?" Marcus laughed, harsh and bitter. "You're the one who's been lying to her. You want the gate opened more than the Council does. You want to unleash what's on the other side."

Selene's eyes flickered. "That's not—"

"Don't." Marcus stepped forward, his blade aimed at her throat. "I know what you are. What you've always been. A traitor to both worlds."

Maya looked between them, her mind reeling. Two adults, each claiming the other was lying. Two paths, each promising danger.

The book burned hotter.

And in the margin of the page, new words appeared, written in the same silver ink:

*Trust neither. They both want the same thing.*

*You.*

The world tilted.

Selene moved first—a flash of silver light, a sweep of her staff that sent Marcus crashing into the wall. The other wolves lunged, and she met them with a snarl, her coat billowing, her eyes blazing.

In the chaos, Maya ran.

She didn't know where she was going. She just knew she couldn't stay. Couldn't trust. Couldn't stop.

She burst out of the observatory, into the cold night, the book clutched to her chest, the sound of battle echoing behind her.

She ran until her legs gave out.

And when she finally collapsed, gasping, in a hollow between two ancient oaks, she opened the book again.

The map had changed.

Now it showed only one path—a line of silver light leading from the observatory to a place she didn't recognize. A place marked with a single word.

*Sanctuary.*

Below it, in smaller letters:

*Come alone. Tell no one. Trust nothing.*

Maya's hand trembled.

She looked up at the moon, silver and cold, and felt the weight of everything she'd learned pressing down on her.

Her parents had been murdered.

The Council wanted to use her.

Selene wanted something else.

And the book—the book that had chosen her—was telling her to run.

She heard footsteps in the distance. Not wolves. Humans. Students, maybe, drawn by the sounds of the fight.

She closed the book, shoved it into her bag, and

title: The Council's Trap
content:
The three wolves didn't move. They stood like statues carved from shadow and muscle, their amber eyes fixed on me with the patience of predators who knew their prey had nowhere to run.

I gripped the book tighter. The warmth had spread from the pages to my palms, seeping into my skin like it was trying to become part of me.

"I'm not going anywhere with you."

The wolf in the center tilted his head. He was massive—easily twice the size of the students I'd seen shift during training. Gray fur streaked with black, a scar running from his snout to his ear.

"You don't have a choice, hybrid." The word dripped like poison. "The Council has summoned you. Refusal is an act of war."

"Then declare war." The words came out before I could stop them. My heart hammered, but something else burned beneath the fear. Something hot and defiant.

The wolf on the left laughed. It was a horrible sound, guttural and wrong coming from a wolf's throat.

"She has spirit," he said. "The Council didn't mention that."

"They never do." The scarred wolf took a step forward. "Last warning, girl. Come peacefully, or we drag you. Your choice doesn't change the outcome."

I calculated my options. Run. Scream. Shift. Fight.

I'd never fought a wolf before. Not like this. Not for my life.

The book pulsed in my hands. I looked down.

The ink had shifted again. The drawing of me now showed my hand raised, fingers spread. And around the edges of the page, symbols I didn't recognize, glowing faintly silver.

It wasn't just a history book.

It was a weapon.

I didn't have time to figure out how to use it. The scarred wolf lunged.

I dove sideways, rolling through the dirt, the book pressed against my chest. His jaws snapped where I'd been standing. I scrambled to my feet, backing toward the tree line.

"Clever," he said, circling. "But speed won't save you."

The other two wolves fanned out, cutting off my escape routes. They moved like they'd done this a hundred times. Professional hunters. Council enforcers.

I was prey.

No.

I stopped backing up. I planted my feet.

I wasn't prey. I was the thing they feared. The hybrid. The Shadow Wolf. The first of my kind in three centuries.

"Kael!" I screamed his name, putting every ounce of lung power into it. "KAEL!"

The wolves paused. Exchanged glances.

"Kael Silverwood isn't coming," the scarred one said. "He's being detained as we speak. Questioned about his association with you."

My stomach dropped.

"They'll let him go if you cooperate," the left wolf added. "If you resist, he becomes an accomplice. The Council has long memories. They'll make an example of him."

I wanted to believe they were lying. But the look on their faces told me otherwise.

This was a trap. A clean, simple trap. Come with us, or your friend pays the price.

I'd known Kael for barely a day. He'd shown me kindness when everyone else showed contempt. He'd pushed me into a secret passage and told me to trust him.

I couldn't repay that by getting him destroyed.

"Fine." The word tasted like ash. "I'll come."

The scarred wolf nodded, satisfied. "Smart girl."

I walked toward them, my legs shaking but my spine straight. The book still warm in my hands. The symbols still glowing.

I didn't know what I was. But I was done running from it.

---

They didn't take me through the school. They led me through the forest, the three wolves flanking me like I was a prisoner on death march. The moon filtered through the trees, casting silver patterns on the ground.

After twenty minutes, we reached a clearing.

A helicopter sat in the center. Black, unmarked, rotors already spinning.

I stopped. "Seriously? A helicopter? What happened to pack runs and ancient traditions?"

The scarred wolf shifted. It was the first time I'd seen a wolf change forms up close. It wasn't like the movies. It was brutal—bones cracking, muscles reshaping, fur retreating into skin. He stood naked, unashamed, his scar now a jagged line across his human face.

"We adapt," he said. "The Council doesn't waste time on theatrics."

He tossed me a bag. "Clothes. Put them on."

I caught it one-handed. Inside was a simple black jumpsuit. No logos. No labels.

"You expect me to change in front of you?"

"I expect you to do what you're told."

I glared at him, but I pulled the jumpsuit on over my clothes. It was too big, but it would do.

The other two wolves shifted, and all three of them dressed from bags hidden near the helicopter's landing skids. They moved with military precision. Efficient. Lethal.

I climbed into the helicopter.

The interior was all dark leather and metal. No windows in the back. I was strapped into a seat facing the three of them.

The scarred one—the leader—sat across from me. "The book. Hand it over."

"No."

His eyes narrowed. "That wasn't a request."

"You didn't request. You demanded. And I'm saying no." I held the book against my chest. "You want it? Take it. But I'll make sure you remember the fight."

He stared at me for a long moment. The helicopter lifted off, the ground falling away beneath us.

"Keep it," he said finally. "The Council will take it from you anyway."

The flight took an hour. I spent it watching the three of them, memorizing their faces, their tells, the way they held themselves. Information was power. And I was done being powerless.

We landed on a rooftop. The city sprawled below us, glittering and unfamiliar.

"No landmarks," I said. "Classy."

"Move."

They led me down a stairwell, through a sterile hallway, into an elevator that went down instead of up. The floors ticked past. Floor 1. Floor -1. Floor -5.

Floor -10.

The doors opened onto a hallway of black marble and cold light. The walls seemed to absorb sound. My footsteps echoed anyway, a lonely rhythm in the silence.

At the end of the hall, a door. No handle. Just a smooth surface that slid open as we approached.

Inside, a room that could have been a courtroom. Raised dais at the far end, seven chairs arranged in a crescent. Six of them occupied.

The Council.

They were old. That was the first thing I noticed. Old in a way that went beyond years. Ancient. Their eyes held centuries of judgment.

One of them rose. A woman with silver hair and a face that had once been beautiful, now hardened into something cold and regal.

"Maya Chen." Her voice echoed. "You stand before the High Council of the Silver Fang Accord. You have been brought here to answer for your existence."

I laughed.

I couldn't help it. The absurdity of it hit me like a wave. They'd sent enforcers, a helicopter, dragged me to a secret underground bunker—to put me on trial for being born.

"You find this amusing?" The woman's eyes flashed gold.

"I find it pathetic." I met her gaze. "I didn't ask to be a hybrid. I didn't ask to be a Shadow Wolf. I didn't ask for your precious book to start glowing in my hands. But here I am. So either tell me what you actually want, or let me go back to school so I can fail my morning classes like a normal teenager."

Silence.

Then the woman smiled. It wasn't a kind smile.

"Oh, Maya. We don't want to hurt you." She stepped down from the dais, approaching me slowly. "We want to use you."

The book in my hands went cold.

"You see, the prophecy of the Shadow Wolf isn't about unity. It's about power. The last time a hybrid walked these lands, she nearly destroyed our kind." The woman stopped inches from me. "Do you know what she was called?"

I shook my head, my throat tight.

"The Moon Killer."

She reached out and touched my cheek. Her fingers were ice.

"You are not the first hybrid, Maya. You are the second. And the first one—" Her smile widened. "—was my sister."

The room seemed to tilt.

"Your sister—"

"Was executed three hundred years ago. By the very Council that now sits before you. Including me." Her eyes glistened. "I voted to kill her. And I would do it again."

I couldn't breathe.
