The female enforcer nodded slowly. "You have more control than most. But control isn't enough. You need to understand what you are. What you're becoming."

"So teach me."

"Can't." The male enforcer shook his head. "The book is the only record of the Shadow Wolf's abilities. And it's yours now. The Council will want it back."

"Then they'll have to take it."

"Don't be stupid, girl. You're strong, but you're not that strong. Three enforcers could tear you apart before you finished your first shift."

"Then why haven't you?"

Silence. The wind picked up, rustling the leaves, carrying the scent of pine and distant rain. The male enforcer's jaw tightened.

"Because the Alpha of the Northern Pack requested we bring you in peacefully. He wants to meet you."

"Meet me?"

"Tomorrow. At dawn. The Council chambers."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then we take you by force. And trust me, hybrid—" his eyes flashed amber, "—you don't want that."

I weighed my options. Fight. Run. Surrender. None of them were good. Fighting meant death. Running meant they'd hunt me, and they'd find me. Surrendering meant walking into the lion's den, alone, with nothing but a book that might be lying to me.

But surrender also meant time. Time to read. Time to understand. Time to prepare.

"Fine." I held up the book. "But this stays with me."

The female enforcer stepped forward. "Unacceptable. The book is Council property."

"Then tell the Council to take it from me themselves."

Her eyes narrowed. "You're arrogant."

"I'm desperate. There's a difference."

The male enforcer held up a hand. "Enough. She can keep the book until morning. It's warded against leaving the grounds anyway. She's not going anywhere."

I hadn't known that. But I kept my face neutral.

"Fine. I'll be there."

"Good." He shifted back to wolf form, his bones cracking, his fur rippling in the moonlight. The female enforcer followed. The third, who hadn't spoken, remained in human form, watching me with an expression I couldn't read.

They waited.

"Now," the male enforcer's voice came from the wolf's throat, distorted and deep. "We walk you back to your dorm."

"I know the way."

"Walk. Or we carry you."

I walked.

The path to the dorms was silent, the three wolves flanking me like shadows. Students we passed stopped and stared. Whispers followed. I kept my chin up, my grip on the book tight.

When we reached the dormitory steps, the male enforcer shifted back. "Dawn. Don't be late."

"Wouldn't dream of it."

He studied me for a long moment. Then he turned and disappeared into the trees. The other two followed.

I stood there, alone in the moonlight, the book still warm in my hands. My heart was pounding. My hands were shaking. But underneath the fear, something else was growing.

*Anger.*

They'd called me an experiment. A threat. Something to be controlled. They'd told me my kind had all died, that I was a failure waiting to happen.

*They were wrong.*

I climbed the stairs to my room, locked the door, and opened the book to the first page.

The ink had changed again. Now it showed a map. Not of the school, but of something else. Underground tunnels. A hidden chamber. Marked with a symbol I didn't recognize.

And at the bottom, new words:

*The Council meets at dawn. But the truth waits beneath. Find the hollow oak. Descend. Before they decide your fate.*

I read it three times. Then I grabbed my jacket and slipped out the window.

The moon was high. The school was quiet. I moved through the shadows, following the map etched into my memory, my wolf whispering beneath my skin.

*Find the hollow oak.*

*Before they decide your fate.*

I found it at the edge of the forest, an ancient tree split by lightning, its trunk hollow and dark. I climbed inside. The ground gave way beneath my feet.

I fell.

And when I landed, I was in a chamber carved from stone, lit by torches that burned with silver fire. And standing in the center, waiting for me, was a woman with eyes like mine.

Silver.

"You came," she said. "Good. We don't have much time."

"Who are you?"

She smiled, and it was sad and fierce and ancient all at once.

"My name is Lena. And I'm the Shadow Wolf who should have died three hundred years ago."

title: The Blood That Binds
content:
The three wolves didn't move. They didn't need to. Their presence alone was a cage, their amber eyes pinning Maya in place like prey.

She'd faced bullies before. Purebloods who thought her very existence was an insult. Professors who graded her harder, watched her closer, waited for her to fail. But these wolves—these were different. They carried themselves with the kind of confidence that came from decades of power. From killing without consequence.

The one who'd spoken stepped forward. He was tall, even in human form, with silver-streaked hair and a face carved from granite. His scent hit her—old blood, old magic, old authority.

"You have something that doesn't belong to you," he said.

Maya's arms tightened around the book. "I found it in the library."

"The library is restricted to students of your... pedigree."

"My pedigree?" The words came out sharp, defiant. She couldn't help it. "You mean my blood."

"Your blood is exactly what I mean." His eyes flicked to the book. "That text is bound to a lineage that was supposed to have ended. Yet here you are, holding it. Reading it. Awakening it."

"I didn't awaken anything. I just opened it."

"You opened it because it recognized you." Another step closer. "That book has been sitting in that library for seventy-three years. No one has been able to open it. Not the Headmaster. Not the Council's own scholars. But a half-breed walks in, touches the cover, and the pages start moving?" His voice dropped, low and dangerous. "You expect me to believe that's coincidence?"

Maya's mind raced. Kael was still in the library. Had he held them off? Was he okay? She couldn't hear fighting, couldn't hear anything but the wind and her own pounding heart.

"I don't know why it opened for me," she said, forcing her voice steady. "But I'm not giving it back."

The wolf laughed. It wasn't a pleasant sound. "You don't have a choice."

He moved.

Maya didn't think. She reacted. Three years of sneaking through the city at night, avoiding gangs and patrols, had honed her instincts. She dropped low, spun left, and sprinted toward the trees.

Behind her, she heard the shift. Bones cracking. Fabric tearing. The guttural snarl of a wolf taking form.

She didn't look back.

The forest was dark, the moon filtered through branches that clawed at the sky. She ran blind, the book pressed against her chest, roots and rocks trying to trip her. Her lungs burned. Her legs screamed. But she didn't stop.

She could hear them behind her. Three sets of paws. Closing fast.

She needed to shift. Her wolf was there, just beneath her skin, waiting. But shifting took focus, took control, and right now she had neither. She was pure panic, pure survival, pure instinct.

A shadow broke from the trees to her left, cutting her off. She veered right. Another shadow, faster. They were herding her. Driving her somewhere.

She burst into a clearing and skidded to a stop.

The Council wolves were already there. Six of them. Standing in a loose semicircle, their amber eyes fixed on her. The three from the forest emerged behind her, blocking her retreat.

She was surrounded.

The tall one stepped forward again, still in human form. He'd shifted back faster than she'd thought possible. "The book, Maya. Hand it over, and we'll talk."

"You'll kill me."

"If we wanted you dead, you'd already be dead. We're not assassins. We're the Order of the Silver Dawn. We protect the balance."

"By hunting hybrids?"

"By preserving what's sacred." His voice softened, almost kind. "You don't understand what you are. What that book means. The Shadow Wolf isn't a legend—it's a prophecy. And prophecies are dangerous. They upset the natural order."

"The natural order," Maya repeated, bitter. "You mean your order."

"I mean the order that keeps our kind alive. Three hundred years ago, the last Shadow Wolf nearly destroyed us. Not because she was evil, but because she was powerful. Too powerful. She couldn't control what she was, and her existence—her mere existence—threatened the Council's authority. Wars were fought. Blood was spilled. And when she finally fell, the Council made sure no hybrid would ever rise again."

"Until me."

"Until you." His eyes met hers, and for a moment, she saw something almost like regret. "You're not a monster, Maya. But you're a disruption. And disruptions have consequences."

Behind him, the wolves shifted restlessly. One of them growled low in its throat.

The tall one held out his hand. "The book. Last chance."

Maya looked at the book in her arms. The cover was warm now, almost hot. The pages seemed to hum, vibrating against her fingers. She thought of the drawing that had changed, the words that had appeared: *The hunt begins.*

They'd known. Somehow, they'd known she would find it.

"Who told you I was here?" she asked.

The tall one's expression flickered. "What?"

"The book. It said the Council sees. But you didn't see me coming—someone told you. Someone in the school."

He didn't answer. But his silence was answer enough.

"There's a traitor at Silver Fang," Maya whispered.

"There are many things at Silver Fang that you don't understand. The book—"

"Is mine." She said it with a certainty that surprised even her. "It opened for me. It responds to me. And I'm not giving it to people who want to erase what I am."

The tall one's face hardened. "Then you leave us no choice."

He raised his hand.

The wolves lunged.

Maya didn't have time to shift. She didn't have time to run. She closed her eyes, clutched the book, and braced for impact.

The impact never came.

Instead, she heard a sound like thunder cracking stone. She opened her eyes.

A wall of silver flame had erupted around her, curving like a dome, pushing the wolves back. They snarled and snapped, but the fire didn't burn them—it held them at bay, crackling and hissing like living thing.

The tall one stared, his composure finally cracking. "Impossible."

Maya looked down. The book was open in her hands. The pages were glowing, silver light spilling from them like liquid metal. And her own hands—her veins were glowing too. Silver lines traced beneath her skin, pulsing with the same light.

She felt it. The power. Cold and hot at once, ancient and new, roaring through her like a river breaking a dam.

The tall one took a step back. "She's already awakened. We're too late."

"What do you mean, too late?" one of the other wolves demanded.

"The book wasn't just showing her the past. It was unlocking her potential. She's not reading a prophecy—she's becoming one."

Maya looked at her hands. At the silver fire. At the wolves who had come to capture her, now held at bay by a power she didn't understand.

She should have been terrified. She should have been confused.

Instead, she felt something else. Something that had been buried deep, deep down, ever since she'd first arrived at Silver Fang.

She felt alive.

"Tell the Council," she said, her voice carrying over the crackling flames. "Tell them I'm not hiding anymore. Tell them the Shadow Wolf is awake. And tell them—" She met the tall one's eyes. "—if they want to hunt me, they'd better be ready to bleed."

The silver fire flared, blinding bright.

When it faded, Maya was gone.

The wolves stood in the empty clearing, the book's warmth fading from the air, the only trace of her a single silver hair caught on a branch.

The tall one picked it up, staring at it for a long moment.

"Find her," he said quietly. "Before the Council does something we can't undo."

But even as he said it, he knew.

The hunt had only just begun.

title: Chapter 15
content:
CHAPTER 15: The Blood in Her Veins

The three wolves circled her, their movements synchronized, practiced. Hunters. Maya's wolf clawed at her insides, desperate to break free, but she held it back. These weren't students testing their strength. These were adults who had killed before.

"I don't know who you think I am—"

"Don't." The lead wolf stepped closer. He was massive, gray-furred, with a scar running from his ear to his jaw. "We've been watching you since you stepped through the gate. Did you really think the Council would let a hybrid wander unsupervised?"

Maya's grip tightened on the book. "Then you know what I am."

"We know what you *should* be." His eyes flickered to the book. "The question is whether you've become it yet."

"What does that mean?"

"It means the Shadow Wolf is a legend. A myth. A story the old ones tell to frighten pureblood pups." He took another step. "But legends don't usually survive their first shift."

Maya's blood ran cold. "My first shift?"

"The full moon is in three nights." His lips curled, revealing teeth that were already lengthening. "Either you'll become what the book says you are, or you'll tear yourself apart trying. The Council wants to make sure it's the former."

"And if it's not?"

"Then we put you down before you become a danger to every pack within a hundred miles."

The book throbbed in her hands. She looked down. The ink had shifted again, the words rearranging themselves into a single sentence:

*Run. Now.*

She didn't question it.

Maya bolted. Not toward the dorms—they'd expect that. Toward the forest, where the trees were thick and the shadows deeper. She heard them behind her, the pound of heavy paws, the snarling breaths. But the forest was her home. She'd grown up hiding in the wilderness, learning to move without sound, to disappear into the dark.

She ducked under a low branch, veered left, jumped a fallen log. The book was warm against her chest, almost guiding her, pulling her toward something. She didn't fight it.

The trees thinned. A cliff edge loomed ahead, the ground falling away into a ravine. She skidded to a stop, rocks crumbling beneath her feet.

Dead end.

She turned. The three wolves emerged from the trees, their amber eyes gleaming.

"Nowhere to run, hybrid."

Maya looked down at the book. The page was blank. No more guidance. No more warnings.

She was alone.

She closed her eyes. She thought of her mother, human, fragile, who had loved a wolf and paid the price. She thought of every taunt, every sneer, every time she'd been told she didn't belong. She thought of Kael, who had believed in her before he even knew what she was.

And she thought of the wolf inside her, the one she'd spent eighteen years suppressing, the one that had never been allowed to run free.

*If I'm going to die,* she thought, *I'm going to die as myself.*

She let go.

The shift ripped through her like lightning. Bones cracked, muscles tore and reformed, skin split and sealed. Pain. Pure, blinding, beautiful pain. And then—

Silence.

She looked down at her paws. Silver fur, pale as moonlight. She looked at her reflection in a puddle—her eyes were silver now, burning like stars. Her wolf was half in shadow, half in light, exactly like the drawing in the book.

The three wolves stopped. For the first time, she saw fear in their eyes.

"The Shadow Wolf," the scarred one whispered. "Impossible."

Maya opened her mouth. A sound came out—not a growl, not a howl, but something in between. A word. A name.

*Council.*

The wolves flinched.

*Take me to them.*

The scarred one shook his head, but his legs were trembling. "We can't. We were ordered to bring you to the border. The Council will decide your fate from there."

*Then take me to the border.*

She stepped forward. The wolves parted, instinct overriding training. She walked through them, her silver fur brushing against their gray and brown, and they didn't dare touch her.

The forest stretched ahead, dark and endless. She walked for what felt like hours, the wolves following at a distance, the book somehow still clutched in her jaws. She didn't know where the border was. But something inside her did. Something ancient, something that had been waiting for this moment.

The trees opened onto a clearing. A stone archway stood at the center, carved with symbols she didn't recognize but somehow understood. The border.

Beyond it, the land was gray, lifeless. No moonlight touched it. No wind stirred.

The scarred wolf stopped. "This is as far as we go."

Maya shifted back. The change was smoother this time, less painful. She stood on two legs again, the book in her hands, her body humming with power she didn't fully understand.

"Now what?"

A figure stepped out from behind the archway. Tall. Pale. Dressed in black, his hair white as bone. His eyes were silver—the same silver as hers.

"Maya Chen." His voice was soft, almost gentle. "I've been waiting for you."

"Who are you?"

"I am the Council's emissary. My name is Alistair Crowe." He smiled, but it didn't reach his eyes. "And I'm here to offer you a choice."

"What choice?"

"You've awakened. The Shadow Wolf is real. But power without control is dangerous." He stepped closer. "The Council wants to train you. To guide you. To make sure you don't become a threat."

"And if I refuse?"

His smile widened. "Then we'll have to remove the threat."

Maya looked at the book. The page had changed again. New words, written in what looked like fresh ink:

*Don't trust him. He killed the last one.*

Her blood froze.

*The last Shadow Wolf. Three hundred years ago. He was there.*

She looked up at Alistair. His smile never wavered.

"What do you say, Maya? Ready to learn what you really are?"

She clutched the book tighter. Behind her, the three wolves waited. Ahead of her, a man who had murdered her predecessor.

And in three nights, the full moon.

"You're lying," she said. "I can see it in your eyes."

Alistair's smile flickered. Just for a second.

"I don't know what that book told you, but—"

"It told me enough." She took a step back. "The last Shadow Wolf. You killed her."

His face went still. The air around him seemed to darken, the temperature dropping.

"That's a dangerous accusation, child."

"I'm not a child. And I'm not your pawn." She turned to the three wolves, who were watching the exchange with growing unease. "You heard him. He's a murderer. Do you really want to serve someone like that?"

The scarred wolf hesitated. The others exchanged glances.

Alistair's voice turned cold. "You're making a mistake."

"No." Maya met his silver eyes with her own. "I'm making a choice."

She turned and ran. Back into the forest, back toward the academy, the book pressed against her chest. She heard Alistair's voice behind her, sharp and commanding:

"After her. And this time, don't hold back."

The sound of wolves howling filled the night.

Maya ran faster.

She didn't know where she was going. She only knew she had three nights to become strong enough to survive.

Three nights until the full moon.

Three nights until she either became the legend—or died trying.

The book's pages glowed, and new words appeared:

*Three nights. Find the Packless. They will teach you what the Council fears.*

Maya's lungs burned. Her legs screamed. But she kept running.

Behind her, the hunt had begun.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Cage of Bones

The three wolves didn't shift. They didn't need to. Their human forms carried the same menace as their animal ones—broad shoulders, predatory stillness, eyes that tracked Maya like she was prey already caught.

Maya's fingers dug into the book's cover. The warmth had faded, replaced by a cold that seeped into her palms. "Who sent you?"

The one who had spoken stepped forward. He was older than the professors, his face lined, his hair streaked with gray. But his build was solid, his jaw set. "The question isn't who sent us. It's how fast you're going to make this easy."

"I'm not going anywhere with you."

"You will." He pulled something from his pocket—a small metal disk, no bigger than a coin. He pressed the center, and it split open, revealing a dark, viscous liquid that dripped into the grass. The grass withered on contact.

Maya's wolf snarled inside her. *Silver compound. Fast-acting. If he touches you with that—*

"I know what that is," Maya said, keeping her voice steady. "And I know you're not supposed to use it on students."

"You're not a student. You're an anomaly." He took another step. "The Council has been watching you since the moment you stepped onto this campus. We knew what you were before you knew yourself."

"Then you know what I am. The Shadow Wolf."

His eyes flickered. Surprise. Or fear. She couldn't tell which.

"The book told you that?" He nodded at the tome in her hands. "That book shouldn't exist. It was supposed to be destroyed three centuries ago."

"Then why is it here?"

"Because someone wanted you to find it." His voice dropped. "Someone who wants chaos. Someone who wants to tear down everything the Council has built. And you're their weapon."

Maya's mind raced. Kael. The passage. The book waiting for her. Had he known? Had he led her there deliberately?

*No. He didn't know. He looked as shocked as I did.*

But doubt was a splinter she couldn't remove.

"I'm not a weapon," she said. "I'm a student. I came here to learn."

"You came here to survive." The man's voice was almost kind now. "And you have. Against all odds. But the Council doesn't let anomalies survive. They contain them. Or they end them."

The other two wolves moved, flanking her. She was surrounded. The dorm was too far. The library was too close. The forest stretched behind her, dark and endless.

She could run. She could shift. She could fight.

But the silver compound in his hand would end her before she took three steps in wolf form.

"What does the Council want with me?"

"They want to study you. Understand you. Then decide if you're a threat."

"And if I am?"

He didn't answer. He didn't need to.

Maya tightened her grip on the book. "Then they'll have to kill me. Because I'm not going quietly."

The man sighed. "I was hoping you'd say that." He tossed the silver compound at her feet.

The liquid spread, forming a circle around her. The grass died instantly, turning black. The ground seemed to pulse, and Maya felt a sharp burn in her chest—her wolf receding, forced back, caged.

She dropped to one knee, gasping. The book fell from her hands.

"The compound doesn't just burn," the man said. "It suppresses. You can't shift. You can't fight. You can't run."

Maya's vision blurred. The silver was in the air now, in her lungs, her blood. She felt her wolf howl in fury and pain.

*Get up. Get up. Get up.*

But her body wouldn't obey.

The man crouched in front of her, his face level with hers. "Your mother was a fool to hide you. Your father was a coward to abandon you. And you were stupid to believe you could belong here."

Maya's eyes snapped to his. "My father didn't abandon me."

"He did. The day you were born. He left your mother to raise you alone, knowing what you were, knowing the Council would come for you eventually."

"You're lying."

"I'm telling you the truth. Your father is a pureblood elder. He sits on the Council. And he voted to have you eliminated the moment they found you."

The words hit her like a physical blow. She couldn't breathe. Couldn't think.

*No. No, that's not true. He wouldn't—*

But the man's eyes held no deception. Only pity.

"He's been watching you from afar. Making sure you didn't become a threat. But now you've found the book. And that changes everything."

Maya's hands shook. The silver burned. Her wolf screamed.

"Why?" she managed.

"Because the Shadow Wolf isn't just a hybrid. It's a prophecy. A prophecy that says one day, a wolf of two bloods will unite the warring packs and bring peace to the Silver Lands. But the Council doesn't want peace. They want control. And you—" He tilted his head. "You are the end of their control."

Maya stared at him. At the book. At the darkness closing in.

"Let me go," she whispered.

"I can't."

"Then kill me."

He almost smiled. "That's not my decision. That's the Council's."

He reached for her.

And then the world exploded.

A blur of silver and black slammed into him from the side, sending him crashing into a tree. Maya blinked, her vision swimming, and saw Kael standing over her, his eyes blazing gold, his fists clenched.

"Get up," he said. "Now."

"Kael—"

"I said get up!"

She forced herself to her knees. The silver compound was still burning, but Kael grabbed her arm and pulled her out of the circle, dragging her through the dead grass, away from the three wolves who were already recovering.

The other two shifted mid-stride, their bodies rippling into massive wolves with bared fangs and glowing eyes.

Kael shoved Maya behind him. "Run. The forest. Don't stop."

"They'll kill you."

"They can try."

He shifted. His wolf was massive, darker than the night, his eyes burning like molten gold. He let out a roar that shook the trees.

The two Council wolves lunged.

Kael met them head-on.

Maya wanted to stay. Wanted to fight. But her legs were already carrying her into the forest, the book clutched against her chest, the sound of snarling and snapping echoing behind her.

She ran until her lungs burned. Until the forest swallowed every sound. Until she collapsed against a tree, gasping, shaking.

She looked down at the book.

The ink had changed again.

Now it showed a map. A winding path through the forest, leading to a cave marked with a symbol she didn't recognize. Beneath it, new words:

*The truth is buried. Dig deep enough, and you'll find your father's name.*

Maya's heart stopped.

She traced the path with her finger.

And then she heard it.

A howl. Not from behind her. From ahead.

Close.

Too close.

She slammed the book shut and ran again, deeper into the dark, with nothing but a prophecy and a ghost of a father she'd never known.

The hunt had only just begun.

title: The Blood in Her Veins
content:
The three wolves didn't move. They didn't need to. Their presence alone was a cage, their amber eyes pinning Maya in place like prey caught in a hunter's sight.

Maya's fingers dug into the book's leather cover. The warmth had faded, replaced by something cold and warning. Her wolf stirred beneath her skin, restless, ready.

"Who sent you?" Maya kept her voice steady. She'd learned long ago that fear was a scent predators could track.

The lead wolf—gray-furred, scarred across one eye—stepped closer. His shift was seamless, bone and muscle reshaping in a fluid ripple that spoke of decades of practice. The man who emerged was tall, broad-shouldered, with a face carved from granite and eyes that held no warmth.

"I asked you a question first, girl." His voice matched the gravel from before. "But I'll answer yours. The Council sent us. Which means you have exactly two choices: come quietly, or don't come at all."

Maya's mind raced. The library. Kael. The book that had predicted her arrival. The Council knew she was here, but how? She'd only been at Silver Fang for three weeks. She'd kept her head down, trained hard, avoided trouble.

Except trouble had a way of finding her anyway.

"The Council has no authority over students," she said, repeating the first rule every hybrid learned before they were old enough to shift. "The Academy Charter grants me protection until graduation. You can't touch me."

The scarred wolf laughed. It wasn't a pleasant sound. "The Charter protects students from *disciplinary action*. We're not here to discipline you, Maya. We're here to collect you for *testing*."

Testing. The word hit her like a blow to the chest. She'd heard rumors about Council testing. Wolves who disappeared into Council facilities and came back different. Or didn't come back at all.

"The book," she whispered, looking down at the cover. "You know about the book."

"We know about *you*." The wolf on the left spoke now, her voice female, cold as winter steel. "We've known since the moment you stepped through the Academy gates. The question was whether you'd reveal yourself or stay hidden. You chose poorly."

Maya's blood ran hot. "I didn't choose anything. I didn't ask for whatever that book shows."

The scarred wolf smiled. It was worse than his laugh. "No one asks for what they are. But everyone answers for it."

He reached for her.

Maya moved.

She'd spent eighteen years learning to survive. Her mother had taught her to read a room before entering it, to map exits in her head, to know when to fight and when to flee. The three wolves had positioned themselves to block the obvious paths—the dorms, the main building, the training fields.

But they'd left one gap open.

The forest.

It was a stupid move. The forest was dangerous, especially at night, especially for someone who hadn't fully learned to control her shift. But it was the only option that didn't end with her in a Council facility.

She bolted.

Behind her, she heard the snarls of shifting bodies, the thud of heavy paws hitting dirt. They were faster than her in wolf form, but she had a head start, and she knew the terrain from weeks of early morning runs.

Maya dodged left, ducked under a low-hanging branch, leaped over a fallen log. The book was still clutched against her chest, an anchor she couldn't let go. Behind her, the wolves crashed through the underbrush, gaining fast.

She needed to shift.

The thought terrified her. She'd only managed it twice before, and both times had been accidents—once when a group of purebloods had cornered her in the gym, once when she'd been so overwhelmed by emotion that her body had made the choice for her. She didn't know how to control it. She didn't know *what* she became when the shift took hold.

But the wolves were closing in.

Maya skidded to a halt in a small clearing, moonlight flooding down through a gap in the canopy. She turned to face her pursuers. The three wolves emerged from the trees, circling her, their amber eyes gleaming with predatory satisfaction.

"You can't outrun us," the scarred wolf said, his voice distorted by his shifted jaw. "You can't outfight us. Come quietly, and we won't hurt you."

Maya laughed. It surprised her. "You really think I believe that?"

She dropped the book.

It hit the ground with a thud, falling open to the page that still showed her face, her silver eyes, her half-formed wolf shadow. The ink seemed to glow in the moonlight, pulsing like a heartbeat.

And then Maya let go.

She stopped fighting the shift. She stopped fearing it. She opened herself to the wolf inside her, the part of her that had always been there, waiting, watching, wanting out. The change came like a wave crashing over her, bone and muscle and fur and fire.

When it was done, she stood on four legs, her breath misting in the cold air, her heart pounding with a rhythm that was both hers and not hers.

The three Council wolves stopped.

They stared.

The scarred wolf's ears flattened against his skull. "Impossible."

Maya looked down at herself. Her fur was silver-gray, like moonlight on water. Her eyes—she could feel them—burned silver, not gold. And her shadow... her shadow cast itself in two directions, as if she existed in two places at once.

She felt stronger than she'd ever felt. Faster. More alive.

The scarred wolf growled. "Doesn't matter what you are. Orders are orders."

He lunged.

Maya met him head-on.

Their bodies collided with a force that shook leaves from the trees. He was bigger, older, more experienced. But she was faster, and something else—something she didn't understand—surged through her veins like liquid fire.

She dodged his claws, spun, sank her teeth into his hind leg. He yelped, twisted, tried to bite her neck. She was already gone, circling, her movements fluid and unpredictable.

The other two wolves joined the fight.

Maya fought like she'd been born to it. Her body moved without thought, anticipating attacks before they came, finding openings that shouldn't exist. She took a claw to the shoulder, ignored the pain, retaliated with a bite to the female wolf's snout that drew blood.

But there were three of them, and she was tiring.

The scarred wolf caught her off-balance, slammed her into the ground. His jaws closed around her throat, not hard enough to kill, hard enough to pin.

"Enough," he snarled. "Shift back. Now."

Maya couldn't. She didn't know how.

The female wolf approached, something silver glinting in her paw. A collar, enchanted, meant to suppress a wolf's powers. "This will make it easier. Hold her still."

The scarred wolf pressed down harder. Maya gasped, her vision blurring at the edges.

The female wolf lifted the collar.

And then she stopped.

Her eyes went wide. Her mouth opened, closed, opened again. The collar fell from her paw as she crumpled to the ground, unconscious.

Maya didn't understand.

Neither did the other two wolves.

But then a figure stepped out of the shadows, and Maya's heart seized in her chest.

It was Kael.

But not the Kael she knew.

His eyes were pure black, no white, no iris, just endless darkness. His skin was pale, almost translucent, and veins of silver ran across his arms and neck like cracks in porcelain. He moved without sound, without breath, like something that existed between worlds.

The scarred wolf released Maya, backing away. "You. You're supposed to be—"

"Dead?" Kael's voice was different too. Echoing. Ancient. "I've been told that before."

He raised his hand. The scarred wolf flew backward, slammed into a tree, and didn't get up. The third wolf turned to run, but Kael was already there, his hand on the wolf's head, the animal going limp instantly.

In less than ten seconds, all three Council wolves lay unconscious on the forest floor.

Maya shifted back. It happened without her willing it, her body returning to human form as naturally as breathing. She stood on shaking legs, naked, cold, staring at Kael.

"What are you?" she whispered.

Kael turned to face her. The black in his eyes receded, slowly, like ink dissolving in water. The silver veins faded. Color returned to his skin. In a few heartbeats, he was the Kael she knew again—the quiet boy with the guarded eyes and the too-old smile.

But she'd seen what was underneath.

"The same thing you are," he said softly. "The thing the Council fears most."

He gestured to the book, still open on the ground. The page had changed again. Now it showed two figures—a girl with silver eyes, and a boy with darkness in his veins.

And beneath them, a single line of text:

*The Shadow Wolf and the Void Wolf. The beginning of the end. Or the end of the beginning.*

Maya looked up at Kael. He met her gaze, and for the first time, she saw fear in his eyes.

Not of her.

For her.

"I need to tell you everything," he said. "But we have to move. The Council sent three tonight. When they don't report back, they'll send thirty."

Behind him, the scarred wolf stirred, groaned, began to shift back.

Kael grabbed Maya's hand. "Trust me?"

She looked at the book, at the wolves, at the boy who was something more than she'd ever imagined.

"I don't have a choice," she said.

And they ran.

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Price of Knowing

The wolves circled, their amber eyes never leaving me. Three of them, massive even by werewolf standards, their fur dark as oil against the moonlit snow. The book in my hands felt like a beacon, pulsing with warmth that seemed to scream *here she is, here she is*.

I backed up until my spine hit a tree trunk. No escape. The dorms were too far. The library was blocked. And Kael—

Kael was still inside, facing whoever had come for us.

The largest wolf stepped forward. His shift was seamless, bone and muscle rearranging in a fluid motion that spoke of decades of practice. He stood before me, fully human, fully clothed in dark tactical gear that looked military-issue. His face was all sharp angles and cold purpose.

"Maya Chen," he said again. "You will come quietly. No one needs to get hurt."

"Who are you?" My voice came out steadier than I felt. "What do you want?"

"I am Marcus Vane. Enforcer for the High Council." He said it like the name should mean something. It did. Even I'd heard whispers about the Enforcers—the Council's personal hunters, the ones who made problems disappear. "You've been in possession of a restricted text for exactly seventeen minutes. In that time, you've triggered three blood wards, two tracking spells, and a territorial alarm that hasn't been activated in twelve years."

Seventeen minutes. It had felt like seconds.

"That book belongs to me," I said. "It responded to me."

"Exactly the problem." Marcus's eyes flicked to the book in my arms. "That volume is sealed. Meant to remain sealed. The fact that it opened for you is... concerning."

"Concerning enough to send three Enforcers?"

"Concerning enough to send three Enforcers." He took a step closer. "The Council has been watching you since the moment you stepped onto Academy grounds. A hybrid admitted to Silver Fang? First in three centuries? We're not fools. We knew something was different about you. We just didn't know how different until tonight."

"Then you know what I am."

"We know what the book claims you are." His voice hardened. "Claims are not facts. The Shadow Wolf is a legend, a myth used to frighten pureblood children into obedience. It's not a real thing."

"Then why are you here?"

"Because legends have power even when false. And power needs to be controlled." He extended his hand. "Give me the book. Come with us. There will be a hearing. Questions will be asked. If you cooperate, you'll be back in class by morning."

I didn't believe him. Not for a second.

"If I refuse?"

"Then we take you anyway." He smiled, and there was nothing friendly in it. "One way or another, Maya. Make this easy on yourself."

I thought about running. I could shift, maybe, if I could figure out how. My wolf was close, closer than she'd ever been, a presence at the edge of my consciousness that felt like a second heartbeat. But I didn't know how to call her. I didn't know if I could control her.

And these three were trained killers.

"Do it," I said.

Marcus nodded. The other two shifted back to human form, and I saw the patches on their shoulders—silver crescents on black. Council Enforcers. The real deal.

They moved toward me.

I held the book tighter, ready to fight even if I knew I'd lose.

Then the air split.

A howl, sharp and commanding, cut through the night like a blade. Not a warning. A summons. The kind of howl that made every hair on your body stand up, that triggered something primal and ancient in the blood.

The Enforcers froze. Marcus's head snapped toward the trees.

"No," he breathed.

A shape emerged from the darkness. Massive. Golden fur that caught the moonlight like liquid fire. Eyes like molten amber. A wolf so large I thought for a moment I was hallucinating.

But I wasn't.

Because I recognized those eyes.

*Professor Aldaine.*

The Alpha of Silver Fang Academy walked out of the shadows, and the very air seemed to bow to her. She was in her wolf form, and she was terrifying—not in the way the Enforcers were terrifying, with their cold efficiency and tactical gear. She was terrifying the way the moon was terrifying. The way the wilderness was terrifying. The way every story you'd ever been told about wolves and monsters and things that hunted in the dark was terrifying.

She stopped between me and the Enforcers.

And then she shifted.

It was the most graceful transformation I'd ever seen. No pain, no struggle. Just a flow of light and shadow, and then Professor Aldaine stood before us in her tailored black suit, her silver-streaked hair perfect, her expression carved from stone.

"Marcus," she said, her voice velvet over steel. "You're on my territory."

"Elara." He didn't bow. Didn't show any deference. "We're acting on Council orders."

"Your Council has no jurisdiction within Academy walls. You know this."

"The book is a Council matter."

"The book is an Academy matter until it leaves this campus." Professor Aldaine stepped forward, and Marcus stepped back. I saw it. The smallest retreat, but it was there. "And this student is under my protection. You will leave. Now."

"You're making a mistake."

"I've made many mistakes, Marcus. This isn't one of them." She smiled, and it was sharp as broken glass. "Tell the Council that if they want a conversation about Maya Chen, they can send a formal request. Through proper channels. With proper documentation. And maybe, if I'm feeling generous, I'll consider it."

"You're harboring a—"

"I'm harboring nothing." Her voice cracked like a whip. "I'm protecting a student. As is my right. As is my duty. Now. Leave. Before I decide that three Enforcers trespassing on Academy grounds constitutes a threat serious enough to warrant extreme measures."

Marcus held her gaze for a long moment. Then he looked at me. His eyes promised things I didn't want to imagine.

"This isn't over," he said.

"It never is."

He turned. The other two followed. They melted back into the trees, and within seconds, I couldn't sense them anymore. They were just... gone.

I sagged against the tree, my legs threatening to give out.

Professor Aldaine didn't look at me. She stared into the darkness where the Enforcers had disappeared, her jaw tight.

"Professor," I started.

"Not here." She finally turned, and her eyes were softer than I'd ever seen them. Worried. Scared, even. "We need to talk. Privately."

"I have questions."

"I know."

"The book says I'm—"

"I know what it says." She held up a hand. "I know more than you can imagine. And I should have told you sooner. That's my failure. But I need you to trust me for a few more hours. Can you do that?"

I looked down at the book in my hands. The pages had gone still, the ink no longer moving, the silver-eyed wolf frozen in place. But I could feel it, humming beneath my fingers. Waiting.

"Kael," I said. "He's still in the library."

"Kael can handle himself. I have people taking care of it."

"People?"

"People I trust." She finally met my eyes. "Which is more than I can say for most of this Academy right now."

She started walking, and I followed. Not because I trusted her completely—I wasn't sure I trusted anyone at this point. But because I didn't have any other options. Because the Enforcers were out there. Because the Council knew my name. Because the book in my hands had called me a legend and a target in the same sentence.

Professor Aldaine led me to her office. Not the classroom. Her private office, hidden behind a bookshelf, filled with artifacts and old manuscripts and the smell of ancient paper. She closed the door and locked it with a spell I felt more than saw.

"Sit," she said.

I sat.

She poured two glasses of amber liquid from a decanter and handed one to me. "Drink. You'll need it."

"What is it?"

"Something that'll take the edge off. Trust me."

I drank. It burned going down, but warmth spread through my chest, steadying my shaking hands. I set the glass down and looked at her.

"You knew," I said. "You knew what I was when you admitted me."

"I suspected." She sat across from me, her drink untouched. "I wasn't sure until tonight. The book confirmed it."

"How?"

"The Sealed Codex of Lunar Origins doesn't open for just anyone. It's keyed to a specific bloodline. A bloodline that was supposed to be extinct." She paused. "Your father must have been something very special."

"Was he a Shadow Wolf too?"

"I don't know. I never met him. But I know he was a hybrid. I know he was powerful. And I know the Council killed him before you were born."

The words hit me like a physical blow. I'd never known anything about my father. My mother refused to talk about him, and I'd learned not to ask. But hearing it spoken aloud, so casually, so certainly—

"They killed him," I repeated.

"Hybrids are dangerous to the old power structures. They represent change. And the Council hates change." She leaned forward. "Maya, I need you to understand something. The Enforcers aren't the worst of what's coming. The Council has methods you can't imagine. They have resources. They have centuries of experience making problems disappear."

"Then why are you helping me?"

"Because I was young once. And scared. And someone helped me." Her eyes went distant, remembering. "The Shadow Wolf isn't just a legend, Maya. It's a prophecy. And prophecies don't come true unless someone fights for them."

"Prophecy?" My voice cracked. "I'm not a prophecy. I'm just a girl who wants to survive school."

"No. You're not." She stood and walked to her desk, pulling out a drawer. She removed something small and silver and brought it to me. A pendant on a chain. A wolf, half-formed, caught between shadow and light. "This belonged to your father. I've been keeping it safe for eighteen years, waiting for the right moment."

I took it. The metal was warm against my palm. It felt like it recognized me.

"Where did you get this?"

"From your mother. The night she left you here." Professor Aldaine's voice was gentle now. "She knew this day would come. She asked me to protect you until you were ready."

"My mother gave you this?"

"She gave me more than that. She gave me instructions. Warnings. And a promise." She paused. "She said you would be the one to change everything. That the Shadow Wolf would rise again, and the old world would fall, and something new would take its place."

I stared at the pendant. At the wolf caught between forms.

"I don't want to change everything," I whispered. "I just want to belong somewhere."

"Sometimes those are the same thing."

A knock at the door made us both freeze.

Professor Aldaine moved faster than I could track, her hand on the handle, her body positioned between me and whoever was outside. "Who is it?"

"Kael."

She opened the door. He stood there, breathing hard, his shirt torn, a bruise forming on his jaw. He looked past her, straight at me.

"They know," he said. "The whole Academy knows. Someone saw you leave the

title: The Weight of Prophecy
content:
Maya's first instinct was to run. Her second was to shift. But before she could do either, the largest of the three wolves—a male with a scar splitting his left ear—stepped closer, his amber eyes fixed on the book in her arms.

"You have something that doesn't belong to you."

"My hands. My feet. My dignity. Take your pick." Maya's voice came out steadier than she felt. "The book stays with me."

The wolf to his left—leaner, with a patch of white fur over one eye—growled low. "You don't know what you're holding, girl."

"Then enlighten me." Maya shifted her weight, calculating the distance to the dorm. Thirty feet. Maybe twenty-five if she sprinted. But they'd catch her before she made it halfway. She needed time. Time for Kael to get out of the library. Time for someone to notice three full-grown wolves prowling behind the student dorms.

Scar-Ear tilted his head. "That book is Council property. Sealed in the restricted archives for a reason."

"And breaking the seal to read it?" White-Eye added. "That's a violation punishable by exile."

Maya laughed. Actually laughed. The sound surprised even her. "Exile? I've been here four days, and half the school wants me gone. What's one more person on the list?"

The third wolf—silent until now, smaller than the others but with eyes that held a cold, calculating intelligence—spoke. "The book isn't the only thing we were sent to retrieve."

Maya's blood went cold.

"We were told to bring you to the Council. Willingly or otherwise."

"Will you settle for a strongly worded letter of refusal?"

White-Eye lunged.

Maya was faster.

She dove left, rolling across the damp grass, the book pressed against her ribs. The wolf's jaws snapped where her leg had been. She came up running, her heart hammering, her bare feet slipping on the wet earth. She'd left her shoes in the library. Of course she had.

Behind her, she heard them give chase. Three sets of paws, gaining ground.

The dorm was close. Twenty feet. Fifteen.

A body slammed into her from the side.

She hit the ground hard, the impact driving the air from her lungs. The book skidded across the grass, pages fluttering open under the moonlight. Maya scrambled for it, but White-Eye was already there, his paw pinning the cover, his muzzle inches from her face.

"Stupid girl. Did you really think you could hide from us?"

Maya's wolf surged inside her. Not the controlled, calm presence she'd been learning to manage. This was raw, furious, *desperate*. Her nails elongated. Her vision sharpened. The silver in her eyes burned so bright she saw the wolf flinch.

"Get off me," she snarled, and her voice wasn't entirely human.

White-Eye hesitated. Just a fraction of a second. But it was enough.

A shape exploded from the trees—dark fur, golden eyes, and a snarl that tore through the night like thunder. Kael hit White-Eye at full speed, sending the wolf tumbling across the grass. He stood over Maya, hackles raised, blood dripping from a gash on his shoulder.

*Run*, his eyes said. *Now.*

Maya grabbed the book and ran.

She didn't look back. She didn't stop. She crashed through the dormitory doors, up the stairs, down the hall, and into her room, slamming the door behind her. Her hands were shaking so badly she dropped the book twice before she locked the deadbolt.

She stood there, chest heaving, listening.

Silence.

No howls. No footsteps. No scratching at her door.

She slid down the wall, the book clutched to her chest, and tried to remember how to breathe.

---

Twenty minutes later, a knock came at her window.

Maya nearly shifted on the spot. But it was Kael—human again, wearing a shirt that was torn and stained with blood, his face pale but his eyes steady. She unlatched the window and let him climb in.

"You're bleeding," she said.

"I'll live." He winced as he sat on the edge of her bed. "They're gone. For now."

"For now." Maya's voice was flat. "That's supposed to reassure me?"

"It's the best I've got." He looked at the book on her desk. "Did you read more of it?"

"I was a little busy being hunted by Council assassins."

"They weren't assassins. They were scouts. Information gatherers." Kael's jaw tightened. "If they'd wanted you dead, you'd be dead."

"That's supposed to make me feel better?"

"It's supposed to make you understand how serious this is. The Council sent scouts to retrieve a book and a student. That means they're watching. They've been watching since you arrived."

Maya paced the small room, her mind racing. "The prophecy said the hunt begins. I thought it was metaphorical."

"It wasn't."

"So what do I do? Hide in my room for the rest of the semester? Transfer schools? Fake my own death?"

Kael was quiet for a moment. Then he said, "There's someone who can help. Someone who knows more about the Shadow Wolf than any living wolf."

Maya stopped pacing. "Who?"

"My grandmother."

She stared at him. "Your grandmother."

"She was the librarian here forty years ago. She's the one who told me about the restricted section. The one who warned me that the Council was hiding something." He met her eyes. "She's the one who told me to watch for someone like you."

Maya's heart hammered. "She knew I was coming?"

"She knew *someone* was coming. The prophecy isn't new, Maya. It's been in the book for centuries. What changed is that you showed up." He stood, swaying slightly. "My grandmother lives off-grid. No Council surveillance. No pack loyalties. If anyone can help you understand what you are and why the Council wants you, it's her."

"And how do we get there without being caught?"

Kael smiled—a tired, crooked smile that didn't reach his eyes. "Leave that to me."

---

They left at dawn.

Kael had a plan, and Maya had to admit it was good. They used the maintenance tunnels beneath the school, the ones that led to the old service road behind the kitchens. Kael had stolen a set of keys from the groundskeeper's shed—a battered truck that smelled like fertilizer and desperation.

"This is our escape vehicle?" Maya asked, staring at the rust-bucket.

"It's inconspicuous."

"It's a death trap."

"It's a death trap that runs." Kael cranked the engine. It coughed, sputtered, and roared to life. "Get in."

They drove for three hours, winding through back roads and forest trails, the book sitting on Maya's lap like a hot coal. She hadn't opened it again. She was afraid of what she'd see.

Kael's grandmother lived in a cabin at the edge of a lake, so remote that the last ten miles were unpaved. The cabin was small, weathered, smoke curling from a stone chimney. An old woman sat on the porch, rocking, a blanket over her knees.

She looked up as the truck approached. Her eyes were the same golden-brown as Kael's. And they fixed on Maya with an intensity that made her want to shrink into the seat.

"So," the old woman said as they climbed out. "You're the one."

Maya swallowed. "I guess I am."

The woman studied her for a long moment. Then she gestured to the book in Maya's hands. "You've read the first page."

"Yes."

"You've seen the wolf."

"Yes."

"Then you know what they're afraid of." The woman's voice was soft, ancient, like wind through dry leaves. "You're not just a hybrid, Maya. You're a bridge. Between wolves and humans. Between the old ways and the new. Between the light and the dark."

Maya's hands tightened on the book. "I'm just trying to survive school."

"No." The woman shook her head. "You're trying to survive a war that started before you were born. And now that you're here, now that the book has chosen you, there's no going back."

Maya felt the weight of those words settle on her shoulders like a shroud.

Kael stepped forward. "Grandmother, can you help her?"

The old woman was quiet for so long that Maya thought she hadn't heard. Then she lifted her hand and pointed at Maya's chest.

"The help she needs isn't from me. It's from the wolf inside her. The one she's been hiding from." Her eyes locked with Maya's. "You've spent your whole life suppressing your wolf. Treating it like a curse. A weakness. But the prophecy isn't about a hybrid who learned to fit in. It's about a hybrid who learned to *embrace* what she is."

Maya's throat tightened. "I don't know how."

"Yes, you do." The old woman smiled—a sad, knowing smile. "You've been doing it all along. Every time you survived. Every time you fought back. Every time you refused to break."

She reached out and touched Maya's cheek, her fingers papery and cool.

"The Shadow Wolf isn't a monster, child. It's a gift. But only if you're brave enough to claim it."

Maya closed her eyes. And for the first time in eighteen years, she stopped fighting the wolf inside her.

She let it rise.

The shift came faster than it ever had before—not painful, not terrifying, but natural, like taking a deep breath. Her bones reshaped. Her senses sharpened. The world bloomed into a symphony of scents and sounds.

She opened her eyes.

She was in her wolf form. Silver eyes. Fur as dark as midnight with a streak of white running down her chest. She looked at her reflection in the cabin window and saw the wolf from the prophecy. Half-shadow. Half-light.

Kael stared at her, his breath caught.

The old woman nodded slowly. "There you are."

Maya felt the wolf's voice inside her—not separate from her own, but part of it. Part of *her*. And for the first time, she didn't feel torn between two worlds.

She felt whole.

Then the old woman's face changed. Her smile vanished, replaced by something cold and sharp.

"You need to leave. Now." She turned to Kael. "They're coming. More than scouts this time."

Kael's face went pale. "How many?"

"The Council sent a hunter. A real one." The old woman's voice cracked. "He'll be here within the hour."

Maya shifted back, her heart pounding. "A hunter?"

"An executioner." Kael grabbed her arm. "We need to move."

But Maya stood frozen, the book heavy in her hands, the prophecy burning in her mind.

*The hunt begins.*

She looked at Kael. "I'm done running."

His eyes widened. "Maya—"

"If the Council wants a war, I'll give them one." Her voice was steady, her wolf humming beneath her skin. "But I'm not going to be the prey anymore."

The old woman laughed—a dry, rasping sound. "Good. Because running wouldn't have saved you anyway."

From somewhere in the distance, a howl split the air.

It was unlike any howl Maya had ever heard. Deep. Ancient. Hungry.

The hunter had arrived.

title: Chapter 6
content:
CHAPTER 6: The Council's Grip

The three wolves stood in a loose semicircle, cutting off any escape back toward the dorms. Their fur was dark—two black, one deep gray—and they moved with the fluid precision of predators who'd spent decades perfecting their craft.

Maya's fingers dug into the book's leather cover. The pages hummed against her palm, a low vibration that traveled up her arm and settled behind her ribs.

"I don't know who you think I am," she said, keeping her voice steady, "but I'm just a student."

The gray wolf stepped forward. In the moonlight, his form rippled, muscles shifting, bones realigning. The transformation was seamless—one breath he was a wolf, the next he was a man. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Silver-streaked hair. Eyes that held centuries of judgment.

"I'm aware of who you are, Maya Chen." His voice matched the gravel she'd heard. "I'm aware of your mother. Your father. And the blood that runs through your veins."

Maya's heart slammed against her ribs. She'd never known her father. Her mother refused to speak of him. Every question had been met with silence, deflection, or tears.

"I don't know what you're talking about."

The man smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "The book you're holding suggests otherwise."

She looked down. The cover had changed. Where there had been plain leather, silver letters now curled across the surface, forming words she couldn't read but somehow understood. *Blood of the Betrayer. Heir of the Shadow. Marked for extinction.*

"You're reading it now, aren't you?" The man took a step closer. "You can see the words. The ones meant only for your bloodline."

Maya lifted her chin. "So what if I can?"

"Then you know why we're here."

"I know you're Council." She forced the words out, buying time, her mind racing through escape routes. "I know you want hybrids dead. I know you think we're abominations."

"We think nothing of the sort." The man's tone shifted, something almost like regret coloring his words. "We fear what you represent. There's a difference."

"Doesn't feel like one from where I'm standing."

The two black wolves behind him growled, low and warning. The man raised a hand, silencing them.

"You have three options, Maya. Come with us peacefully. Come with us unconscious. Or don't come with us at all, and watch everyone you've spoken to at this academy face the consequences of harboring an unregistered hybrid."

Her blood went cold. "That's not a choice. That's blackmail."

"It's leverage. There's a difference."

"Funny. Doesn't feel like one from where I'm standing."

The man's smile tightened. "Clever girl. Your mother was the same way. Right up until the night she disappeared."

Maya's vision went red. "What do you know about my mother?"

"I know she made a choice. A dangerous one. She chose to protect you, even knowing what you would become. Even knowing the price." He paused. "She paid that price in full."

"She's not dead." Maya's voice cracked. "She's not."

"She's not dead," the man agreed. "But she's not free either. And she won't be, as long as the Council has reason to keep her contained."

The book pulsed against Maya's chest, hot now, almost burning. The words on the cover shifted again. *Her blood. Her choice. Her war.*

"You're lying."

"I never lie, Maya. It's inefficient." The man extended his hand. "Come with me. Speak to the Council. Tell them what you know about your heritage, your abilities, your father. Do that, and your mother walks free."

"And if I refuse?"

"Then your mother stays in the cell she's occupied for the last eighteen years. And everyone you've met at this academy—your roommate, your instructors, the boy who helped you find that book—they all face questioning. Internal Affairs. The kind of questioning that leaves marks."

Maya's hands trembled. The book was so hot now she could barely hold it. The silver letters had turned to gold, pulsing like a heartbeat.

She thought of her mother. Of the last time she'd seen her, standing in the doorway of their small apartment, tears streaming down her face, whispering *run*.

She thought of Kael. Of the way he'd pushed her into that passage without hesitation. Of the trust in his eyes.

She thought of herself. A half-wolf, half-human hybrid, holding a book that responded to her blood, standing in the moonlight, facing down three wolves who wanted to cage her.

No.

Not cage.

Extinguish.

"I'll come with you," she said.

The man's expression didn't change, but something in his posture relaxed. "Wise choice."

"On one condition."

"You're not in a position to negotiate."

"I'm the Shadow Wolf." The words came out before she could stop them, and she felt the air shift around her, the moonlight seeming to bend toward her skin. "The first of my kind in three centuries. The *last* of my kind, if you have your way. I think that puts me in exactly the position to negotiate."

The two black wolves shifted, uneasy. Even the gray wolf's composure flickered, just for a moment.

"Name your condition."

"I want to see her. My mother. Before I speak to the Council. I want proof she's alive."

The man studied her for a long moment. The wind picked up, rustling the leaves, carrying the scent of pine and distant rain.

"Fine," he said finally. "You'll see her tomorrow night. But if you try anything—"

"Everyone I care about pays the price. I heard you." Maya tucked the book under her arm. "Let's go."

She fell into step beside him, the two black wolves flanking them. Her heart was racing, her mind spinning, but she forced herself to breathe, to think, to plan.

The book had told her she was hunted.

It had also told her she was the Shadow Wolf.

And somewhere in these pages, she was going to find out exactly what that meant.

They walked through the trees, away from the dorms, away from the academy, toward a black car waiting on a dirt road Maya had never noticed before. The Council's man opened the back door.

"After you."

She climbed in. The leather seats were cold. The windows were tinted black. The door closed with a heavy thunk, and she was alone.

The book still burned against her chest.

She opened it to the last page she'd read. The ink had shifted again, forming new words:

*The Council thinks they cage the wolf.*
*But the wolf has always been the cage.*
*Break it.*

Maya traced the letters with her finger. They glowed silver at her touch.

She didn't know what it meant.

But she was going to find out.

The car pulled away from the academy, headlights cutting through the dark, taking her toward a future she'd never imagined.

Behind her, she heard the distant howl of a wolf.

It sounded like Kael.

It sounded like a warning.

And then the trees swallowed the sound, and the road curved into shadow, and Maya was gone.

title: Chapter 7
content:
CHAPTER 7: The Blood in the Stone

The three wolves didn't move closer. They didn't need to. Their presence alone was a cage, their amber eyes pinning Maya in place like prey.

She clutched the book tighter. The pages hummed against her fingers, a low vibration that traveled up her arms and settled in her chest. *The hunt begins.* The words echoed in her skull.

"You have the wrong person," she said, keeping her voice steady. "I'm just a student."

The lead wolf—gray-furred, scarred across his muzzle—let out something between a growl and a laugh. "You're Maya Chen. Half-blood. Library intruder. Possessor of stolen texts." He took a step forward. "That's three strikes. The Council doesn't give fourth chances."

"I didn't steal anything. The book—"

"Will be returned. Along with you."

The wolf on the left shifted. His bones cracked, his form twisting and reshaping until a man stood where the beast had been. Tall. Bald. A silver tattoo curling around his throat like a noose. He was dressed in black, no insignia, no uniform. Council enforcers didn't need uniforms.

"The Headmaster will hear about this," Maya said, backing up a step. Her heel hit the edge of the tree line.

"The Headmaster answers to the Council," the bald man said. "Just like everyone else."

The third wolf remained in form, circling around her left flank, cutting off any escape toward the dorms. They'd done this before. They knew how to box in a target.

Maya's wolf stirred beneath her skin. Not the frantic scrabbling of fear, but something colder. Sharper. The silver-eyed wolf from the book, half in shadow, half in light. *The first of her kind in three centuries.*

She felt it then. A pulse. Low and rhythmic, like a second heartbeat. Coming from the book.

"You feel that?" the scarred wolf said, his amber eyes narrowing. "The text recognizes its keeper. That alone condemns you."

"I'm not condemned for reading a book."

"You're condemned for being what you are." He shifted, his body rippling into human form. A man in his forties, hard-jawed, cold-eyed. A brand on his forearm—a crescent moon inside a circle. Council mark. "The hybrid experiment ended seventy years ago. The survivors were... dealt with. You shouldn't exist."

Maya's blood went cold. "Experiment?"

"You think you're the first? The Council spent decades trying to merge wolf and human bloodlines. Every attempt failed. Every hybrid turned feral or died." He smiled, and there was nothing kind in it. "Except you. Which means either you're a miracle, or someone's been lying to the Council. Either way, you're a problem that needs solving."

He reached for her.

Maya moved.

She didn't think. Her body acted before her mind caught up. She dropped low, twisted past his grasp, and drove her elbow into his ribs. He grunted, surprised. The book flared hot against her chest, and suddenly she was faster, sharper, her senses expanding like a burst of light.

She saw the bald man reaching for her from the left. She heard the third wolf's claws scraping dirt as he lunged. She felt the air shift, the angles of attack, the openings.

She dodged the bald man's grab by inches. She spun, the book swinging like a weapon, and cracked it across the wolf's snout as he leaped. He yelped, stumbling back.

The scarred man recovered, his eyes wide. "She's already manifesting. Take her alive—the Council will want to study—"

Maya didn't wait to hear the rest. She ran.

The trees swallowed her. The night was cold and dark, but her eyes adjusted, the world sharpening into grays and silvers. She could smell them behind her—three scents, all male, all wolf, all hunting.

She pushed harder. Her legs burned. The book pulsed in rhythm with her heart.

*She comes. The Council sees. The hunt begins.*

The words weren't a warning. They were a trigger.

She burst through the tree line and hit the edge of the training fields. Empty at this hour. The obstacle course loomed ahead, wooden walls and rope climbs and mud pits. She could lose them there, double back, reach the dorms—

A howl split the night. Not behind her. Ahead.

Two more wolves emerged from the shadows near the gymnasium. Younger. Students. Their eyes glowed with a fervor that had nothing to do with moonlight.

"Council operatives," one of them said, his voice reverent. "We've been waiting for the signal."

They were students. *Students.* Recruited to hunt her.

Maya skidded to a halt, trapped between the wolves behind and the wolves ahead. Five of them now. The odds were impossible.

The book burned.

She looked down. The page had changed again. The ink swirled, forming words in a script she shouldn't have been able to read but understood perfectly:

*The blood remembers. The stone answers. Speak the name of the first.*

"What name?" she whispered. "I don't know any names."

The wolves closed in. The scarred man stepped into the clearing, rubbing his ribs where she'd hit him. "Clever little hybrid. But clever doesn't save you. It just makes the dissection more interesting."

Maya's hand trembled on the book. The words pulsed. *Speak the name of the first.*

She didn't know. She didn't *know.*

But the book did.

She opened it to a random page. The text blazed silver, illuminating her face, casting long shadows across the grass. The wolves flinched, shielding their eyes.

The words rearranged themselves, forming a name:

*ALDRIC.*

Maya spoke it. "Aldric."

The ground trembled.

The wolves froze. The scarred man's composure cracked. "That's not possible. She can't—she doesn't have the bloodline—"

A crack split the earth between Maya and the wolves. Not a fissure. A seam. Stone rose from the dirt, gray and ancient, carved with symbols that glowed the same silver as the book's ink.

It kept rising. A pillar. An obelisk. Standing twice as tall as any wolf, covered in writing that predated the Council, the Academy, everything.

Maya stared. She felt the pull of it, the recognition. The blood remembers. The stone answers.

The lead wolf's voice was hoarse. "The Council stone. It hasn't risen in three hundred years." He looked at Maya with something new in his eyes. Not contempt. Fear. "Who the hell are you?"

Maya didn't answer. She couldn't. Because the stone was speaking to her, not in words, but in images. Flashes of a woman with silver eyes, standing before an army of wolves. A child born in secret, hidden away. A bloodline erased from history, hunted to extinction.

Her bloodline.

The stone knew her. The book knew her. And now the Council knew she existed.

She looked at the wolves surrounding her. Five of them. More coming. The Academy was no longer a school. It was a trap.

"Get away from her."

The voice came from behind the stone. Low. Familiar.

Kael stepped out of the shadows, his eyes blazing gold, his wolf close to the surface. He wasn't in uniform. He was bloody, one sleeve torn, a gash across his cheek.

He'd fought his way out of the library.

"I said get away from her." His voice carried a weight that made even the scarred man hesitate. "She's under my protection."

"Your protection means nothing to the Council," the scarred man said.

"Then the Council can answer to my father."

Silence.

The bald man's eyes widened. "The Alpha of the Northern Pack has no jurisdiction here."

"Then call him. Tell him his son is harboring the hybrid." Kael's smile was sharp and cold. "See how that conversation goes."

The scarred man's jaw tightened. He looked at the stone, at the book, at Maya. Then he took a step back.

"This isn't over," he said. "The Council knows. They'll send more. And next time, they won't send students."

He turned and shifted, disappearing into the trees. The others followed, one by one, until the clearing was empty except for the stone, the book, and the two of them.

Maya's legs gave out. She sank to her knees, the book falling from her hands, her breath coming in ragged gasps.

Kael was beside her in an instant, his hand on her shoulder. "We need to move. Now. They'll regroup and be back before dawn."

"Where?" Her voice cracked. "Where do I go?"

He looked at the stone. At the symbols glowing in the dark. At the book lying open on the grass, its pages still silver.

"There's only one place the Council can't reach," he said. "The place that stone came from. The original Silver Fang temple. Buried beneath the Academy for three centuries."

Maya looked up at him. "You know where it is?"

"No." He picked up the book, closed it, and held it out to her. "But the book does. And it chose you."

She took the book. It felt heavier now. Weighted with purpose.

"I don't understand any of this," she said.

"You don't have to. Not yet." He pulled her to her feet. "But you need to decide. Right now. Do you run, or do you fight?"

Maya looked at the stone, still glowing, still waiting. She thought of the woman with silver eyes. The child born in secret. The bloodline they'd tried to erase.

She thought of her mother, who'd died when Maya was six, who'd never told her the truth about what she was. Who'd hidden her, protected her, loved her until the very end.

The book pulsed in her hands.

She looked at Kael. "I fight."

He nodded. "Good. Because the Council's not the only thing coming." He pointed at the stone, where new words were forming, crawling up the surface like living things.

*The blood awakens. The old ones stir. The war begins anew.*

Maya stared at the words. "What does that mean?"

Kael's face was pale in the silver light. "It means the hybrids weren't all killed. Some were imprisoned. And you just woke them up."

The ground trembled again. Deeper this time. From somewhere beneath the Academy, something answered.

Maya clutched the book.

The hunt had only just begun.

title: Chapter 8
content:
CHAPTER 8: The Hunt Begins

Maya's wolf surged beneath her skin, demanding release. Three amber-eyed wolves blocked her path, their massive forms half-hidden in the treeline. The book in her hands burned hot, almost painful.

"Who sent you?" Maya's voice came out steadier than she felt.

The lead wolf—gray-furred, scarred across one eye—stepped closer. "The Council doesn't announce itself. You come, or we drag you."

Behind her, the library door crashed open. Kael's voice rang out: "Maya, run!"

She ran.

Not toward the dorms. Not toward safety. Toward the forest, where the trees were thick and the moonlight barely reached the ground. Her legs pumped, her breath came sharp, and the wolves gave chase.

They were faster.

She felt the heat of them, the weight of their presence closing in. The book slipped in her sweaty hands. She adjusted her grip, risked a glance back—and saw Kael shift mid-stride, his uniform shredding as a massive black wolf tore free.

He hit the gray wolf like a battering ram.

They rolled, snarling, snapping. The other two wolves veered around them, still chasing Maya.

She burst through a thicket, branches scratching her arms, her face. The ground sloped down, then up again. She didn't know where she was going. She just knew she couldn't stop.

A wolf lunged from the side.

Maya twisted, barely avoiding its jaws. She stumbled, fell, rolled. The book flew from her hands, landed open on the forest floor. The glowing ink cast strange shadows across the leaves.

The wolf circled her, its amber eyes hungry.

"No," Maya whispered. She pushed herself up, felt her wolf clawing at her insides, demanding to be let out. But she'd never shifted before. She didn't know if she could. Didn't know if her hybrid blood would even let her.

The wolf lunged again.

Maya threw up her hands—

And something *happened*.

The air around her rippled, turned cold. Shadows gathered at her feet, twisting upward like smoke. The wolf stopped mid-lunge, its eyes going wide, its body frozen in place.

It hung there, suspended, as if time itself had stopped.

Maya stared. Her hands were wreathed in darkness, not solid, not quite shadow, but something *other*. She could feel it—a connection to the night, to the spaces between light, to the hidden places.

She dropped her hands.

The wolf crashed to the ground, scrambled up, and ran. It vanished into the trees, whimpering.

Maya stood alone in the forest, her hands still tingling, her heart hammering. The book lay open at her feet. She knelt, picked it up, and read the new words forming on the page:

*The Shadow Wolf's gift: dominion over darkness. The space between moments. The power the Council fears most.*

She heard footsteps.

Kael emerged from the trees, human again, his clothes torn, blood on his lip. He stopped when he saw her face, saw the book in her hands, saw the shadows still clinging to her fingers.

"What did you do?" His voice was hoarse.

"I don't know." Maya looked at her hands. The shadows were fading, retreating back into the night. "But I think I just found out why they want me dead."

---

They didn't go back to the dorms.

Kael led her to a small cabin at the edge of the academy grounds—abandoned, dusty, but secure. He lit a kerosene lamp, and the yellow light pushed back the darkness.

Maya sat on a worn couch, the book in her lap. She didn't want to open it again. She was afraid of what she'd see.

Kael paced. "That was Shadow magic. Purebloods don't have it. Regular wolves don't have it. It's extinct. Has been for centuries."

"Apparently not." Maya finally opened the book. The pages were still changing, still writing themselves. "It says I have 'dominion over darkness.' Whatever that means."

"It means the Council will stop at nothing to control you. Or kill you." Kael ran a hand through his hair, frustration bleeding through his usual calm. "Why didn't you tell me you could do that?"

"I didn't know I could. I've never shifted. Never done anything except hide."

"Hide." Kael stopped pacing. He looked at her, really looked. "You've been hiding your whole life, haven't you?"

Maya didn't answer. She didn't have to.

"Listen to me." He crouched in front of her, his eyes serious. "What you did tonight—that's not something you learn. It's something you *are*. And if the Council knows, they'll come for you with everything they have."

"Then I'll fight."

"You can't fight the Council. Not alone."

"Then help me." She met his gaze. "You know more than you're telling me. About the book. About the academy. About what I am."

Kael was silent for a long moment. Then he reached into his pocket and pulled out a small silver coin, worn and old. He pressed it into her palm.

"This was my mother's. She was like you."

Maya's breath caught. "She was a hybrid?"

"She was the last one before you. And she died because the Council found her before she was ready." Kael's voice cracked, barely. "I've been waiting for someone else like her. Someone the book would choose."

"The book chose me?"

"It's called the Codex of Unwritten Paths. It records the history of wolves who don't fit the mold. It's been dormant for thirty years." He pointed at the page. "Until tonight."

Maya looked down at the book. The ink was still shifting, still writing. New words formed:

*Kael Ashford. Son of the last Shadow Wolf. Keeper of the Codex. Bound by blood to protect what his mother died for.*

"Your mother." Maya's throat tightened. "She had the book."

"She had everything. Power, vision, hope. And they took it all." Kael stood, turned away. "I've been hiding too, Maya. Hiding what I know. Hiding who I am. Waiting for the right moment."

"Is this the right moment?"

He turned back, and there was something new in his eyes—not just determination, but *fear*. "I don't know. But we don't have a choice anymore."

A knock at the cabin door made them both freeze.

Maya's wolf surged. The shadows around her flickered, responded to her panic. She clutched the book, ready to run, ready to fight.

The door opened.

Professor Thorne stepped inside. His silver eyes swept the room, landed on Maya, lingered on the book. His expression was unreadable.

"Maya Chen." His voice was calm. Too calm. "The Headmaster requests your presence. Immediately."

Kael stepped in front of her. "She's not going anywhere with you."

"Mr. Ashford." Thorne's tone hardened. "You're already on thin ice. Don't make it worse."

"The Council—"

"The Council has no jurisdiction here. Not yet." Thorne's gaze shifted to Maya. "The Headmaster wants to offer you protection. Before things escalate."

"Protection?" Maya stood, the book pressed against her chest. "Those wolves weren't here to protect me."
