"Those wolves were rogue. Acting without authority." Thorne's eyes narrowed. "Do you know what happens to rogues who touch a student of Silver Fang Academy?"

"Enlighten me."

"They disappear."

The word hung in the air, heavy and cold.

Maya looked at Kael. He gave a small, almost imperceptible shake of his head. Don't trust him.

But what choice did she have?

---

The Headmaster's office was nothing like she expected.

No dark wood, no ancient tomes, no firelight. It was modern, sterile, white walls and glass desks. Technology hummed quietly. Screens displayed maps of the academy grounds, live feeds of the forest perimeter.

Headmaster Aldric Vane sat behind his desk, hands folded, expression patient. He was an older wolf, gray at the temples, but his eyes were sharp. Calculating.

"Maya." He gestured to a chair. "Please, sit."

She didn't sit. Kael stood beside her, tense. Professor Thorne closed the door and remained by it, a silent sentinel.

"I understand you had an eventful evening." Vane's voice was smooth, practiced. "Three wolves attacked you. A book responded to your touch. You discovered abilities you didn't know you had."

"How do you know all that?"

"I know everything that happens on my grounds." He leaned back. "I also know that the Council has placed a bounty on your head. Alive or dead."

Kael stiffened. "A bounty?"

"Word travels fast among the old families. A hybrid with Shadow abilities? That's a threat to their power structure." Vane's eyes locked onto Maya's. "But I can protect you."

"Why would you do that?"

"Because I don't agree with the Council." His voice lost its smoothness, gained an edge. "I've spent twenty years trying to change their policies. Trying to make this academy a place for *all* wolves, not just purebloods. You're proof that change is possible."

"Or you want to use me," Maya said flatly.

Vane smiled. It didn't reach his eyes. "Smart girl. Yes, I want to use you. I want to train you. I want to show the world what a hybrid can become." He leaned forward. "But I also want to keep you alive. Those two things aren't mutually exclusive."

Maya's hands tightened on the book. The shadows at her feet stirred, responding to her doubt.

"What about my mom?" she asked. "She's human. She doesn't know where I am. She doesn't know what I am."

"She's safe. I've made sure of it." Vane slid a photograph across the desk. Maya's mother, sitting in their kitchen, drinking tea. Unaware she was being watched.

The shadows flared.

"Touch her," Maya said, her voice dropping, "and I'll burn this academy to the ground."

Vane's smile widened. "Good. You have fire. You'll need it."

He stood, walked to the window, looked out at the moonlit campus. "The Council will send more wolves. They won't stop. But I can give you time. Training. Allies."

"In exchange for what?"

"In exchange for your trust." He turned. "And when the time comes, your loyalty."

Kael grabbed Maya's arm. "Don't."

But Maya was already thinking. She had no family here. No allies. No power she understood. The Headmaster was offering a deal—and deals with wolves always had teeth.

But what was the alternative?

Running forever?

She looked down at the book. The ink had settled. A new line had formed:

*The Headmaster knows more than he says. He carries a secret that could destroy him. Trust him, but watch him.*

Maya looked up. Met Vane's eyes.

"I'll consider your offer."

Vane nodded. "That's all I ask. Professor Thorne will escort you to your new quarters. You'll be moved to the senior dormitory. Private room. Security detail."

"I don't want a security detail."

"You don't have a choice."

Thorne opened the door. Maya glanced at Kael. His jaw was tight, his eyes troubled. He wanted to say something, but not here.

As she walked past him, he pressed something into her hand

title: Chapter 9
content:
CHAPTER TITLE: The Hunter's Snare

The book burned against Maya's chest. Not with heat, but with something else—a vibration that hummed through her ribs and into her bones. The three wolves stood motionless, their amber eyes fixed on her like she was prey that had already been caught.

"You have five seconds to explain why I should go anywhere with you," Maya said, surprised at how steady her voice came out.

The wolf in the center shifted. His body twisted, fur receding, bones cracking and reforming until a man stood in his place. Tall. Gray at the temples. A scar that cut from his left eye to his jaw. He wore a dark suit that looked completely wrong in the middle of the woods behind a school dormitory.

"I am Councilor Vance." He didn't smile. "You've been in possession of a restricted text for approximately twelve minutes. That text contains information that threatens the security of every pack under the Council's protection."

"It's a book."

"It's a weapon." Vance stepped closer. His bare feet made no sound on the grass. "One that should have been destroyed three hundred years ago. One that your mother should have burned before you were born."

Maya's grip on the book tightened. "You knew my mother?"

"I knew of her." His voice carried something that might have been disgust. "She was a fool who thought love could transcend the natural order. She proved what happens to those who try."

"Don't talk about her."

"She died alone, didn't she?" Vance's eyes glittered. "Abandoned by her pack. Hunted by her own kind. All because she couldn't accept that some barriers aren't meant to be crossed."

Maya's wolf surged. She felt it—the silver burning behind her eyes, the shift threatening to tear through her skin. The other two wolves growled, muscles tensing.

"Easy," Vance said, raising a hand. "I didn't come here to fight you, hybrid. I came here to offer you a choice."

"Some choice."

"An honest one." He reached into his jacket and pulled out a silver amulet. It caught the moonlight, casting strange shadows that seemed to move on their own. "The Council has watched you since birth. We knew you'd eventually manifest. We knew you'd find your way here. What we didn't know was whether you'd become a threat or an asset."

"And what did you decide?"

"That depends on you." He tossed the amulet at her feet. It landed in the grass, the chain coiling like a snake. "Wear that. It will suppress your wolf completely. You'll live as a human. Graduate. Leave. And the Council will forget you exist."

"Or?"

"Or we take the book by force. We lock you in a containment facility. And we study exactly how a half-breed managed to survive when every other hybrid experiment in history has died before their first shift."

Maya's blood ran cold. "Experiments?"

Vance's smile was thin and cruel. "You didn't think you were an accident, did you? Your mother was part of a program. A breeding initiative designed to create a new kind of soldier. She ruined everything when she fell in love with your father and ran."

The words hit like a physical blow. Maya's knees went weak, but she refused to fall. "I don't believe you."

"Check the book." He nodded at the text in her arms. "Page forty-seven. The original research notes are written in your mother's handwriting."

Maya's hands shook as she opened the book. The pages fluttered, stopping at exactly the right spot. She recognized the script immediately—the same looping cursive she'd found in her mother's journals after she died.

*Subject 7: Gestation proceeding normally. The hybrid embryo shows no signs of rejection. If this succeeds, we will have proven that the bloodlines can merge without fatal consequences. I've named her Maya. She deserves a name before they take her from me.*

Below it, in different ink, someone had added: *Subject escaped with infant. Tracking beacon destroyed. All remaining data classified.*

Maya looked up. Her eyes were wet, but she wouldn't let the tears fall. "You were going to take me."

"You were never meant to be a person, Maya." Vance's voice was almost gentle. "You were meant to be a weapon. Your mother's betrayal cost the Council fifteen years of research. But now you're here. And you can still serve a purpose."

"By wearing a collar?"

"It's not a collar. It's a mercy." He gestured to the amulet. "Wear it, and you're free. Refuse, and you become property of the Council. Either way, that book comes with me."

Maya looked down at the amulet. It pulsed with a faint, sickly light. She could feel what it would do—the cold metal against her skin, the slow suffocation of her wolf, the silence where her instincts had always lived.

She thought of her mother's journals. The pages filled with love and fear and hope, all leading to this moment. Her mother hadn't died to give Maya a cage. She'd died to give her a choice.

"I'm not wearing it."

Vance's expression didn't change. "That's unfortunate."

He snapped his fingers. The two wolves lunged.

Maya didn't think. She moved. Her body twisted, bones shifting, fur breaking through skin. The transformation that had always been painful, awkward, wrong—suddenly felt natural. Her wolf form exploded out of her, silver eyes blazing, and she met the first wolf mid-air.

They crashed to the ground, fangs and claws. He was bigger. Stronger. His jaws snapped at her throat, and she barely twisted away, feeling teeth graze her fur. She kicked out, catching him in the ribs, and he yelped but didn't release her.

The second wolf circled, waiting for an opening.

Vance watched, arms crossed. "Impressive shift. Faster than any pureblood. But speed isn't everything."

The first wolf pinned her. His weight pressed down, crushing, and she couldn't breathe. She thrashed, clawing at his belly, but he was too heavy, too experienced. His jaws closed around her neck.

Not hard enough to kill. Hard enough to hold.

"Last chance," Vance said. "The amulet, or the cage."

Maya couldn't speak in wolf form. But she could think. She could remember the drawings in the book—the Shadow Wolf, half in shadow, half in light. The text said she was meant to bridge two worlds.

But worlds didn't bridge themselves. They had to be broken open.

She stopped fighting. Went limp. The wolf above her loosened his grip, confused.

That was the opening she needed.

Maya twisted, not away from him but into him, her claws raking across his muzzle, her teeth finding his throat. She didn't bite down—just held, a warning. He froze.

"Don't," she growled, the word coming out as a guttural snarl that shouldn't have been possible in wolf form.

Vance's eyes widened. "That's not—"

"I said no."

The second wolf charged. Maya released the first and spun, meeting him head-on. This time, she didn't try to overpower him. She flowed around him, using his momentum against him, sending him crashing into a tree. He slumped, unconscious.

She turned to face Vance.

The Councilor hadn't moved. But his hand was raised, and in it, he held a small silver device. "I was hoping it wouldn't come to this."

He pressed a button.

The amulet at Maya's feet exploded with light. Pain ripped through her—not physical, but something deeper. Her wolf was being torn away, ripped out of her by invisible hooks. She collapsed, her body convulsing, her shift reversing against her will.

She was human again. Naked. Shivering. Helpless.

"It's not a collar," Vance said, picking up the amulet. "It's a trigger. And the real device was implanted in you eighteen years ago, when you were still in your mother's womb."

Maya couldn't speak. Her throat was raw, her voice gone.

"The Council doesn't leave loose ends, Maya." He knelt beside her, holding the amulet where she could see it. "Your mother thought she could remove the tracker. She was wrong. It's bonded to your nervous system. As long as I hold this, I control your wolf."

He stood, pocketing the amulet. "I'll give you tonight to reconsider. Tomorrow, you'll make your choice."

He turned and walked away, the two wolves following—the first limping, the second still unconscious, dragged by his scruff.

Maya lay in the grass, staring at the stars, unable to move.

The book lay beside her, open to the last page.

New words had appeared, glowing faintly silver:

*The hunter has the snare. But the wolf has the teeth. Trust the night, Maya. It remembers what the Council has forgotten.*

She wanted to laugh. Trust the night. What was that supposed to mean?

But as the cold seeped into her bones and the stars blurred above her, she heard something. No—felt something. A pulse. Deep underground. Below the school. Below everything.

The pack. The rumor of a pack that existed outside the Council's reach.

They were calling to her.

And tomorrow, when she faced Vance again, she would have to decide if she could trust a call she couldn't see, from a pack she couldn't find, to fight an enemy that had owned her since before she took her first breath.

A twig snapped behind her.

Maya didn't have the strength to turn.

"Kael," she whispered, though she wasn't sure it was him. "Is that you?"

A shadow fell over her.

Not Kael.

A woman's voice, low and ancient, like wind through a cave.

"No. But I've been waiting for you to ask for help."

title: The Blood Price
content:
Maya's wolf surged beneath her skin, demanding release. But she'd spent eighteen years learning to cage it, and that discipline saved her now.

"Who wants to know?" She kept her voice steady, even as her heart hammered against her ribs.

The lead wolf stepped closer. Moonlight caught the silver streak in his dark fur—a Council mark. She'd read about those in the book's forbidden pages. Elders who served directly under the Alpha Council wore them as brands of allegiance.

"You know who we are." His voice rumbled like distant thunder. "The real question is what you are."

The other two wolves circled, cutting off escape routes. Maya calculated distances, angles, exits. The dorm was forty feet away. The forest edge, twenty. But wolves could cover that in seconds.

"The Council doesn't send three enforcers for a first-year student," she said. "Unless they're scared of something."

The lead wolf's lip curled, exposing fangs. "We don't fear hybrids. We eliminate them before they become threats."

"Then why haven't you attacked?" Maya's eyes flicked to the book in her hands. Still warm. Still pulsing. "Because you're not sure what I am either."

A sound behind her. Not from the wolves. From the trees.

The lead wolf's ears swiveled. His companions tensed.

Maya didn't look. She kept her focus on the three Council wolves, but she felt whatever was approaching—a pressure in the air, a shift in the moonlight that made shadows deepen.

"You're out of time," the lead wolf snarled. "Take her."

They moved as one, three streaks of amber-eyed fury.

Maya dropped and rolled, the book pressed against her chest. She felt claws tear through her jacket, scoring her shoulder. Pain flared, hot and immediate, but she was already moving, using the momentum to spring to her feet.

The second wolf lunged. She sidestepped, barely, and slammed the book into its snout. It yelped, recoiling, and she kicked out, catching it under the jaw.

Three against one. Human form. No weapons. No backup.

The odds were impossible.

Then the shadows behind her exploded.

Something massive and dark burst from the tree line—a wolf so black it seemed to absorb light, its eyes burning violet rather than gold or amber. It hit the lead Council wolf mid-leap, carrying him to the ground in a tangle of fur and snarls.

Maya scrambled back, watching in stunned silence as the black wolf tore into the Council enforcer with brutal efficiency. Not killing—disabling. Precision strikes to tendons and joints.

The other two wolves hesitated, clearly recognizing the newcomer.

The black wolf lifted its head, blood dripping from its fangs. Its violet eyes fixed on the remaining enforcers, and something passed between them—a communication Maya couldn't hear but could feel, like pressure in her skull.

The Council wolves backed away, dragging their injured leader.

"We'll be watching, hybrid," one of them spat before they vanished into the trees.

The black wolf turned to Maya.

Heart pounding, she held her ground. "Are you going to kill me too?"

The wolf's form rippled, shifted, condensed—and in its place stood a girl about Maya's age, with dark skin, close-cropped hair, and eyes that still glowed faintly violet even as they faded to brown.

"Not tonight," the girl said. Her voice was rough, like she didn't use it often. "But you need to come with me. Now."

"And if I refuse?"

The girl's expression didn't change. "Then the Council enforcers I just chased off will come back with fifty more. Your choice."

Maya looked down at the book. The words had shifted again: *The Shadow Wolf's first ally. Found in darkness. Bound by blood.*

She looked up. "Who are you?"

"Name's Rook. I'm your roommate."

"My what?"

Rook's lips twitched—almost a smile. "Administration reassigned me this morning. Heard you needed someone who knows how to handle Council wolves." She jerked her head toward the dorms. "We can trade sob stories later. Right now, we need to get that book somewhere safe."

Maya hesitated. Every instinct screamed at her not to trust anyone. Not after Kael's betrayal. Not after the Council's attack.

But Rook had saved her life. And there was something in those violet eyes—something Maya recognized.

Loneliness. The kind that came from being different in a world that demanded sameness.

"Fine." Maya tucked the book under her arm. "But if this is a trap—"

"It's not." Rook started walking, her gait fluid and predatory. "Though I should warn you. Your boyfriend's been looking for you."

"Kael's not my—" Maya stopped. "Wait. He's looking for me?"

"He tore through the dorms about ten minutes ago, demanding to know where you were. Caused quite a scene." Rook glanced back. "Seemed genuinely worried."

Maya's chest tightened. "He's the one who told me to run."

"Doesn't mean he wanted you to disappear." Rook pushed open a side door to the dorms—one Maya had never noticed, hidden behind overgrown ivy. "He's been trying to reach you through the pack bond. Figured you were blocking him."

Maya hadn't been blocking anything. She hadn't felt a pack bond at all.

But Rook was already moving through a narrow hallway, her footsteps sure, as if she'd lived in this building for years. They passed door after door, descending stairs that Maya hadn't seen on any floor plan, until they reached a basement level that shouldn't have existed.

"This is a safe room," Rook said, pushing open a heavy steel door. "Warded against Council magic. Soundproofed. We can talk here."

The room was small—a desk, two chairs, a cot in the corner. Shelves lined the walls, filled with books and scrolls and objects Maya couldn't identify.

She set the book on the desk. It had stopped pulsing, but the ink still shifted faintly, as if watching them.

"How do you know about this place?" Maya asked.

"Because my mother was a Shadow Wolf too."

Maya's blood went cold. "Was?"

Rook's jaw tightened. "She died protecting the last one before you. Twenty years ago. The Council found her, and she made sure they never found what she was protecting." She met Maya's eyes. "My mother was your mother's protector."

"My mother never mentioned—"

"Because she didn't know." Rook sat down heavily, suddenly looking exhausted. "The Shadow Wolves operate in cells. One protector per hybrid. My mother died before she could pass the information to yours. I only found out because I found her journals after she was gone."

Maya sank into the other chair. Her mind raced, trying to piece together a puzzle she hadn't known existed. "You're telling me my mother had a guardian. And that guardian was your mother. And neither of us knew until—"

"Until you walked into that library and triggered a blood-tracking spell that woke every Council operative within a hundred miles." Rook leaned forward. "That book chose you, Maya. It's been waiting for three centuries for someone with your blood to open it. And now that you have, the Council knows exactly what you are."

"Which is?"

Rook's expression was grim. "A threat to everything they've built. A bridge between two worlds that shouldn't connect. A weapon." She paused. "Or a savior. Depends on who you ask."

Maya stared at the book. At the shifting ink that seemed to form words just beyond comprehension. At the image of herself, half-shadow, half-light.

"I didn't ask for this."

"No one does." Rook's voice softened. "But you're here now. And you have a choice. You can run, try to disappear, live your life hiding what you are. Or you can fight."

"Fight the Council? I'm one person. One hybrid who can barely shift."

"You're not one person." Rook reached into her jacket and pulled out a folded piece of paper, yellowed and cracked with age. She slid it across the desk. "My mother's last message. She said to give it to the next Shadow Wolf when they appeared."

Maya unfolded it. The handwriting was elegant, precise, and the words made her breath catch:

*To the one who follows—*

*You are not alone. We have been waiting. We have been preparing. When you read this, find the seven marked by the crescent moon. They will know you. They will protect you. They will help you do what must be done.*

*The Council fears what you can become. Show them why.*

*—Elara, Shadow Wolf of the Third Line*

Maya looked up, questions burning on her lips.

But before she could speak, the steel door exploded inward.

Kael stood in the doorway, his chest heaving, his eyes wild. Behind him, the hallway was filled with smoke and the sound of alarms.

"Council raid," he gasped. "They're burning the library. They know about the book."

Maya grabbed the book, shoving it into her bag. "How did they find out so fast?"

"Because someone told them." Kael's gaze shifted to Rook. "The question is who."

Rook's hand went to her throat, where a crescent moon tattoo peeked above her collar. "It wasn't me."

"Then we've got a traitor." Kael stepped into the room, his jaw tight. "And we've got about two minutes before they find this place."

Maya looked between them—the boy who'd pushed her into a dark passage, the girl who'd saved her from Council wolves. Two strangers. Two allies. Two questions she couldn't answer.

She looked down at her bag, where the book pulsed like a second heartbeat.

"I need to find the seven," she said.

Kael and Rook exchanged a look she couldn't read.

"There's a problem with that," Rook said quietly. "My mother's list is thirty years old. Most of those people are dead."

Maya's heart dropped. "Then what do we do?"

Rook's violet eyes met hers. "We find their descendants. And hope they inherited more than just blood."

The alarms grew louder. Footsteps thundered in the hallway above.

Kael grabbed Maya's hand. "Later. Right now, we run."

They ran.

But as they burst through a hidden exit into the moonlit forest, Maya couldn't shake the feeling that they weren't escaping.

They were walking into a trap that had been set thirty years ago.

And the only question was who would spring it.

title: The Council's Game
content:
Maya's muscles locked. The book pressed against her ribs like a second heartbeat, warm and pulsing. The three wolves stood in a loose semicircle, cutting off every escape route through the trees.

The one who'd spoken stepped forward. He was tall for a wolf, even in human form, with gray-streaked hair and eyes that had seen too many moons. His face held no cruelty—just cold, clinical purpose.

"You know who I am," he said. It wasn't a question.

"Elder Marcus." The name came out steadier than Maya felt. She'd seen his picture in the academy's welcome materials, listed under "Board of Directors." She'd assumed that was a formality. She'd assumed a lot of things.

"You've been busy, hybrid." He nodded at the book in her arms. "That doesn't belong to you."

"It belongs to the library."

"It belongs to us." He took another step. "Give it back, and we'll forget this happened."

Maya's grip tightened. The book's warmth spread through her fingers, up her arms, settling somewhere deep in her chest. It felt like recognition. Like home.

"I don't think you'll forget anything," she said. "I think you've been watching me since I stepped through the gates. I think you knew exactly where I'd go tonight."

Marcus's eyes flickered. Just for a second. But Maya caught it.

"You're smarter than they said," he admitted. "That's unfortunate. Stupid hybrids are easier to control."

"Try me."

The two wolves behind him shifted, their bones cracking, their bodies expanding. Fur rippled across their skin. Teeth lengthened. In seconds, two massive wolves stood where men had been, their growls vibrating through the ground.

Maya's own wolf stirred. Not with fear. With hunger.

"Last chance," Marcus said. "The book. Your cooperation. Or we do this the hard way."

"Define 'the hard way.'"

He smiled. It was not a kind smile.

"The Council doesn't want you dead, Maya. That would be too simple. Too clean. They want you broken. They want to show every hybrid, every half-breed, every abomination that thinks it can rise above its place—they want to show them what happens when they try."

Maya's blood burned. Her wolf clawed at her insides, demanding release.

"Then come get me."

She threw the book.

Not at Marcus. Into the trees behind her. It arced through the moonlight, pages fluttering, and disappeared into the darkness. The two wolves lunged after it. Marcus's face twisted with fury.

"You stupid—"

Maya moved.

She didn't shift. Didn't need to. She drove her shoulder into his chest, felt him stagger, heard the air leave his lungs. She grabbed his collar and pulled him down, using his momentum to roll past him, coming up on her feet.

The book. She needed the book.

She sprinted into the trees, branches whipping at her face. Her night vision sharpened without her willing it, the world bleeding into shades of gray and silver. She could hear the wolves behind her, crashing through the underbrush, gaining.

The book lay in a clearing ahead, its pages open to the moonlight, glowing faintly.

Maya dove for it.

Her fingers touched the cover just as something massive hit her from the side. She rolled, claws raking her shoulder, pain exploding through her arm. She bit back a scream and swung the book like a weapon, catching the wolf across the muzzle.

It yelped and staggered back.

She scrambled to her feet, blood soaking her sleeve, the book clutched in her good hand. The wolf shook its head and snarled. Behind it, the second wolf emerged from the trees. Behind them both, Marcus walked slowly, deliberately, his hands in his pockets.

"You're making this worse," he said.

"Funny. I was about to say the same thing."

"You're bleeding. You're outnumbered. The book is spelled to your bloodline, which means every Elder in a hundred miles felt the moment you touched it. There is nowhere you can run."

Maya looked at the book. The drawing of her was still there, her silver eyes glowing, the shadow wolf behind her. The words beneath had changed again.

*The hunt is a game. The game has rules. Break them.*

She looked up at Marcus.

"Who wrote this book?"

His face went still. For the first time, something like unease crossed his features.

"That's not your concern."

"It's my blood in the pages. I think that makes it my concern."

"You're a child playing with fire—"

"And you're an Elder who's afraid of a book." She smiled, cold and sharp. "What's in here, Marcus? What don't you want me to see?"

He didn't answer. But his hands came out of his pockets, and she saw the silver glint of a blade.

"We're done talking."

He moved faster than she expected. The knife came at her throat. She twisted, the blade grazing her skin, and brought the book up to block his next strike. The cover split under the impact, pages scattering like wounded birds.

Maya screamed.

Not in pain. In fury.

The wolf inside her tore free.

She didn't remember shifting. One moment she was human, bleeding, desperate. The next she was fur and fangs and rage. Her body felt different—wrong and right at once, like she'd been wearing a skin that didn't fit and now she was finally herself.

Marcus's eyes went wide.

The other two wolves backed away, whining.

Maya looked down at her paws. At her fur, which wasn't brown or gray or black. It was silver. Silver like the moon, like the eyes in the drawing, like the blood that pulsed through her veins.

She was the wolf from the page.

She was the Shadow Wolf.

And she was done running.

She lunged.

Marcus tried to meet her, his knife raised, but she was faster. Stronger. She knocked the blade from his hand and pinned him to the ground, her jaws inches from his throat. He smelled of old magic and older fear.

"Kill me," he rasped. "Go ahead. Prove them right. Prove you're nothing but a monster."

Maya's jaws trembled. Her wolf wanted to bite. Wanted to taste his blood. Wanted to show him what a monster really looked like.

But the book's words echoed in her mind.

*The game has rules. Break them.*

She pulled back.

She shifted.

It hurt more than the first time, bones snapping back into human shape, skin stretching over muscle. She knelt over him, human again, her hands shaking, her voice raw.

"I'm not a monster."

Marcus stared up at her. His face held no gratitude. Only calculation.

"No," he said quietly. "You're something worse. You're a threat."

He moved so fast she didn't see it. Something cold clamped around her wrist. Silver. It burned, and she screamed, the pain dropping her to her knees.

A collar. He'd put a silver collar on her.

The other wolves were on her before she could recover. Paws on her back. Teeth at her neck. She felt her wolf retreating, cowering from the silver's burn.

Marcus stood, brushed off his clothes, and looked down at her with cold satisfaction.

"The Council sends its regards, hybrid. They've been waiting for you to show your true colors."

He leaned down, close to her ear.

"Welcome to the game."

Darkness closed in. The last thing Maya saw was the ruined book, its pages scattered across the clearing like fallen leaves.

And on one page, visible in the moonlight, new words forming:

*The cage is not the end. The cage is the beginning. Remember who you are, Maya Chen.*

*The shadows remember.*

*So will you.*

title: The Blood We Carry
content:
The three wolves didn't move. They didn't need to. Their presence alone was a cage, their amber eyes pinning Maya in place like prey caught in torchlight.

She clutched the book tighter. The pages hummed against her skin, a low vibration that traveled up her arms and settled behind her ribs. The words from the last page still burned in her mind: *The hunt begins.*

"I don't know who you are," she said, forcing her voice steady. "But you've got the wrong person."

The wolf in the center tilted his head. He was massive—easily the size of a small car, his fur the color of old ash, his muzzle streaked with gray. Old. Powerful. The kind of wolf who'd survived decades by being smarter and meaner than everyone else.

"You carry the book," he said. His voice didn't come from his wolf throat. It resonated in Maya's skull, a direct psychic push. "You carry the blood. There is no mistake."

"I found it in the library. That's all."

"You were chosen by it." He stepped forward, and the other two moved with him, a synchronized shift that cut off her escape routes. "The Grimoire of the Veil does not choose lightly. It hasn't chosen anyone in seventy-three years."

Maya's heart slammed against her ribs. *Seventy-three years.* That was before the hybrid purge. Before the Council declared half-breeds an abomination. Before her mother fled into the human world and never looked back.

"Then tell me why it chose me," she said. "Because I sure as hell don't know."

The old wolf's eyes flickered. Something like surprise crossed his ancient face. "You truly don't know."

"I'm a half-breed who spent eighteen years hiding what I am. The only thing I know is that everyone in this school wants me dead or expelled. So if you're here to kill me, get it over with. I've had a long night."

The wolf let out a sound that might have been a laugh. It came out like stones grinding together. "Brave. Stupid, but brave."

"The two aren't mutually exclusive."

Another laugh. Then the wolf began to shift. It was slow, deliberate—not the violent snap and crack of a forced change, but a smooth, controlled transition that spoke of centuries of practice. Fur receded. Bones reshaped. In ten seconds, a naked man stood before her, gray-haired and scarred, his eyes still glowing amber in the moonlight.

He didn't seem to care about his nudity. Wolves never did.

"I am Eldric," he said. "Council Elder. Keeper of the Old Records. And I am not here to kill you, Maya Chen. I am here to warn you."

"Warn me about what?"

"The book in your hands is not a weapon. It is a key." He stepped closer, and Maya forced herself not to back away. "The Council has spent three centuries burying the truth about hybrids. About the Shadow Wolves. About what really happened during the Great Purge."

"I read about the Purge. It was a war. Purebloods versus hybrids. The hybrids lost."

Eldric's lips curved into something that wasn't quite a smile. "That's what the history books say. The history books written by the victors."

A chill crawled down Maya's spine. "What really happened?"

He reached into a pouch hanging around his neck—she hadn't noticed it before, hidden in the thick fur of his wolf form—and pulled out a small, flat stone. It was black, polished smooth, with symbols carved into its surface that seemed to move when she looked directly at them.

"The Grimoire chose you because you are the last of the Shadow Wolf bloodline. The only one who can open the Veil."

"The Veil? What Veil?"

Eldric's expression hardened. "The Veil between worlds. The barrier that separates the wolf realm from the human realm. The barrier that the Council has used to trap every hybrid soul for the past three hundred years."

Maya's stomach dropped. "Trap?"

"Hybrids weren't killed in the Great Purge, Maya. They were imprisoned. Every half-breed, every Shadow Wolf, every wolf who refused to bow to the pureblood Council—their souls were torn from their bodies and sealed behind the Veil. They've been screaming in the darkness for three centuries. And you are the only one who can free them."

The book burned hotter in her hands. The pages rustled, as if stirred by a wind she couldn't feel.

"Why me?" she whispered.

"Because you were born from both worlds. A true hybrid. A bridge." Eldric's eyes bore into hers. "The Council fears you because you can tear down everything they've built. The purebloods hate you because you're proof that their bloodlines aren't sacred. The professors doubt you because they've been trained for generations to see your kind as lesser."

He stepped closer, close enough that she could smell the forest on his skin, the age in his bones.

"But the hybrids—the ones trapped behind the Veil—they don't fear you. They don't hate you. They've been waiting for you."

Maya's mind raced. Three hundred years of trapped souls. A Council that had lied about everything. A book that had chosen her before she even knew it existed.

"I'm just a student," she said. "I can barely control my own wolf. How am I supposed to free anyone?"

Eldric's hand shot out and grabbed her wrist. His grip was iron. "The Veil doesn't require strength. It requires blood. Yours."

A cold dread settled in her bones. "You want me to die."

"No." His voice dropped, urgent. "I want you to *live*. The Council doesn't. They've already sent hunters to this school. The three of us are the only Elders who oppose them. We've been watching you, protecting you from the shadows. But we can't protect you forever."

"Then what do you want me to do?"

"Run."

The word hung in the air, sharp and final.

"I can't run. I just got here. I'm supposed to—"

"To what? Prove yourself? Graduate? Become a respected member of a society that will never accept you?" Eldric's grip tightened. "The moment the Council confirms you're the Shadow Wolf, they will execute you. Publicly. As a warning to any other hybrids who might rise."

Maya pulled her arm free. "Then I'll fight."

"You can't fight the entire Council."

"Watch me."

Eldric stared at her for a long moment. Then he sighed, a sound heavy with centuries of regret. "You have your mother's fire."

Maya's breath caught. "You knew my mother?"

"Everyone knew Li Chen. She was the last Shadow Wolf before you. The only hybrid the Council ever feared." His eyes softened. "She didn't run either. And they killed her for it."

The words hit Maya like a physical blow. Her mother had died when she was six. Car accident, they'd said. But Maya had always known there was more to the story. The way her father never talked about it. The way he flinched whenever she asked.

"Car accident," Maya said, her voice hollow.

"There are no accidents, Maya. Only Council orders."

The book trembled in her hands. The pages flipped on their own, stopping at a section she hadn't seen before. A map. Intricate, ancient, pulsing with faint light. Lines connected dots across the continent, forming a pattern she didn't understand.

"The map shows the locations of the Veil anchors," Eldric said. "Seven sites. Seven seals. Break all seven, and the Veil falls. The hybrids are freed."

"Break them how?"

"The same way the Grimoire chose you. Blood and will." He reached into his pouch again and pulled out a silver ring. It was simple, unadorned, but it glowed with the same light as the book. "This belonged to your mother. She wanted you to have it when you were ready."

Maya took the ring. It was warm, alive. When she slipped it onto her finger, it resized to fit perfectly. A surge of energy shot through her—not painful, but awakening. Like something inside her had been sleeping and was finally stirring.

"I can feel her," Maya whispered.

"She's with you. She always has been." Eldric stepped back. "Now go. The hunters won't wait long. I've bought you time, but not much."

"Come with me."

"I can't. I have to return to the Council, pretend I don't know anything. It's the only way I can keep feeding you information."

"And if they find out?"

"Then I'll join your mother in the Veil." He smiled, and for a moment he looked almost young. "It would be an honor."

Maya wanted to argue. She wanted to demand more answers, more time, more anything. But the book was vibrating now, and the ring on her finger was pulsing, and she could feel something pulling at her chest—a thread connected to the map, to the seven anchors, to the souls waiting in the darkness.

"One more thing," Eldric said. "Don't trust Kael Silvermane."

Maya's head snapped up. "What?"

"He's pureblood. His family has served the Council for generations. Everything he's told you, everything he's done—it could be a trap."

"Kael saved my life. Multiple times."

"That's exactly what a well-trained spy would do."

Maya's jaw tightened. "You don't know him."

"I know his father. And his father's father. The Silvermane line has never crossed the Council. Not once."

"He's not like them."

Eldric's eyes held hers. "Are you willing to bet your life on that? And the lives of every hybrid behind the Veil?"

Maya didn't answer. Because she didn't know.

And that uncertainty was worse than any threat the Council could make.

A howl split the night. Close. Too close.

"They're here," Eldric said, his body already beginning to shift back to wolf form. "Run, Maya. Run and don't look back."

She ran.

The forest swallowed her, branches clawing at her face, roots trying to trip her. She held the book and her mother's ring and the weight of three centuries of trapped souls. She ran until her lungs burned, until her legs gave out, until she collapsed at the edge of a frozen stream.

She looked at the ring. She looked at the book.

She thought of Kael. Of his silver eyes. Of the way he'd pushed her into that passage without hesitation.

*Don't trust him.*

But she remembered the way he'd looked at her. The way his voice had cracked when he said *trust me.*

She couldn't decide if that made her smart or stupid.

The howls grew closer.

Maya forced herself up, shoved the book into her bag, and kept running into the dark.

Behind her, the moon watched. Silver and cold.

And somewhere in the distance, a wolf that wasn't quite a wolf howled back.

title: Chapter 13
content:
CHAPTER 13: The Blood in Her Veins

The wolves didn't move. They didn't need to. Their presence alone was a cage, three sets of amber eyes pinning Maya in place like prey.

She'd been stupid. She'd run straight into their trap without even thinking. The book had warned her. *The hunt begins.* And she'd walked right into it.

"Put the book down," the lead wolf said. His voice was rough, like he didn't bother with human speech often enough to keep it smooth. "Nice and slow."

Maya's fingers tightened on the spine. "Who sent you?"

"You know who."

"The Council."

He didn't confirm it. He didn't have to. The way his jaw tightened said everything.

She'd read about Council enforcers in the history texts that weren't sealed away in forbidden sections. Purebloods who served as hunters, trackers, executioners. They didn't exist in official records. Every mention was a footnote, a rumor, a warning passed down through generations.

They existed now. Right in front of her.

"The book," he repeated. "Last time."

Maya's mind raced. She couldn't fight them. She was strong, faster than most purebloods her age, trained by Kael for weeks now. But these weren't students. These were killers who'd been hunting for decades.

She couldn't outrun them either. They'd tracked her through the dark without hesitation.

That left one option.

"Why?" she asked, buying time. "Why does the Council want a hybrid dead so badly? What are we so afraid of?"

The wolf on the left laughed. It was an ugly sound, half-growl. "Afraid? The Council doesn't fear anything."

"Then why send three enforcers for one student?"

The lead wolf's eyes flickered. Just barely. But she caught it.

She was right. They were afraid.

"The Shadow Wolf prophecy," she pressed. "It says hybrids are meant to bridge the worlds. That scares you. Because if I can unite purebloods and humans, then everything you've built falls apart."

"Enough." The lead wolf stepped forward, his form rippling, shifting into his human shape. He was tall, scarred, his face a map of old battles. "You talk too much for someone who's about to die."

"Then kill me."

He stopped.

"Go ahead," Maya said. She let her voice drop, let the cold she'd been hiding all her life bleed into it. "Kill me right here. But you should know something first."

She held up the book.

"This thing is spelled to my bloodline. If I die, it burns. Every page. Every secret. Everything the Council's been hiding for three centuries goes up in smoke."

The enforcer's face went still.

"You're bluffing," he said.

"Try me."

She wasn't bluffing. She had no idea if the book would burn. But the look on his face told her she'd hit a nerve.

"Last chance," she said. "Walk away. Tell the Council you couldn't find me."

"I can't do that."

"Then we have a problem."

The other two wolves shifted back to human form, flanking their leader. They were younger, harder, their eyes carrying the deadness of men who'd killed before.

"Take the book," the leader ordered. "Kill her after."

They moved.

Maya didn't think. She dropped, rolled, felt claws graze her shoulder as she came up behind a tree. The book was still in her hands, warm, pulsing, almost guiding her.

She opened it.

The pages were blank.

"What the—"

Then the words appeared, writing themselves in silver ink:

*Let them come.*

She didn't understand. But the book's warmth spread through her fingers, up her arms, into her chest. It felt like power. Like permission.

The enforcers circled.

Maya looked at the page again. New words had formed:

*You are not prey. You are the hunt.*

She closed the book.

Something shifted inside her. Not her wolf—something deeper. Older. The part of her that had been hiding since birth, the part that knew what she really was.

She let it out.

Her eyes went silver.

The enforcers stopped.

"What the hell?" one of them breathed.

Maya felt it now. The power wasn't just in the book. It was in her. Had always been in her. She'd just never known how to access it.

Shadow pooled around her feet, dark and alive, moving like it had a will of its own. It rose up her legs, wrapped around her arms, settled across her shoulders like a cloak.

"The Shadow Wolf," the lead enforcer whispered. "It's real."

"You didn't believe it?" Maya asked. Her voice sounded different. Echoing. Like she was speaking from somewhere else.

"We thought it was a myth. A story to scare children."

"Guess you were wrong."

She raised her hand. The shadow obeyed, lashing out like a whip, slamming into the enforcer on the left. He flew backward, crashed into a tree, didn't get up.

The other two attacked.

Maya was faster now. Stronger. The shadow moved with her, around her, through her. She caught the second enforcer's arm, twisted it, felt something snap. He screamed. She shoved him away.

The leader was smarter. He hung back, watching, studying.

"You can't control it," he said. "You're just reacting. That power will burn you out."

"Then I'll burn bright."

She pushed the shadow forward. It surged, a wave of darkness, swallowing the space between them.

The leader shifted mid-air, wolf form emerging before he hit the ground. He landed, charged, jaws open.

Maya met him head-on.

The shadow wrapped around her, lifted her, carried her forward. She collided with him in mid-air, felt his teeth graze her arm, felt her shadow slam into his chest.

They hit the ground together.

He was stronger. He was faster. He'd been doing this for decades.

But she had something he didn't.

She had everything to lose.

She pushed harder. The shadow responded, pouring out of her, into him. He convulsed, his wolf form flickering, unable to hold.

"Stop," he gasped. "Please."

She almost did.

Then she remembered every pureblood who'd sneered at her. Every professor who'd doubted her. Every moment she'd spent hiding, shrinking, making herself small so they wouldn't notice her.

She didn't stop.

The shadow consumed him. He went still.

Maya stood over him, breathing hard, her hands shaking. The shadow receded, sinking back into her skin like it had never left.

She looked at her hands.

They were silver. Not just her eyes. Her skin, glowing faintly in the moonlight.

"Is that it?"

The voice came from behind her.

She turned.

More wolves were emerging from the trees. Not three. A dozen. Their eyes glowed in the dark, a sea of amber and gold.

At their center stood a woman. Tall. Ancient. Her silver hair fell past her waist, and her eyes were the color of winter.

"Maya Chen." The woman's voice was soft, almost kind. "I'm impressed. You survived the welcoming party."

"Who are you?"

"I'm the one you've been looking for." The woman smiled. "I wrote that book."

Maya's blood froze.

"That's impossible," she said. "The book is three hundred years old."

"It is."

"Then you'd be—"

"Old." The woman laughed. "Very old. Old enough to remember when hybrids weren't hunted. Old enough to remember when they were celebrated."

She stepped forward, and the wolves parted for her like water.

"I wrote the prophecy," she said. "I hid the book where you would find it. I've been waiting for you, Maya. For a very long time."

"Why?"

"Because the Council isn't the only one who knows you're here." The woman's smile faded. "There are things in this world older than the Council. Older than the purebloods. Older than the wolves themselves. And they've been waiting for the Shadow Wolf to rise."

She stopped in front of Maya, close enough to touch.

"They're coming," she said. "And when they arrive, Silver Fang Academy will burn."

Maya clutched the book tighter.

"What are you talking about? Who's coming?"

The woman leaned in, her ancient eyes meeting Maya's silver ones.

"The ones who made us."

She reached out, touched Maya's forehead, and the world went black.

When Maya opened her eyes, she was lying on cold stone. The sky above her was wrong—too dark, too close, no stars.

She sat up.

She wasn't at Silver Fang anymore.

She was somewhere else. Somewhere the air tasted like iron, and the ground hummed with power, and the shadows moved without wind.

The book was still in her hands.

She opened it.

The page read:

*The Academy falls in three days. The old ones rise in four. You have one chance to stop them.*

*Find the first hybrid. Before they do.*

Maya looked up.

The woman was gone. The wolves were gone. She was alone in a place that felt like the beginning of the world.

And somewhere in the distance, something ancient and terrible was waking up.

title: The Council's Gambit
content:
The three wolves didn't shift. They didn't need to. Their human forms radiated power that made the air thick, made Maya's wolf cower and snarl at the same time.

"Who the hell are you?" Maya's voice came out steadier than she felt.

The one who had spoken stepped forward. He was tall, silver-haired, with eyes that held centuries of cold authority. "I am Aldric Vance. Head of the Eastern Council. These are my colleagues, Elders Miriam and Holt."

The woman—Miriam—had iron-gray hair pulled tight, a face carved from judgment. Holt was broader, scarred, his jaw set like he'd already decided she was guilty.

Maya's grip on the book tightened. "I don't answer to the Council. I'm a student here."

"You're an anomaly here." Aldric's voice was calm, patient, like he was explaining something to a child. "And anomalies require investigation."

"I haven't done anything."

"You've been in the restricted section. You've touched texts that should have been destroyed. You've awakened a bloodline that was meant to stay buried." He took another step. "Give me the book, Maya. And come quietly. No one needs to get hurt."

Maya's mind raced. Kael was still in the library. The Elders were here. The book was warm against her chest, almost pulsing, like it had a heartbeat.

"Why should I trust you?"

"Because we're the only ones who can protect you from what's coming." Miriam's voice was sharp, cutting. "The pack you were born to? They're already mobilizing. They've been waiting for a sign that you existed. Now they have it."

"What pack?"

"The Lunar Fang." Holt's voice was gravel. "Your father's pack. Before he was exiled. Before he was killed."

Maya felt the ground tilt. "My father was a rogue. He died before I was born."

"Is that what your mother told you?" Aldric's eyes softened, just barely. "Your father was Alpha of the Lunar Fang. One of the most powerful packs in the northern territories. When he took a human mate, when he had you, the Council declared him abomination. He was executed. Your mother fled. She hid you among humans, hoping you'd never manifest."

The book burned hotter.

"But I did manifest."

"Yes." Miriam's voice was bitter. "And now the Lunar Fang knows. They've been searching for their Alpha's heir for eighteen years. They'll come for you, Maya. And when they do, they won't ask nicely."

Maya's knees wanted to buckle. She forced them to lock.

"So you're here to... what? Save me? Recruit me?"

"Neither." Aldric's eyes flickered to the book. "We're here to contain you. The hybrid prophecy cannot be fulfilled. It would destabilize every pack, every territory, every law we've built over a thousand years."

"You want to lock me up."

"We want to give you a choice." He extended his hand. "Come with us. Voluntarily. We'll keep you safe, educate you, help you control your abilities. Or stay here, and face the Lunar Fang alone. They'll be here by sunrise."

Maya's wolf howled inside her. *Fight. Run. Trust no one.*

"Three against one," she said slowly. "And I'm just a student. You could take me by force."

"We could." Aldric's voice was almost sad. "But that would make us no better than the ones who killed your father. We're giving you dignity, Maya. Take it."

She looked down at the book. The ink had shifted again. The words now read: *They lie. All of them. Trust the one who bleeds for you.*

Trust the one who bleeds for you.

Kael.

He'd pushed her into the passage. He'd stayed behind. He'd told her to trust him.

She looked up at the Elders, her face hardening.

"I'll make a deal with you."

Aldric's eyebrow rose. "You're in no position to negotiate."

"Sure I am. You want the book. You want me contained. But you don't want to hurt me, because that would make you the villains. So here's my offer." She held the book up. "I'll come with you peacefully. I'll answer your questions. But you let me say goodbye to one person. One. And you don't touch him."

"Kael Thornwood."

"How did you—"

"We know everything, Maya." Aldric's smile was thin. "We've been watching you since the moment your wolf manifested. We know about the training, the kiss, the library. We know he's marked you as pack."

Maya's heart stopped.

"He what?"

"He hasn't told you." Miriam's laugh was cold. "The pureblood heir, the golden boy of Silver Fang Academy, and he's already claimed you. Not as a mate. Not officially. But his wolf has chosen. He'll fight anyone who threatens you. He'll die for you. And he hasn't told you a word of it."

Maya's hand went to her chest, where Kael's mark should be. There was nothing. No scar, no sign.

"Wolves don't need to bite to claim," Holt said, reading her confusion. "It's a scent thing. A pack thing. His wolf has woven itself into yours. You might not feel it yet, but you will. The moment you're separated, you'll feel like half of you is missing."

The book pulsed again. *He bleeds for you. He has always bled for you.*

"Fine," Maya said, her voice rough. "Then let me see him. Let me say goodbye."

Aldric studied her for a long moment. Then he nodded.

"One hour. Then we leave. And Maya—" His voice hardened. "If you try to run, if you try to fight, I will not be gentle. The prophecy ends tonight, one way or another."

He stepped aside, gesturing toward the dorms.

Maya walked past them, her heart pounding, her mind whirling. She had one hour. One hour to figure out what the hell was happening, who to trust, and how to survive.

The book felt like fire in her hands.

She found Kael in his room. He was pacing, his shirt torn, a bruise blooming on his jaw. When he saw her, his entire body relaxed, then tensed again.

"You're okay."

"They let me go. For now." She closed the door behind her. "Kael. Why didn't you tell me?"

"Tell you what?"

"That you've claimed me. That your wolf has—"

He went pale. "Who told you that?"

"The Council Elders. They're here. They want to take me away." She stepped closer, the book still clutched to her chest. "And you've been hiding something from me. Something important."

Kael's jaw worked. He looked at the door, at the window, at the ceiling, anywhere but at her.

"Because if I told you," he said finally, his voice raw, "you'd think I was trying to trap you. Control you. Like every other wolf who's ever looked at you like you were prey."

"Is that what you're doing?"

"No." He met her eyes, and she saw something she'd never seen there before. Fear. Real, raw fear. "Maya, I've been trying to protect you from myself. My wolf chose you the moment you walked into that library. The moment you stood up to Marcus. The moment you showed everyone that you were stronger than they'd ever be. And I've been fighting it every single day because I know what it means."

"What does it mean?"

He stepped closer, close enough that she could feel the heat of him, the tension in his muscles.

"It means that if they take you, if they hurt you, I will tear through every pack, every Council, every law they've ever written. I will burn the world down to get you back." His voice dropped to a whisper. "And I'm not sorry for it."

Maya looked at him, at the boy who had been her rival, her trainer, her reluctant ally. The boy who had kissed her like she was the only thing that mattered.

She reached out and touched his face, her fingers tracing the bruise on his jaw.

"Then help me fight them."

"How?"

She opened the book. The pages were blank now, empty, waiting.

"I don't know yet. But this book knows. It's been guiding me since I touched it." She looked up at him. "Trust me, Kael. Like you asked me to trust you."

He took her hand, his fingers lacing with hers.

"Always."

The door exploded inward.

Aldric stood in the doorway, his eyes blazing amber, his wolf riding just beneath his skin.

"Time's up, Maya. And I see you've chosen poorly."

Behind him, Miriam and Holt shifted, their forms rippling, fur tearing through skin, bones cracking, wolves emerging from the wreckage of their human bodies.

Kael stepped in front of Maya, his own shift beginning.

"No," Maya said, grabbing his arm. "Not here. Not like this."

She held up the book. It was burning now, literally burning, flames licking the edges but not consuming it.

"If you want the prophecy," she said, her voice steady despite the fire in her hands, "you'll have to take it from me. And I promise you, Aldric Vance, I will make sure every single word of it comes true."

The book exploded in a burst of silver light.

And everything went white.

title: The Council's Debt
content:
Maya's wolf surged beneath her skin, begging for release. Three against one. Bad odds even if she shifted. But these weren't students—they were enforcers. She could tell by the way they moved, the economy of their steps, the cold calculation in their amber eyes.

"Who's asking?" She kept her voice steady, even as her heart hammered against her ribs.

The lead wolf stepped closer. Mid-forties, silver threading his dark hair, a scar cutting through his left eyebrow. "The Council doesn't introduce itself to hybrids. It collects them."

"Collection implies consent. I'm not consenting."

He almost smiled. "You misunderstand. The Council doesn't ask."

The other two spread out, flanking her. Maya calculated distances. Twenty feet to the dorms. Fifteen to the tree line. The book burned against her chest, warm as a living thing.

"I have a question," she said, buying time. "If the Council wants me dead, why not just kill me? Why the elaborate collection?"

Scar-eyes tilted his head. "Who said anything about death?"

"You're hunting a hybrid. The book says—"

"The book says what it's programmed to say." He took another step. "You're valuable, Maya Chen. More valuable than you know. The Council doesn't waste resources."

"Then what do they want?"

"Answers. Cooperation. Your blood in a vial and your name on a contract." He spread his hands. "Painless, if you come quietly."

Maya laughed. It came out sharp, bitter. "My mother said the same thing before she sold me out to my father's pack. 'Come quietly, Maya. It'll be easier.'" She shook her head. "I've heard that lie before."

Something flickered in Scar-eyes' expression. Recognition? Respect? Hard to tell in the dark.

"Your mother made a choice," he said. "I'm offering you the same courtesy."

"You're offering me a cage. Different paint, same bars."

The two flankers moved closer. Maya shifted her weight, ready to run. She'd made it eighteen years hiding her wolf. She could outrun three enforcers.

Then Scar-eyes spoke again. "Kael Stormcrest is already in custody. If you run, he takes the fall for the book. For the library breach. For everything."

Maya's blood stopped moving.

"Liar."

"I wish I was." He pulled out his phone, turned the screen toward her. A photo. Kael, on his knees, hands bound behind his back, blood running from his nose. The same fierce eyes, but tired now. Defeated.

No. Not Kael. Not because of her.

"The Council works fast," Scar-eyes said. "We had orders to bring you both. He chose to delay us. Chose to give you time to escape." He pocketed the phone. "His choice. His consequences."

"What did you do to him?"

"Nothing permanent. Yet." He met her eyes. "That's the deal, Maya. You come with us, answer the Council's questions, sign the contract—and Kael walks free, no record, no punishment. You refuse, and he takes the fall for everything. Theft of classified materials. Trespassing in restricted archives. Conspiracy with a hybrid."

The words hit like a physical blow. Maya's grip on the book tightened until her knuckles went white.

"That's not a deal. That's blackmail."

"Call it what you like. It's the only offer on the table."

She thought of Kael. The way he'd pushed her into that passage. The way he'd said *trust me* like it cost him something. The way he'd stood between her and danger without hesitation.

She thought of the book. The prophecy. The hunt.

She thought of her mother, standing in the doorway of their apartment eighteen years ago, tears streaming down her face, telling Maya to run. *Run and don't look back. They'll use you, Maya. They'll break you open and take everything.*

She'd run then. She'd survived.

But survival meant nothing if she became the thing her mother warned her about.

"Fine." The word tasted like ash. "I'll come. But I want to see Kael first. I want to see him walk free before I sign anything."

Scar-eyes studied her for a long moment. Then he nodded. "Reasonable."

He gestured, and the two flankers stepped back. Maya walked toward him, each step heavier than the last. The book pulsed against her chest, warm and urgent, like a heartbeat.

*Don't do this,* it seemed to say. *Don't trust them.*

But what choice did she have?

They led her through the trees, away from the academy, toward a road she'd never noticed before. A black SUV waited, engine running. Scar-eyes opened the back door.

"After you."

Maya climbed in. The leather seats smelled new, sterile. The door closed with a thud that sounded final.

The drive took twenty minutes. Maya watched the academy disappear through the tinted windows, the lights growing smaller until they were swallowed by darkness. The book rested in her lap, still warm, still pulsing.

She opened it to the last page.

The ink had shifted again. The drawing of her was gone. In its place, a single line:

*The cage is not where they put you. The cage is what you believe.*

Maya stared at the words. Her breath caught.

The SUV pulled into a compound—concrete buildings, floodlights, barbed wire. A facility she'd never seen, hidden in the middle of nowhere. Scar-eyes opened her door.

"Welcome to your new home, Maya Chen."

She stepped out, the book hidden beneath her jacket. The air smelled different here. Cold. Sterile. Like a hospital that had forgotten what life felt like.

They led her inside, down fluorescent-lit hallways, past doors with no windows. The building hummed with energy—magic, technology, something in between. She felt it in her teeth, her bones, her blood.

They stopped at a door at the end of the hall. Scar-eyes unlocked it, pushed it open.

Inside, a room. Sparse. A bed. A desk. A mirror that reflected nothing—no, that reflected *everything*. Maya saw herself from every angle, her wolf form flickering beneath her skin like a second shadow.

"Get comfortable," Scar-eyes said. "The Council will see you tomorrow."

He closed the door. The lock clicked.

Maya stood alone in the room, the book warm against her chest, her mind racing. She opened it again.

The words had changed:

*Tomorrow, they will ask you three questions. Lie to all three. Your life depends on it.*

Below that, in smaller script:

*And when they ask you about the Shadow Wolf, tell them she died with the last of her kind.*

*She did not.*

*She is you.*

Maya's hands shook. She read the words again, and again, until they burned into her memory.

Then she closed the book, sat on the bed, and waited.

Outside, the moon rose full and silver. Inside, Maya Chen began to plan.

---

She didn't sleep. Couldn't. Every time she closed her eyes, she saw Kael on his knees, blood on his face. She saw the amber eyes of the enforcers. She saw the words of the book, dancing behind her eyelids like fireflies.

At dawn, the door opened.

Scar-eyes stood in the hallway, his expression unreadable. "The Council is ready."

Maya stood. Her muscles ached from tension, from waiting. She'd spent the night running scenarios, planning escapes, calculating outcomes. None of them ended well.

But the book had given her a path. A dangerous one. A lie that could get her killed.

She followed Scar-eyes down the hallway, through another door, into a room that opened into something else entirely.

A cavern. Huge. Ancient. The ceiling disappeared into darkness, and the walls were lined with torches that burned with silver flame. At the center, a long table carved from black stone. Seven figures sat behind it, their faces hidden in shadow.

The Council.

"Sit." The voice came from the center figure—a woman, old and powerful, her silver hair cascading over her shoulders like a waterfall of moonlight.

Maya sat in the single chair facing them. The book pressed against her ribs, hidden but present, warm as a living thing.

The woman leaned forward. Her eyes caught the light, and Maya saw them clearly for the first time.

Silver. Just like the wolf in the book.

"You know why you're here, Maya Chen."

"Enlighten me."

The woman smiled. It didn't reach her eyes. "You're the first hybrid born in three centuries. The first Shadow Wolf since the Great Purge. The Council has waited a long time for you."

"Waited for what?"

"To finish what we started."

The words hung in the air like smoke. Maya's blood went cold.

"The Great Purge," she repeated. "You killed them. You killed all the hybrids."

"Not killed. *Ended.* There's a difference." The woman's silver eyes bore into hers. "Hybrids are unstable. Dangerous. A threat to the purity of our species. The Shadow Wolves were the most dangerous of all—half human, half wolf, bound to neither world. They had to be eliminated."

"But I'm still here."

"Yes." The woman's smile widened. "And that's your problem, isn't it?"

The first question came like a blade.

"Tell us, Maya Chen: Do you know where the other hybrids are hiding?"

Maya met her silver gaze. The book's words echoed in her mind.

*Lie to all three.*

"No," she said. "I don't."

The woman's smile didn't flicker. "The second question: Do you know who your father is?"

Maya's throat tightened. She thought of her mother's tears, the night she'd fled. The name she'd never spoken aloud.

"No," she said. "I don't know him."

"And the third question." The woman leaned forward, her silver eyes burning. "Are you the last Shadow Wolf?"

Maya's heart stopped.

The book pulsed against her chest, warm and urgent.

*Tell them she died with the last of her kind.*

The lie sat on her tongue, ready.

But something else rose up instead. Something fierce and hungry and *angry*.

Her wolf.

*No more hiding.*

Maya opened her mouth.

And the world exploded.

The ceiling cracked. Stones rained down. The silver flames guttered and died. In the chaos, Maya saw the Council members scramble, heard Scar-eyes shout, felt the ground shake beneath her feet.

And through the dust and fire, a figure emerged.

Tall. Fierce. Eyes burning with silver fire.

Kael.

"Get down!" he roared.

Maya dropped as something massive crashed through the ceiling—a wolf, huge and ancient, its fur the color of midnight, its eyes burning like twin suns.

It landed between her and the Council, and the ground splintered beneath its weight.

The wolf turned its head, looked directly at Maya.

And spoke, its voice resonating in her bones.

*"Run, little Shadow. The cage is breaking."*

Maya didn't wait.

She ran.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Hunters' Prey

Maya's fingers dug into the book's spine. The leather felt warm, almost feverish, against her palms. The three wolves stood motionless, their amber eyes fixed on her with predatory patience.

"I don't think so," she said, her voice steadier than she felt.

The lead wolf—gray-coated, scarred across his muzzle—tilted his head. "That's not a request."

"Then make me."

She knew it was stupid. She was outnumbered, outmatched, and standing in her human form against three shifters who looked like they'd been fighting wars before she was born. But the book burned against her chest, and something in her blood responded to it, a heat that spread through her veins like wildfire.

The gray wolf took a step forward. "Last warning, hybrid."

"I've heard worse."

He lunged.

Maya didn't think. She moved. Her body twisted, ducking under his leap, using the momentum to roll and come up running. She crashed through the underbrush, branches whipping at her face, the book tucked against her ribs.

Behind her, she heard the other two give chase. Their paws hit the earth in rhythm, faster than her human legs could match.

She needed to shift.

But she didn't know how. Not properly. Every time she'd tried since arriving at Silver Fang, something had gone wrong—her wolf half-emerging, her control slipping, her body caught between forms. She'd always managed to stop it before anyone saw too much.

Now there was no one to see. And no time to be afraid.

*Let go,* a voice whispered inside her. *Stop holding back.*

She didn't know if it was her own thought or something else. But she listened.

The shift ripped through her like lightning.

Pain. Bright and sharp. Bones reshaping, muscles tearing and reforming, skin giving way to fur. She fell forward, her hands hitting the ground as paws, her spine arching, her senses exploding into overdrive.

Then she was running on four legs, and the world had changed.

She could smell everything. The damp earth. The pine needles. The wolves behind her—their anger, their hunger, their confidence. She could hear their breathing, their hearts beating, the creak of their joints as they pushed harder.

And she could feel her own wolf. Not as a separate thing, but as part of her. Whole. Complete.

She ran faster.

The trees blurred past. The moon was a white eye above her, watching, judging. She didn't know where she was going. She only knew she couldn't stop.

One of the wolves veered left, trying to cut her off. She swerved right, barely avoiding his jaws. His teeth snapped inches from her flank.

*Faster.*

She pushed herself harder, her lungs burning, her legs screaming. The book was still pressed against her chest—how, she didn't understand. It seemed to cling to her fur, moving with her like it was part of her body.

Ahead, she saw the edge of the forest. Beyond it, the training grounds. Empty at this hour. And beyond that—

The wall.

The academy's perimeter was ringed by a stone wall twelve feet high, topped with silver wire that hummed with energy. Wards. Meant to keep threats out.

And hybrids in.

She skidded to a halt at the tree line. The three wolves emerged behind her, spreading out, forming a semicircle that blocked her retreat.

The gray wolf spoke again, his voice carrying through the night. "Nowhere to run, hybrid. The wards will burn you if you try to cross."

Maya stood her ground. Her wolf form was smaller than theirs, leaner. Built for speed, not power. But she didn't lower her head. Didn't submit.

*They're not going to kill me,* she realized. *The Council wants me alive.*

That gave her an advantage. They had to be careful. She didn't.

"I'll take my chances," she said. The words came out as a growl, but she knew they understood.

She turned and bolted straight for the wall.

The wolves howled behind her. She heard them give chase, heard the gray one shouting orders. But she didn't look back. She focused on the wall, on the silver wire that glowed like a promise of pain.

Three feet away, she leaped.

Her claws hit the stone. She scrambled, finding purchase, hauling herself up. The silver wire sparked as she got close, the energy crackling, the smell of ozone filling her nostrils.

One more push.

She launched herself over the top.

The wire caught her hind leg.

Pain exploded through her. White-hot, blinding. She screamed—a sound that was part human, part wolf—and tumbled over the other side, landing hard on the grass beyond.

She lay there, gasping, her leg burning. The book had fallen a few feet away, its pages fluttering in the wind.

On the other side of the wall, the wolves stopped. She heard their frustrated snarls, their pacing. They couldn't follow. The wards didn't let them.

But they'd report this. They'd tell the Council.

Maya forced herself to stand. Her leg screamed in protest, but she could put weight on it. The burn was superficial. It would heal.

She limped to the book and picked it up. The page had changed again.

This time, it showed a map. The academy. The forest. The mountains beyond. And marked in silver ink, a path leading north, into the wilderness.

A single word was written beneath it:

*Run.*

Maya stared at the page. Her leg throbbed. Her body ached. Her mind was a hurricane of questions.

She heard the wolves retreating from the wall. They'd be back. With others. With the Council.

She looked at the map again. The path led to something marked only as a small circle, no label, no explanation.

"Trust me," Kael had said.

She didn't know if she could trust anyone. Not Kael, not the book, not herself.

But she knew one thing: she couldn't stay.

She shifted back to human form—the transition smoother this time, almost natural—and started walking north.

The moon followed her. The book hummed in her hands. And somewhere behind her, the hunt was just beginning.

---

Dawn found her at the base of the mountains.

She'd walked all night, following the map's path, her leg healing slowly as she moved. The terrain had changed from manicured academy grounds to wild forest to rocky foothills. The air was thinner here. Colder.

The book had gone quiet. The pages were blank now, empty of any image or text. It felt like an ordinary old book, bound in cracked leather, smelling of dust and age.

Maya found a hollow between two boulders and collapsed. She was exhausted. Hungry. Her clothes were torn, her skin scratched, her leg still tender.

She needed to rest. Just for a few minutes.

But sleep wouldn't come.

Every time she closed her eyes, she saw them. The three wolves. The Council. The silver-eyed wolf from the book's drawing. And Kael, pushing her into that passage, his eyes fierce and afraid.

What had he known? What was he hiding?

And why had she trusted him?

She opened the book again, flipping through the pages. Nothing. Just blank parchment, smelling faintly of something she couldn't place.

She was about to close it when she noticed something.

The last page. The one that had shown the map.

There was writing now. Faint, as if it had been there all along, waiting for the right light to reveal itself.

She held the book up to the rising sun.

The words appeared:

*The Council's hunters will find you by nightfall. The path ahead is guarded. Trust the blood that runs in your veins—it remembers what your mind has forgotten.*

*The first test is coming.*

*Do not fail.*

Maya's blood ran cold.

"First test?" she muttered. "Test of what?"

The book didn't answer.

She looked up at the mountains. The path wound upward, through a narrow pass between two peaks. Beyond it, she could see nothing but rock and sky.

Somewhere in those mountains, something waited for her. A test. A trial. A trap.

She had no choice but to find out.

Maya stood, stuffed the book into her jacket, and started climbing.

The sun rose higher. The shadows grew shorter. And behind her, far in the distance, she heard the first howl of the hunt.

They'd found her trail.

She didn't look back. She just climbed faster.

---

The pass narrowed as she ascended, the walls closing in until she had to turn sideways to squeeze through. The rock was cold and damp, slick with morning dew. Her hands slipped. Her breath came in ragged gasps.

The howls were getting closer.

She emerged into a small clearing, ringed by stone, open to the sky. In the center stood a single tree—ancient, twisted, its bark blackened by lightning.

And beneath the tree, a figure.

Maya stopped.

It was a woman. Tall, silver-haired, dressed in robes that seemed to shift between white and gray. Her eyes were closed. Her hands were folded.

She looked exactly like the woman from the library's portrait. The founder of Silver Fang Academy.

The Shadow Wolf's mentor.

"Welcome, child," the woman said, opening her eyes.

They were silver. Just like the wolf in the book. Just like Maya's own, when she let herself see them.

"I've been waiting for you."

title: Chapter 18
content:
CHAPTER 18: The Bonds That Break

The wolves circled, their amber eyes fixed on me with predatory patience. Three of them. Each one easily twice the size of the students I'd faced in the arena. Their fur was matted with age and scars, their muscles corded and lethal.

I didn't run. My legs wouldn't have obeyed anyway.

"Look at her shake," the one in the middle said. His voice was a low rumble that vibrated through the earth. "Smells like fear and defiance. Just like the reports said."

I forced my spine straight. "Who sent you? The Council?"

The wolf on the left laughed, a guttural sound. "Sharp. For a mongrel."

"I've been called worse by better wolves."

The middle wolf stepped forward, and I saw the silver collar around his neck—not jewelry, but a mark of rank. Enforcer. These were Council enforcers, the ones who handled problems too messy for the school administration.

"The book," he said. "Hand it over."

My arms tightened around the leather-bound volume. It pulsed against my chest, warm and alive, like a second heartbeat. "Come take it."

He lunged.

I didn't think. I moved.

The Shadow Wolf inside me surged forward, and suddenly I was faster than I'd ever been. I ducked under his swipe, rolled to the side, and came up running. The trees blurred past me as I sprinted toward the dormitories, toward lights, toward anyone who might hear me scream.

But they were faster.

One of them cut in front of me, materializing from the darkness like a nightmare. I skidded to a halt, my heart slamming against my ribs.

"Clever," he said. "But not clever enough."

I backed up, my eyes darting, searching for an escape. The other two closed in behind me. They had me boxed in, three sides covered, the fourth blocked by a stone wall I couldn't hope to climb.

I looked at the book. The pages were glowing now, light seeping through the leather cover like it was on fire inside.

*The hunt begins.*

That's what it had said. And here I was, cornered like prey.

"No," I whispered. "I'm not dying here."

The Shadow Wolf stirred. Not the panicked scramble of survival instinct, but something deeper. Something that felt like recognition.

The enforcer lunged again.

And I shifted.

Not the controlled shift I'd practiced in training. Not the half-shift that tore through my clothes and left me caught between forms. This was something else entirely.

The change ripped through me like lightning, painless and instantaneous. One moment I was human. The next, I was wolf.

But not a normal wolf.

The enforcers froze.

I couldn't see myself, but I felt it—the wrongness of my form, the way my fur seemed to drink the moonlight rather than reflect it. My paws left no prints in the damp earth. My breath made no mist in the cold air.

The middle enforcer's eyes went wide. "Impossible."

"She's a Shadow Wolf," the one behind me breathed. "A real one. Not just hybrid."

"Get her. Now!"

They attacked together, all three at once.

I met them head-on.
